results.question: How did Romulus die?
results.ideal_answer: The sources disagree. The official version, in both Livy and Plutarch, is that while Romulus was reviewing the army at the Goat's Marsh a sudden storm enveloped him and he was carried up to heaven, thereafter worshipped as the god Quirinus. But both historians also preserve a darker rumor: that the senators (fathers), resentful of his rule, fell upon him and tore his body to pieces, each carrying away a part — Plutarch placing the deed in the temple of Vulcan.
results.answer: The sources present two contradictory traditions regarding the death of Romulus. (1) He was enveloped by a sudden storm "with great thunder and lightning" while reviewing his army near the lake of Capra, and was never seen again; the senators proclaimed he was "carried aloft by the storm" and had become a god [2][3]. (2) An alternative, darker rumour held that the senators "cut his body into pieces" and each carried away a part in his bosom, or more generally that he was "torn in pieces by the hands of the Fathers" [1][2]. Plutarch adds that his body was never found, leaving no physical evidence, and notes that some believed the murder took place in the temple of Vulcan, while others located the disappearance at the Goat's Marsh [1][3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, including the two traditions of Romulus's disappearance, the locations, and the absence of his body, with no invented facts or unsupported details. | completeness: The answer fully covers both contradictory traditions (the storm and apotheosis versus the senators dismembering him), the key locations, and the disagreement among sources, thereby completely addressing what the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree on the historical traditions and rumors surrounding Romulus's death, and the answer accurately summarizes these accounts with correct attributions without inventing a disagreement between the sources.

results.question: How did Themistocles die?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides states plainly that disease was the real cause of his death, noting only as a rumor the story that he poisoned himself on finding he could not deliver Greece to the Persian king. Plutarch gives the dramatic suicide as "the usual story" — that Themistocles sacrificed, feasted with his friends, and drank bull's blood — while acknowledging the alternative that it was a fast-acting poison.
results.answer: According to Plutarch, Themistocles died by suicide in Magnesia. He "drank bull's blood, as is the usual story; as others state, a poison producing instant death; and ended his days in the city of Magnesia, having lived sixty-five years" [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is fully supported by passage [1], correctly glossing Themistocles' decision to end his life as suicide and accurately quoting the details of his death in Magnesia without introducing any new facts. | completeness: It covers Plutarch's suicide account but omits Thucydides' view that disease was the actual cause, leaving out a major historiographical tradition required to fully answer the question. | attribution: Only one source is provided and the answer correctly attributes the information to Plutarch without any misattribution.

results.question: Did Caligula actually intend to make his horse consul?
results.ideal_answer: The two sources differ in confidence. Suetonius reports it only as hearsay — "it is even said that he intended to make him consul." Cassius Dio states it more strongly: that Caligula promised the consulship to the horse Incitatus and "would certainly have done" it had he lived longer. So the claim is a guarded rumor in Suetonius and a near-assertion in Dio.
results.answer: According to Suetonius, "It is even said that he intended to make him consul" [1], presenting it as a rumor. Cassius Dio goes further, writing that Caligula "promised besides that he would appoint him consul. This he would certainly have done, too, if he had lived longer" [2]. Thus, ancient sources report that Caligula did intend to make his horse Incitatus consul, though Suetonius treats it as hearsay while Dio regards it as a definite intention that was only thwarted by the emperor's assassination.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer adds that Caligula's intention was thwarted by his assassination, a new fact not found in any provided passage and not a correct gloss of source content. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by explaining both Suetonius's hearsay report and Cassius Dio's stronger assertion, thereby covering the source disagreement and the nature of Caligula's reputed intention without omitting anything asked. | attribution: The answer accurately attributes the distinct nuances of both sources in prose, correctly noting Suetonius's framing of the claim as hearsay and Dio's presentation of it as a definite intention.

results.question: How did Bucephalus die?
results.ideal_answer: The sources disagree on whether the horse died of battle wounds or of old age. Arrian insists explicitly that Bucephalas died "not from having been wounded by any one, but from the effects of toil and old age." Plutarch reports both: that most authorities say he died under treatment for his wounds after the battle with Porus, though Onesicritus says it was fatigue and age. Both agree he was about thirty and that Alexander founded a city (Bucephala) in his memory.
results.answer: According to Plutarch, most authorities state that Bucephalus died "under cure of his wounds" after the battle with Porus, but the historian Onesicritus reported that he died "of fatigue and age, being thirty years old" [1]. Arrian agrees with the latter, writing that Bucephalus died "not from having been wounded by any one, but from the effects of toil and old age; for he was about thirty years old, and quite worn out with toil" [2]. A modern account by J. B. Bury similarly says he died "of old age and weariness," probably shortly before the battle [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer accurately reports Plutarch and Arrian, but it invents a direct quote and claim about Bucephalus’s death attributed to J. B. Bury that does not appear in the provided passage [3]. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by explaining the conflicting ancient accounts of Bucephalus's death—battle wounds versus old age and fatigue—without omitting any information the question requires. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces and attributes the differing accounts of Plutarch and Arrian in prose, but it misattributes a claim about Bucephalus's death to J. B. Bury, whose provided passage does not mention how the horse died.

results.question: How did Xerxes get back to Asia after Salamis?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus himself records two conflicting accounts. In his main narrative, Xerxes marched his army by land to the Hellespont and crossed to Abydos by ship, the bridges having been broken by a storm. But he then reports "another story": that Xerxes sailed from Eion on the Strymon in a Phoenician ship caught in a storm, and that the Persian nobles on deck leapt into the sea to lighten it and save the king — a tale Herodotus relates but disbelieves.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, there are two accounts of Xerxes' return to Asia after Salamis. In the first, the Persian army, having discovered that the floating-bridges across the Hellespont had been destroyed by a storm, "crossed over the Hellespont in haste to Abydos by means of the ships," and then continued on with Xerxes to Sardis [4]. In the second story, Xerxes himself did not continue by road but embarked on a Phoenician ship at Eion on the Strymon. A violent storm arose, and the ship, heavily laden with Persians, was in danger of sinking; the king cried out in fear, and the pilot told him that the only safety lay in lightening the vessel. Xerxes then called upon his Persian companions to show their care for him, whereupon they made obeisance and "leapt out into the sea," allowing the ship to be lightened and the king to survive [4]. George Rawlinson, summarizing the tradition, notes that Xerxes returned into Asia "not by his magnificent bridge, which a storm had destroyed, but on board a vessel, which, according to some, narrowly escaped shipwreck during the passage" [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the cited passages [3] and [4], with no invented facts, quotes, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer fully covers the question by providing both of Herodotus's conflicting accounts of how Xerxes returned to Asia after Salamis without omitting any required details. | attribution: The answer clearly attributes the two distinct accounts of Xerxes' return to Herodotus in prose and accurately credits Rawlinson for his summary of the tradition.

results.question: How did Remus die?
results.ideal_answer: Livy gives two versions side by side: that Remus fell in the brawl that broke out when the brothers' rival bird-omens were disputed, or — the "more common account" — that he leaped over Romulus's newly-built walls in derision and Romulus killed him in a rage, crying "So shall every one fare, who shall dare to leap over my fortifications." Smith's history follows the leaping-the-wall version as the standard one.
results.answer:
The sources offer several accounts of Remus's death, all stemming from a dispute with his brother Romulus over the founding of Rome.

Livy provides two versions [1]. In the first, after the augury contest, the brothers' supporters came to blows: "there Remus fell from a blow received in the crowd." The second, which Livy calls "a more common account," is that "Remus, in derision of his brother, leaped over his new-built wall, and was, for that reason, slain by Romulus in a passion," with Romulus adding, "So shall every one fare, who shall dare to leap over my fortifications."

Cassius Dio concisely states that after the brothers "got into a conflict in which Remus was killed" [2].

William Smith's history follows the wall-leaping version: "One day Remus leapt over it in scorn; whereupon Romulus slew him, exclaiming, 'So die whosoever hereafter shall leap over my walls'" [3].

Plutarch mentions only that Romulus buried Remus on the mount Remonia [4], without narrating the death itself.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is otherwise fully grounded, but it attributes to Cassius Dio a statement that the source passage explicitly credits to Zonaras. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by providing multiple complete accounts of Remus's death, including Livy's two versions, Cassius Dio's summary, and Smith's preferred wall-leaping narrative, without omitting anything asked. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces the differing accounts of Remus's death and clearly attributes each version to its respective source in prose.

results.question: How did Alexander deal with the Gordian knot?
results.ideal_answer: The sources split on whether he cut it or untied it by a trick. Arrian reports that some say Alexander, unable to loosen the cord, struck it with his sword and cut it through — but that Aristobulus says he pulled out the pin of the wagon-pole and so drew out the yoke. Plutarch gives the same two versions: most authors say he cut it with his sword, but Aristobulus says he undid it easily by pulling out the pin.
results.answer: According to Arrian, when Alexander arrived at Gordium he went up to the citadel to see the famous wagon of Gordius, whose yoke was fastened by an intricate cord of cornel bark that had no visible beginning or end. An oracle declared that whoever could loosen the cord would rule Asia. Alexander, unable to find a way to untie it but unwilling to leave it unloosened, “struck the cord with his sword and cut it through, saying that it had been untied by him” [1]. Arrian adds that Aristobulus gave a different account: Alexander simply “pulled out the pin of the wagon-pole, which was a wooden peg driven right through it, holding the cord together,” and then drew out the yoke [1]. Plutarch records the same two versions, noting that “most authors tell the story that Alexander, finding himself unable to untie the knot … cut it asunder with his sword,” while “Aristobulus tells us it was easy for him to undo it, by only pulling the pin out of the pole” [3]. Thus Alexander either severed the knot with his sword or removed the pole-pin, depending on which tradition one follows.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim, paraphrase, and direct quote in the answer is fully supported by the cited passages [1] and [3], with no invented facts, names, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by presenting both traditions—cutting the knot with his sword and removing the wagon-pole pin—citing Arrian, Aristobulus, and Plutarch, with no required information omitted. | attribution: The sources agree on the two historical traditions regarding the knot, and the answer correctly attributes both versions to Arrian and Plutarch in clear prose without any misattribution.

results.question: What did Nero say about an artist dying, and when?
results.ideal_answer: The wording and timing differ between sources. Suetonius reports that as his grave was dug before his eyes Nero wept and kept repeating "What an artist is now about to perish!" Cassius Dio gives a different form and moment: that Nero spoke the "far-famed sentence" — "Jupiter, what an artist perishes in me!" — at the very instant he killed himself.
results.answer: According to Suetonius, as Nero was preparing to commit suicide after the Senate declared him an enemy and soldiers approached, he repeatedly lamented, 'What an artist is now about to perish!' [1]. This occurred at the end of his life, in his final moments, as he ordered a grave to be dug and awaited his forced suicide.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer accurately reports Nero’s lament and the surrounding final‐hour events, all of which are supported by the provided Suetonius passage, with no invented facts, quotes, or names. | completeness: The answer provides the quote and its timing according to Suetonius, fully satisfying the question's requirements; omitting Cassius Dio's variant account does not matter because the reference includes that as extra detail beyond what was asked. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the quote and its context to the single source provided, Suetonius, with no misattribution or disagreement to surface.

results.question: Why did Tarpeia betray the citadel, and how did she die?
results.ideal_answer: All sources agree she was bribed by the gold the Sabines wore on their left arms and was crushed to death under their shields, but they differ on her intent. Livy gives the standard treacherous version (she was bribed to admit the soldiers and crushed under their arms) but also reports an alternative: that she demanded their shields in good faith, meaning to disarm them, and was killed as if a traitor by the very reward she chose. Plutarch agrees she coveted the golden bracelets and asked for "the things they wore on their left arms."
results.answer:
Tarpeia betrayed the Roman citadel out of greed. According to Plutarch, she was the daughter of the captain Tarpeius and, "coveting the golden bracelets she saw them wear," she asked the Sabines for "the things they wore on their left arms" in return for opening a gate at night [2][1]. Livy similarly says that the Sabine commander Tatius "bribes his maiden daughter with gold" to admit armed soldiers [4]. William Smith's history states that she was "dazzled by the golden bracelets" [3].

Her death resulted from that same treacherous bargain. When she came to claim her reward, Tatius led the Sabines in throwing not only their bracelets but also their shields onto her, crushing her. Plutarch writes that "she, being borne down and quite buried with the multitude of gold and their shields, died under the weight and pressure of them" [1]. Livy records that "those who were admitted crushed her to death by heaping their arms upon her" and notes a variant story that the Sabines threw their shields upon her instead of golden bracelets, because they interpreted her demand for what was "on their left arms" as the shields [4]. Smith concisely states they "threw upon her the shields 'which they wore on their left arms,' and thus crushed her to death" [3]. All sources agree she was buried under the missiles of her supposed allies.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, including the motivations (greed for gold/bracelets), the request for what was worn on the left arms, the night-time betrayal, the crushing death by shields and gold, and Livy's variant explanation, with no invented facts or unsupported quotes. | completeness: The answer fully addresses both parts of the question, explaining her greed for gold as the motive and her death by being crushed under Sabine shields and bracelets, without omitting anything explicitly asked. | attribution: The sources agree on the core narrative of Tarpeia's betrayal and death, and the answer provides clear, accurate prose attribution for the specific details and quotes drawn from each text.

results.question: What was Lycurgus' date and life story?
results.ideal_answer: The sources cannot agree on almost anything about him. Plutarch opens his Life by warning that scarcely anything asserted by one historian is not contradicted by another — as to his family, his travels, the manner of his death, and above all the very age in which he lived. Grote sharpens the specific disagreement: Herodotus makes Lycurgus uncle and guardian of king Labotas of the Agid line (placing him about 996 BC), whereas all other accounts make him a younger brother of the rival Proclid line.
results.answer:
The date of Lycurgus, the legendary Spartan lawgiver, is profoundly uncertain. As Plutarch notes, "scarcely anything is asserted by one of them which is not called into question or contradicted by the rest" and "they cannot, by any means, be brought to an agreement as to the very age in which he lived" [10]. Aristotle held that Lycurgus flourished at the time of Iphitus and jointly established the Olympic truce, citing his name on a copper quoit used in the games [10][9]. In contrast, Eratosthenes and Apollodorus, computing by the Spartan king lists, argued he was "much more ancient than the institution of the Olympic games" [10]. Timaeus conjectured that there were two men named Lycurgus whose deeds were combined, with the elder "not long after Homer" [10][9]. Herodotus, reporting the Spartan tradition, places Lycurgus as the uncle and guardian of King Labôtas, which would put him about 220 years before the first recorded Olympiad, roughly 996 B.C. [4][6]. Grote summarizes: "There was no certainty attainable, even in the third century before the Christian era, respecting the date or parentage of Lykurgus" [9]. Bury goes further, suggesting Lycurgus was not a historical man at all, but a god — "an Arcadian deity or 'hero', — perhaps some form of the Arcadian Zeus Lycaeus" whose name meant "wolf-repeller" and who had a shrine at Lacedaemon [1].

Regarding his life story, the most detailed account comes from Plutarch and later writers compiled by Grote. Lycurgus was a member of the Spartan royal line, son of Eunomus (or Prytanis according to Simonides) and brother to Polydektes [3][8]. When Polydektes died, his pregnant widow offered to marry Lycurgus if he would make away with the unborn child; he feigned acceptance but as soon as a boy was born, he proclaimed the infant Charilaus as the rightful king, serving as his guardian [8][7]. Slanderous accusations from the queen-mother's faction forced him into voluntary exile to protect the young king's claim [8][7]. He traveled first to Crete, where he studied the laws of various cities, and then visited Ionia and Egypt — some authors even added Libya, Iberia, and India — and is said to have obtained a copy of the Homeric poems in Ionia [7][8].

Returning to Sparta, Lycurgus reformed the entire state with the approval of the Delphic oracle. Herodotus reports that the Pythia greeted him as "beloved by Zeus, and by all the Olympic gods" and revealed the Spartan institutions to him [6]. After establishing his laws, he bound the Spartans by oath to observe them until his return, then departed to Delphi. The oracle confirmed the laws were "excellent, and that the people, while it observed them, should live in the height of renown" [5]. To ensure the Spartans never released themselves from their oath, Lycurgus resolved to die in exile. He "made an end of himself by a total abstinence from food" [5]. Conflicting traditions place his death in Cirrha, Elis, or Crete; Aristoxenus adds that the Cretans burned his body and scattered his ashes into the sea at his own request, to prevent any pretext for altering his institutions [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is overwhelmingly grounded in the passages, but it attributes the story of Lycurgus' body being burned and his ashes scattered into the sea to Aristoxenus, when source [2] explicitly attributes that account to Aristocrates; this wrong-author error fits the definition of score 4. | completeness: The answer thoroughly covers both parts of the question—Lycurgus' date (including uncertainty, competing chronologies, and scholarly debate) and his life story (from family and guardianship through travels, reforms, and death). | attribution: The answer excellently surfaces the extensive disagreements among the sources regarding Lycurgus's dates, parentage, and death, clearly attributing each distinct version and conflicting tradition to its respective author in prose.

results.question: Who was to blame for losing the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: Both sources fault the consul Varro (Gaius Terentius), but with different emphasis. Polybius lays the disaster squarely on him personally — his flight to Venusia was "as disgraceful to himself as his conduct in office had been disastrous to his country." Mommsen, while equally scornful of "the hero of the pavement," frames the deeper fault as systemic: the old custom of alternating supreme command between the two consuls day by day, which forced the cautious Paullus to yield to Varro's will.
results.answer:
The ancient sources overwhelmingly blame Gaius Terentius Varro for the disaster at Cannae. Cassius Dio states it most directly: "THE RECKLESSNESS OF TERENTIUS AND THE SUBMISSIVENESS OF PAULUS COMPASSED THEIR DEFEAT" [6]. Plutarch reports that Fabius Maximus urged the other consul, Aemilius Paullus, to oppose "Varro's ignorant eagerness" and that "these good measures were defeated by the importunity of Varro" — it was Varro who, on his day of command, posted the army at Cannae and gave the signal for battle [1]. Polybius likewise notes that Terentius, "from inexperience, of a contrary opinion" to the cautious Aemilius, ordered the advance on his day of command despite his colleague's protests [5]. Appian describes Varro as "a demagogue" who insisted on a speedy battle, while Aemilius thought it best to exhaust Hannibal by delay [3]. Mommsen calls Varro "an incapable man" whose election was a disaster [4], and Smith notes that the Senate viewed his election "with dismay, as Varro possessed no military experience" [2].

While the primary blame falls on Varro's rashness, some sources also fault Paullus for failing to restrain him — Cassius Dio cites his "submissiveness" [6], and Plutarch shows Paullus reluctantly yielding to Varro's pressure [1]. But the consensus is clear: Varro's recklessness and inexperience were the chief cause of the catastrophe.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer fabricates a direct quote attributed to Cassius Dio that does not appear in the provided source passage. | completeness: The answer fully identifies Varro as the primary person to blame and notes Paullus’s secondary submissiveness, directly and completely answering the question without omitting anything it asks for. | attribution: The sources agree in blaming Varro (with some adding Paulus's submissiveness), and the answer correctly attributes each source's specific details and quotes in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: How was the city of Nineveh captured and the Assyrian empire brought to an end?
results.ideal_answer: The accounts disagree on how Nineveh fell. In the version Herodotus and Rawlinson follow, it was taken by a Median assault: Cyaxares, allied with the Babylonian Nabopolassar, pressed the siege until the Assyrian king's means of resistance were exhausted and he burned himself in his palace. A rival tradition, reported from Ctesias, has the walls breached not by storm but by nature — an unusually wet season swelled the Tigris until it burst in and swept away more than two miles of wall, after which the king set fire to himself. Both traditions end with the king's self-immolation, but differ on whether the city fell to siege or to flood.
results.answer: The city of Nineveh was captured by a combined army of Medes and Babylonians under Cyaxares and Nabopolassar after a lengthy siege [1][2]. According to the account of Ctesias, as summarized by George Rawlinson, the siege lasted two full years and into the third, when an unusually rainy season caused the Tigris to swell and burst through the city wall, "sweeping away more than two miles of the wall" [1]. This breach made further resistance impossible, and the Assyrian king (called Sardanapalus by Ctesias, Saracus in other sources) ended the struggle by "burning himself, with his concubines and eunuchs and all his chief wealth, in his palace" [1]. Rawlinson notes that the suicide of the king is also attested by Abydenus (likely following Berosus) and should be accepted as a fact, while the detail of the river's role in the destruction finds a possible echo in the prophet Nahum's prophecy [3][4]. Thus the Assyrian empire fell with the destruction of its capital.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including the combined Medo-Babylonian siege, the Tigris breaching the wall, the king’s self-immolation, and the corroboration by Abydenus and Nahum—is directly supported by the provided passages, with no invented facts or unsupported inferences. | completeness: The answer fully covers how Nineveh was captured (the Median-Babylonian siege and the Tigris flood breach) and how the Assyrian empire ended (the king’s self-immolation and the capital’s destruction), and omitting the rival Herodotean assault-only tradition is acceptable extra detail beyond what the question explicitly asked. | attribution: The sources agree and provide complementary details, and the answer accurately attributes the specific claims to Ctesias, Abydenus, and Nahum as discussed by Rawlinson without any misattribution.

results.question: How large was the army that Xerxes led against Greece?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus gives an enormous figure: the land army alone amounted to one hundred and seventy myriads (1,700,000), a total he says was reached by herding the troops in ten-thousand-man batches into a walled enclosure and counting how many times it filled. Rawlinson rejects the number as untestable and almost certainly grossly exaggerated — armies, he notes, are never really counted, and so crude a method invited inflation — though he allows the host probably did exceed a million.
results.answer: Herodotus gives a very precise total for the army of Xerxes: 5,283,220 men ("five hundred and twenty-eight myriads three thousand two hundred and twenty") when it reached Sepias and Thermopylae [2]. This figure includes both the fighting men and the attendants, whom he supposed to be equal in number to the combatants — the combatant force alone he estimated at 2,641,610 [2]. Modern scholars, however, consider these ancient numbers to be gross exaggerations; J. B. Bury suggests that the actual land forces may have amounted to about 300,000, and hardly more [5], while George Grote thinks any precise estimate is impossible but still regards the army as probably the largest ever assembled in ancient times [6].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the cited passages: Herodotus's precise total and combatant figure come from [2], Bury's estimate of 300,000 land forces comes from [5], and Grote's view about the impossibility of precise estimates and the unprecedented size of the army comes from [6]. | completeness: It fully answers the question by supplying Herodotus's reported figures for the total and combatant forces as well as modern scholarly estimates and skepticism. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces the disagreement between ancient and modern estimates and clearly attributes each distinct claim to its respective author in prose.

results.question: How did Hannibal die when he was cornered at the court of King Prusias?
results.ideal_answer: The common account is that Hannibal, finding the house surrounded by Prusias's soldiers (sent at the demand of the Roman envoy Flamininus), took poison he had long kept ready and drank it; Livy and Appian both give this poison version. Plutarch, however, records rival traditions about the manner of his death: some said he strangled himself by twisting his cloak about his neck with a servant's help, and others that he drank bull's blood.
results.answer: When Hannibal realized his house was surrounded by soldiers sent by King Prusias at the instigation of the Roman envoy Flamininus, he took poison that he had long kept ready for such an emergency. Livy narrates that after finding every exit blocked, Hannibal “called for poison, which he had long kept in readiness to meet such an event, and said, ‘Let us release the Romans from their long anxiety …’ Then imprecating curses … he drank off the contents of the cup” [1]. Cassius Dio similarly records that Hannibal, unable to escape, “committed suicide” [3] and elsewhere specifies that he “died by drinking poison near Bithynia, in a certain place called Libyssa” [4]. Mommsen adds that Hannibal “had long been prepared” and “took poison” when assassins closed in [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including Hannibal's house being surrounded by Prusias's soldiers at Flamininus's instigation, his taking poison he had long kept ready, and the specific quotations and details attributed to Livy, Mommsen, and Cassius Dio—is directly supported by the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully explains that Hannibal died by drinking poison he had kept ready after being cornered by Prusias's soldiers, directly answering the question without needing to include the additional rival traditions found in the reference. | attribution: The sources agree on the circumstances of Hannibal's death, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details to each respective source without any misattribution.

results.question: How large was Hannibal's army when he descended into Italy after crossing the Alps?
results.ideal_answer: The sources do not agree. Polybius gives a precise, low figure — twelve thousand Libyan and eight thousand Iberian infantry and not more than six thousand cavalry — and says he took it from a bronze tablet Hannibal himself set up at the Lacinian promontory. Livy reports that the number is by no means agreed upon by authors: the highest estimates run to a hundred thousand foot and twenty thousand horse, the lowest to twenty thousand foot and six thousand horse (this last agreeing with Polybius).
results.answer: The sources differ on the precise size of Hannibal's army after the Alpine crossing. Livy reports that the highest estimates give 100,000 foot and 20,000 horse, while the lowest give 20,000 foot and 6,000 horse; he notes that Cincius Alimentus (a prisoner of Hannibal) says the army was 80,000 foot and 10,000 horse, but that figure includes Gauls and Ligurians who probably joined later [1]. Polybius states that after crossing the Rhone Hannibal had 38,000 infantry and more than 8,000 cavalry, and that he "lost nearly half in the pass" — implying roughly 20,000 infantry and perhaps 4,000 cavalry survived [2]. Mommsen concurs, asserting that of the 50,000 infantry and 9,000 cavalry at the Pyrenees, more than half were lost, and that Hannibal "now, according to his own statement, numbered not more than 20,000 infantry... and 6000 cavalry" [4]. William Smith likewise writes that Hannibal "had with him no more than 20,000 foot and 6000 horse" [3]. The consensus among these sources is approximately 20,000 infantry and 6,000 cavalry.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, including the correct paraphrase of Livy’s range, the explicit figures in Mommsen and Smith, and a fair arithmetic inference from Polybius’s ‘nearly half’; no new facts are invented. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by reporting the conflicting ancient estimates, citing the relevant classical sources, and providing the consensus figure of roughly 20,000 infantry and 6,000 cavalry that survived the Alpine crossing. | attribution: The answer correctly identifies the differing estimates among the sources and explicitly attributes each specific figure or calculation to its respective author in prose.

results.question: How many Romans were killed at the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius puts the Roman dead at about seventy thousand — nearly the whole army, since of the infantry actually engaged only some three thousand escaped. Livy gives a smaller toll: forty thousand foot and two thousand seven hundred horse slain, with an equal number of allies among them.
results.answer:
The ancient and modern sources disagree on the Roman death toll at Cannae (216 BC):

- **Polybius** gives the highest figure: of the infantry actually engaged, "all the rest died nobly, to the number of **seventy thousand**" [2]. He notes that about 10,000 were captured and roughly 3,000 escaped.
- **Livy** reports a lower number: "Forty thousand foot, two thousand seven hundred horse … are said to have been slain" — approximately **42,700** dead [1]. He adds that 3,000 infantry and 300 cavalry were captured.
- **Appian** states that "in these few hours **50,000** of their soldiers were slain and a great many taken prisoners" [3].
- **Theodor Mommsen** (modern scholarship) follows Polybius: "of the 76,000 Romans who had taken their places in the line of battle **70,000** covered the field" [4].
- **William Smith** (19th‑century handbook) splits the difference, writing that "between **forty and fifty thousand** men are said to have fallen in the field" [5].

Thus there is no single agreed‑upon number. Livy gives the lowest figure (~42,700), Polybius and Mommsen the highest (~70,000), while Appian and Smith fall in the middle (~50,000).

results.question: What was the real cause of the Second Punic War?
results.ideal_answer: The sources frame the war's origin differently. Polybius insists that the siege of Saguntum and the crossing of the Ebro were only the first acts of the war, not its causes, and faults the historians who stop there; the true causes, he argues, lay deeper. Livy grounds the war in Saguntum and in Hannibal's inherited enmity — the boy's altar-oath to be an enemy of Rome, and Hamilcar's lasting grievance over the loss of Sicily and Sardinia.
results.answer: According to Polybius, the real causes of the Second Punic War were deeper than the immediate pretext of the Saguntum affair. He identifies three main causes: first, the resentment of Hamilcar Barca after the First Punic War, who "never relaxed in his determined purpose of revenge" [2]; second, the Roman seizure of Sardinia and the imposition of an additional tribute, which Polybius calls "the second and the most important cause of the subsequent war" [2]; and third, the Carthaginian success in Iberia, which gave them the confidence to embark on war [2]. Polybius emphasizes that Hannibal, when challenged by Roman ambassadors, "said not a word of the real cause, but alleged the fictitious one of the matter of Saguntum" [1]. He also notes that Hamilcar's animosity was passed to his sons, and that Hannibal had inherited a "hatred of Rome" from his father [3]. Livy adds the story of Hannibal's boyhood oath, sworn at an altar, to be an enemy of Rome [5], and Appian similarly mentions the oath and Hannibal's personal ambition to involve Carthage in a great war [4]. Thus, while the attack on Saguntum was the immediate occasion, the real causes lay in Carthaginian grievances, a desire for revenge, and the strategic confidence gained in Spain.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer incorrectly attributes the story of Hannibal's boyhood oath to Appian [4] when it actually appears in Polybius [3] and Livy [5], but all claims are otherwise grounded in the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by explaining Polybius's identified deeper causes (Hamilcar's grievances, Sardinia, Iberian confidence), contrasting them with the Saguntum pretext, and incorporating Livy's and Appian's perspectives on inherited hatred, leaving no required aspect unanswered. | attribution: The answer misattributes the mention of Hannibal's oath to Appian, whose provided passage does not contain this detail.

results.question: Who won the battle of Mantinea, and what did it settle?
results.ideal_answer: The verdict is disputed. Xenophon ends his history on a deliberately bleak note: both sides set up trophies and claimed victory, yet neither gained any territory or advantage, and uncertainty and confusion in Greece were tenfold greater after the battle than before — it settled nothing. Diodorus, by contrast, counts it a victory for the Boeotians (Thebans), the verdict the dying Epaminondas is given before he allows the spear to be drawn from his body.
results.answer: There were two major battles of Mantinea in antiquity. The first (418 BC) was won decisively by Sparta. In Thucydides' account, the Spartan victory — in which the Lacedaemonian centre and right routed the enemy while their own left wing broke — **"wiped out"** all imputations of cowardice that had clung to Sparta since Sphacteria, restoring **"the men themselves"** in Greek eyes as **"the same as ever"** [15]. The second battle (362 BC) was tactically won by Thebes under Epaminondas, whose wedge formation broke the opposing line, but his mortal wound stripped the victory of practical meaning. Its settlement was equivocal: while both sides claimed victory, the Lacedaemonians were forced to solicit a burial truce — the understood **"confession of defeat"** [3] — and the subsequent peace merely **"recogni[sed] the statu quo"**, leaving Megalopolis and independent Messene intact against Sparta's protests [11]. As the Thebans were no longer able to enforce their superiority, **"Thebes did not gain by the battle either city, or territory, or dominion"** — but they preserved their Arcadian allies and anti-Spartan frontier [6].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is grounded throughout, but the tactical detail that the Spartan left wing broke while the centre and right routed the enemy in the first battle is drawn from Grote [5] yet attributed to Thucydides [15], constituting a wrong-source attribution. | completeness: The answer fully addresses who won the relevant battle (Thebes tactically, but practically no one) and its inconclusive settlement, while the added discussion of the earlier 418 BC battle is extra correct detail that does not reduce completeness. | attribution: The answer correctly distinguishes between the two historical battles of Mantinea and accurately attributes the specific details and quotes to their respective supporting sources without any misattribution.

results.question: What happened to the captured Athenian generals Nicias and Demosthenes after the Sicilian disaster?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides says the Syracusans put Nicias and Demosthenes to death — butchered against the wishes of the Spartan Gylippus, who had hoped to carry the captured enemy generals home to Sparta. Plutarch preserves a rival account, which he credits to Timaeus: that they were not executed by the Syracusans at all, but, warned by a message from Hermocrates and with the connivance of their guards, were allowed to put an end to themselves.
results.answer: After the Sicilian disaster, the captured Athenian generals Nicias and Demosthenes were put to death by the Syracusans. According to Thucydides, they were "butchered, against the will of Gylippus", who had hoped to take them alive to Sparta as a triumph [1]. Demosthenes was especially hated as an enemy of Sparta due to the affair of Pylos, while Nicias was generally well-regarded by the Spartans because of his efforts to secure peace and the release of prisoners [1]. However, some Syracusans who had been in correspondence with Nicias feared he might be tortured and reveal their dealings, while others, particularly the Corinthians, worried that his wealth would allow him to bribe his way to freedom and cause further harm; these parties persuaded the allies to execute him [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including the butchering of both generals, Gylippus’s opposition, the divergent Spartan views of Demosthenes and Nicias, and the motives of the Syracusans and Corinthians—is fully supported by passage [1] with no invented facts or quotes. | completeness: The answer fully and correctly states that both generals were executed by the Syracusans against Gylippus's wishes, and omitting Plutarch's rival suicide account is acceptable since that is extra contextual detail beyond what the question asked. | attribution: The answer uses only one source and correctly attributes all claims to Thucydides without any misattribution.

results.question: How do the sources judge Alcibiades?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides judges that his lawless personal extravagance — horses and expenditure beyond his means — made the people fear him as an aspiring tyrant, "and this later on had not a little to do with the ruin of the Athenian state," though his public war conduct was excellent. Plutarch fixes on his chameleon-like versatility, adapting to Spartan austerity or Persian pomp at will. Grote, the modern voice, grants him boldness, resource, and vigor "not surpassed by any contemporary Greek" while damning his want of public principle. Bury, a second modern voice, judges him a born politician but no statesman — lacking the purpose, perseverance, and self-control that statesmanship demands.
results.answer: The sources present a mixed but predominantly critical judgment of Alcibiades: they admire his military and political abilities while condemning his ambition, personal conduct, and lack of moral principle. Plutarch identifies "ambition and desire of superiority" as the strongest of his passions, alongside many inconsistencies [1]. Thucydides notes that although his public direction of the war "was as good as could be desired," his private "licence in his own life and habits" gave offence and caused the people to suspect him of aspiring to tyranny [3]. Grote synthesizes this view, declaring that "eminent capacities for action and command are so thoroughly marred by an assemblage of bad moral qualities" and that his ends were "exorbitant ambition and vanity" and his means "rapacious as well as reckless" [2]. Thus, all three sources agree on his extraordinary talent but see his character as fundamentally flawed.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim and quote is accurately grounded in the cited passages, with no invented facts, names, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer fully captures how the sources judge Alcibiades by synthesizing Thucydides, Plutarch, and Grote on his talents versus character flaws, and omitting Bury (extra detail from the reference) does not leave any part of the question explicitly unanswered. | attribution: The sources agree in their overall assessment of Alcibiades, and the answer correctly attributes the specific points and quotes to each respective source without any misattribution.

results.question: How do the sources judge Themistocles?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus reports he was esteemed throughout Hellas as "the ablest by far of the Hellenes" and uniquely honored at Sparta. Thucydides' obituary calls his untaught native genius for instant judgment and prediction "quite extraordinary and unparalleled." Grote balances the picture: his rapacity made him hated and feared among the allies, and the suspicions of treasonable medism at Athens grew from his own corrupt and double-faced conduct. Bury, a later modern voice, credits the man of genius who made Athens a sea-power as the greatest of all her statesmen.
results.answer:
The sources judge Themistocles with a mixture of admiration for his genius and condemnation for his moral failings. Thucydides offers the most effusive praise, declaring that he 'exhibited the most indubitable signs of genius' and was 'the best judge in those sudden crises which admit of little or of no deliberation, and the best prophet of the future,' adding that his capacity was 'quite extraordinary and unparalleled' [5]. Herodotus similarly records that after Salamis, Themistocles 'was loudly reported of and was esteemed throughout Hellas to be the man who was the ablest by far of the Hellenes' and notes that the Spartans gave him unprecedented honors, escorting him with their picked men [15]. Herodotus also portrays him as clever and persuasive, though not without cunning, noting that he secretly sent a message to Xerxes to build a 'store of gratitude' with the Persian king as a potential refuge [17].

On the negative side, Plutarch preserves the scathing verses of Timocreon, who calls him 'the liar, traitor, cheat, who, to gain his filthy pay,' abandoned his friend and extorted money, 'filling evermore his purse' [10]. Plutarch himself records that Themistocles was 'open to bribes' and provoked the people with vanity, building a temple to Artemis Aristobule (Diana of Best Counsel) to imply that his counsel had been the best for all Greece [4]. He was eventually ostracized, and after the exposure of Pausanias, indicted for treason and fled to Persia, where he lived as a wealthy pensioner of the Great King [3].

The modern historian George Grote synthesizes these judgments, calling him a man of 'eminent' intelligence but 'grossly corrupt in the exercise of power,' who 'ended a glorious life by years of deep disgrace, with the forfeiture of all Hellenic esteem and brotherhood—a rich man, an exile, a traitor, and a pensioner of the Great King' [9]. Similarly, J. B. Bury notes that 'the man of genius had his weaknesses,' being 'accessible to bribes' and betrayed by vanity, which 'conduced to unpopularity' and ultimately his banishment [1]. Thus the sources agree on his extraordinary natural abilities but differ in emphasis: Thucydides focuses on his genius, Herodotus on his cleverness and honors, while Plutarch and the modern historians emphasize his greed, vanity, and disgraceful end.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is grounded in the sources but misattributes the claim that Themistocles was 'open to bribes' to Plutarch rather than J. B. Bury. | completeness: It fully answers the question by detailing the sources' mixed judgments of Themistocles, covering both their praise for his genius and criticism of his corruption. | attribution: The sources do not make incompatible claims but rather emphasize different aspects of Themistocles' character and legacy, and the answer correctly attributes each distinct perspective to the respective authors in clear prose without misattribution.

results.question: How is the disaster at Cannae described?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius gives the soldier's accounting: of six thousand Roman horse only seventy escaped with Varro, the infantry "died nobly" to the number of seventy thousand, both sides fighting with conspicuous gallantry. Dio's fragment calls it the most fearful disaster Rome ever suffered, preceded by portents and Sibylline prophecy. Mommsen, the modern judge, treats the "unexampled success" as the moment Hannibal's grand political combination against Rome seemed to mature. Appian tallies the toll — 50,000 Roman dead in a few hours, with senators, every tribune and centurion, and both their best generals among them. Livy registers the reaction at home: never, while the city stood safe, was there so great a panic, at the loss of two consular armies and their consuls.
results.answer:
The disaster at Cannae is described across the sources as an annihilating defeat of unprecedented scale, a near-total destruction of the Roman army, and a psychological shock that brought Rome to the brink of despair.

**Scale of the slaughter and capture**
The casualty figures are staggering. Livy reports that "forty thousand foot, two thousand seven hundred horse... are said to have been slain" and "three thousand infantry and three hundred cavalry are said to have been captured" [1]. Polybius gives a higher figure: of those actually engaged, "all the rest died nobly, to the number of seventy thousand" [4]. Appian states that "in these few hours 50,000 of their soldiers were slain and a great many taken prisoners" [5]. Mommsen, synthesizing the tradition, writes that "of the 76,000 Romans who had taken their places in the line of battle 70,000 covered the field" [3]. The losses included not just common soldiers but the leadership of the state: Livy lists "both the quaestors of the consuls... twenty-one military tribunes; several who had passed the offices of consul, praetor, and aedile... moreover eighty, either senators, or who had borne those offices" [1]. Mommsen similarly notes "the consul Lucius Paullus, the proconsul Gnaeus Servilius, two-thirds of the staff-officers, and eighty men of senatorial rank" among the dead [3].

**The fate of the commanders**
The consul Aemilius Paullus died on the field. Plutarch describes him, "his body all covered with wounds, and his soul no less wounded with grief," sitting on a stone awaiting death, refusing the horse offered by Cornelius Lentulus [2]. The other consul, Varro, fled: Livy says he "made good his escape to Venusia with about seventy horse" [1]; Polybius calls his flight "as disgraceful to himself, as his conduct in office had been disastrous to his country" [4]. Appian bluntly calls Varro "the most worthless one, who was the cause of the calamity" [5].

**The character of the defeat**
The sources emphasize that this was not merely a defeat but an annihilation. Livy compares it to the disaster at the Allia, but says Cannae was "more important and more horrible with respect to the slaughter of the army; for with respect to the flight at the Allia, as it betrayed the city, so it preserved the army. At Cannae, scarcely seventy accompanied the flying consul: almost the whole army shared the fate of the other who died" [1]. Mommsen declares: "Never, perhaps, was an army of such size annihilated on the field of battle so completely, and with so little loss to its antagonist, as was the Roman army at Cannae" [3]. Polybius notes the tactical lesson: "a lesson to posterity that in actual war it is better to have half the number of infantry, and the superiority in cavalry, than to engage your enemy with an equality in both" [4].

**The psychological impact on Rome**
The news threw Rome into panic. Livy writes: "Never when the city was in safety was there so great a panic and confusion within the walls of Rome... it was not one wound upon another which was announced, but a multiplied disaster, the loss of two consular armies, together with the two consuls: and that now there was neither any Roman camp, nor general nor soldiery" [8]. He adds that "no other nation surely would not have been overwhelmed by such an accumulation of misfortune" [8]. Cassius Dio reports that the people "heard of the defeat but did not believe it" and only later "were filled with sorrow" [10]. Appian describes the scene: "multitudes thronged the streets uttering lamentations for their relatives, calling on them by name, and bewailing their own fate as soon to fall into the enemy's hands. Women went to the temples with their children and prayed that there might sometime be an end to the calamities to the city" [7]. Polybius says the Romans "despaired of retaining their supremacy over the Italians, and were in the greatest alarm, believing their own lives and the existence of their city to be in danger, and every moment expecting that Hannibal would be upon them" [6].

**The battle's enduring fame**
The battle is consistently framed as a disaster of legendary proportions. Livy calls it "equal in celebrity to the defeat at the Allia" [1]. Appian says it "is still famous among the Romans as a disaster" [5]. William Smith summarizes it as a battle in which "the immense army of the Romans was not only defeated, but annihilated" [9].

results.question: How do historians ancient and modern assess Pericles?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides' famous verdict: by rank, ability, and known integrity he led the multitude instead of being led, so that "what was nominally a democracy became in his hands government by the first citizen." Plutarch gives the deathbed self-assessment — his proudest claim was that "no Athenian, through my means, ever wore mourning." Grote notes that his immense ascendancy and unparalleled eloquence are attested even more forcibly by his enemies than his friends. Bury, more measured, rates him a man of finer fibre than Themistocles yet no statesman of originative genius — one who perfected the ideas of others rather than creating them.
results.answer:
Ancient and modern historians assess Pericles in varied but often admiring terms. The ancient historian Thucydides, as reported by George Grote, judged Pericles' leadership as "moderate, cautious, conservative, and successful," describing him as "uniformly keeping back the people from rash enterprises" and maintaining the state's forces in readiness [1]. Thucydides' account, Grote notes, "contradicts, in the most unqualified manner, the reproaches commonly made against Periklês, of having corrupted the Athenian people by distributions of the public money" [5]. Plutarch, another ancient source, emphasizes Pericles' extraordinary oratorical power, nicknaming him "the Olympian" for his "thundering and lightning" when he spoke [3]. He also records the posthumous judgment that "there never had been in nature such a disposition as his was, more moderate and reasonable in the height of that state he took upon him" [4]. Plutarch further notes his mild and liberal conduct toward opponents [6].

Among modern historians, Grote himself endorses Thucydides' favorable view, arguing that Pericles' long ascendency and unparalleled eloquence are facts "attested not less by his enemies than by his friends" [6]. He dismisses the charge that Pericles corrupted the people as inconsistent with the evidence [1]. J. B. Bury offers a more measured assessment: Pericles "was not like Themistocles a statesman of originative genius. He originated little; he elaborated the ideas of others" [2]. Yet Bury acknowledges his "statesmanship of no ordinary tact" in preserving peace and his ability to lead through "wisdom and eloquence" rather than vulgar popularity, though he notes Pericles was "disdainful of the vulgar herd" [7]. Overall, both ancient and modern historians recognize Pericles as a figure of immense influence, eloquence, and personal integrity, though they differ on the extent of his originality and the nature of his democratic leadership.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is largely grounded but misattributes Grote's observation about Pericles' 'mild and liberal conduct toward opponents' to Plutarch. | completeness: The answer fully covers the question by detailing assessments from both ancient historians (Thucydides, Plutarch) and modern historians (Grote, Bury), addressing their views on leadership, character, eloquence, and originality without omitting any required component. | attribution: The sources provide complementary assessments without making incompatible claims, and the answer accurately attributes the distinct contributions to the correct authors in prose without misattribution.

results.question: What did ancient writers know and say about Britain and its people?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar, the first eyewitness, reports inland Britons who sow no corn, live on milk and flesh, dress in skins, dye themselves blue with woad, and hold wives in common. Tacitus weighs their origins from physical type — the ruddy-haired Caledonians suggesting German stock, the swarthy Silures Iberian. Strabo maps the island as a triangle parallel to the Gallic coast, roughly 4,300–4,400 stadia along its longest side. Cassius Dio, writing later, divides the Britons into two great races, the Caledonians and the Maeatians, who dwell coatless and shoeless in tents, hold their women in common, and rear their children as a community.
results.answer:
Several ancient writers left substantial accounts of Britain and its inhabitants, drawing on firsthand military experience, traders' reports, and earlier geographies. The four principal sources in the corpus are Julius Caesar, Strabo, Tacitus, and Cassius Dio.

**Geography and shape**

Caesar described Britain as "triangular in its form" with one side facing Gaul (about 500 miles), another towards Spain and the west (700 miles), and a third towards the north (800 miles), making the whole island "about 2000 miles in circumference" [1]. Tacitus notes that Livy and Fabius Rusticus "have likened the figure of Britain to an oblong target, or a two-edged axe" exclusive of Caledonia, which "is gradually contracted in form of a wedge" [7]. Tacitus also records that the Roman fleet was the first to prove "that Britain was an island" by sailing around the northern coast [7]. Cassius Dio confirms that "it is an island" and gives its length as seven thousand stades [8]. Tacitus adds that the sea around northern Britain is "reported to be sluggish and laborious to the rower" and that tides "penetrate into the heart of the country" [7]. The climate, Caesar says, "is more temperate than in Gaul, the colds being less severe" [1]; Tacitus similarly notes the sky is "deformed by clouds and frequent rains; but the cold is never extremely rigorous" and that days are markedly longer than in Rome — at the island's extremity the night is "so short, that the close and return of day is scarcely distinguished by a perceptible interval" [6]. Strabo reports that even on clear days "throughout the whole day the sun is only visible for three or four hours about noon" owing to persistent mist [3].

**Peoples and origins**

Tacitus treats the question of origins as "involved in the obscurity usual among barbarians" but deduces differences from physical appearance: the "ruddy hair and large limbs of the Caledonians point out a German derivation," the "swarthy complexion and curled hair of the Silures" together with their position opposite Spain suggest descent from ancient Iberians, and those nearest Gaul resemble the Gauls [4]. Caesar reports a tradition that the interior inhabitants "were born in the island itself" while the maritime peoples came from Belgic Gaul "for the purpose of plunder and making war" and kept the names of their parent states [1]. Cassius Dio distinguishes "two principal races of the Britons—the Caledonians and the Maeatians" — the Maeatians live near the cross-wall dividing the island, the Caledonians beyond them, both inhabiting "wild and waterless mountains, desolate and swampy plains, holding no walls, nor cities, nor tilled fields, but living by pasturage and hunting and a few fruit trees" [8].

**Customs and way of life**

Caesar reports that the inhabitants of Kent are "the most civilised" and "do not differ much from the Gallic customs," while most inland people "do not sow corn, but live on milk and flesh, and are clad with skins" [2]. All Britons "dye themselves with wood, which occasions a bluish colour" to appear more terrifying in battle, and "wear their hair long, and have every part of their body shaved except their head and upper lip" [2]. He also records that "ten and even twelve have wives common to them, and particularly brothers among brothers, and parents among their children" [2]. Strabo likewise notes the Britons are "more simple and barbarous" than the Kelts — some "though possessing plenty of milk, have not skill enough to make cheese, and are totally unacquainted with horticulture and other matters of husbandry" [3]. He adds that "forests are their cities": they enclose a space with felled trees, build huts, and lodge their cattle there "though not for any long continuance" [3]. Cassius Dio says the Caledonians and Maeatians "dwell coatless and shoeless in tents, possess their women in common, and rear all the offspring as a community" and "can endure hunger and cold and any kind of wretchedness" — including plunging into swamps "for many days with only their heads above water" [8].

**Military practices**

Tacitus records that "their military strength consists in infantry; some nations also make use of chariots in war" in which "the most honorable person guides the reins, while his dependents fight from the chariot" [4]. Strabo confirms that "in their wars they make use of chariots for the most part, as do some of the Kelts" [3]. Cassius Dio describes the Britons going into battle "on chariots with small, swift horses" and carrying a "shield and short spear, with a bronze apple attached to the end of the ground-spike, so that when the instrument is shaken it may clash and inspire the enemy with terror; they also have daggers" [8]. Tacitus notes the Britons' political disunity works to Rome's advantage: they were "formerly governed by kings, but at present they are divided in factions and parties among their chiefs; and this want of union … is the most favorable circumstance to us," since "while they engage singly, they are all subdued" [4].

**Resources and economy**

Caesar says Britain produces "tin … in the midland regions; in the maritime, iron; but the quantity of it is small: they employ brass, which is imported" [1]. He notes they use "brass or iron rings, determined at a certain weight, as their money" and that the number of cattle is great [1]. Tacitus records that "the earth yields gold and silver and other metals" and that "the ocean produces pearls, but of a cloudy and livid hue" [6]. Ammianus Marcellinus also confirms that pearls are "produced and collected in the remote parts of the British sea; though of an inferior value" [9]. Strabo enumerates exports: "corn, cattle, gold, silver, and iron … and also skins, and slaves, and dogs sagacious in hunting; the Kelts use these, as well as their native dogs, for the purposes of war" [3].

**Historical context and interactions with Rome**

Strabo notes Julius Caesar "twice passed over to the island, but quickly returned, having effected nothing of consequence" owing to troubles in Gaul and the loss of ships at the full moon, though he "gained two or three victories over the Britons" and brought back "hostages and slaves and much other booty" [3][10]. Tacitus remarks that Caesar "may be considered rather to have transmitted the discovery than the possession of the country to posterity" [6]. Strabo, writing under Augustus, says that "some of the princes there have, by their embassies and solicitations, obtained the friendship of Augustus Caesar" and brought the island into closer union with Rome, with moderate duties paid on imports and exports [10]. Tacitus observes that the Britons "cheerfully submit to levies, tributes, and the other services of government, if they are not treated injuriously; but such treatment they bear with impatience, their subjection only extending to obedience, not to servitude" [6].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims, including direct quotations and paraphrases, are fully grounded in the provided source passages with no invented facts, names, quotes, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer comprehensively covers ancient writers’ accounts of Britain’s geography, climate, peoples, origins, customs, military practices, resources, and interactions with Rome, fully addressing everything the broad question asks for. | attribution: The answer draws on several different sources and correctly attributes each distinct contribution to the specific ancient writer in prose, with no misattributions.

results.question: How is Cleopatra's death reported across the sources?
results.ideal_answer: All three sources report the famous asp while admitting uncertainty. Plutarch describes her found dead in royal ornaments on a golden bed, Iras dying at her feet and Charmion adjusting her diadem — but says "what really took place is known to no one," weighing the asp in the figs against poison in a hollow bodkin. Dio likewise: "no one knows clearly in what manner she perished," only slight indentations on her arm. Suetonius adds that Augustus, wanting her alive for his triumph, sent the Psylli snake-charmers to suck out the poison.
results.answer:
The ancient sources agree that Cleopatra committed suicide to avoid being paraded in Octavian's triumph, but they differ on the exact method. All three main accounts mention the bite of an asp (a venomous snake), though they present it with varying degrees of certainty and alongside alternative explanations.

Plutarch provides the most detailed narrative. He describes how Cleopatra, after feasting, sent a sealed letter to Caesar asking to be buried with Antony, then shut herself in the monument with her two women, Iras and Charmion. When the doors were opened, she was found "stone-dead, lying upon a bed of gold, set out in all her royal ornaments," with Iras dying at her feet and Charmion adjusting her diadem [3]. Plutarch then reports multiple versions of the means: some say an asp was brought in a basket of figs and she held out her bare arm to be bitten; others say the snake was kept in a vase and provoked with a golden spindle; still others claim she carried poison in a hollow bodkin hidden in her hair. He stresses that "what really took place is known to no one" and notes that no asp was seen in the monument, only "something like the trail of it" on the sand outside, and that some reported "two faint puncture-marks" on her arm. He adds that Caesar himself seemed to credit the asp story, since his triumph featured "a figure of Cleopatra, with an asp clinging to her" [2].

Cassius Dio likewise reports uncertainty: "No one knows clearly in what manner she perished, for there were found merely slight indentations on her arm." He gives two main versions: some say she applied an asp brought in a water-jar or among flowers; others say she used a poisoned needle she kept in her hair, making a small scratch on her arm and dipping the needle in the blood. He adds that she died with her two handmaidens, and that her eunuch "presented himself voluntarily to the serpents" and died by their bites. Caesar, shocked, sent for the Psylli—a tribe believed immune to snake venom—to try to suck out the poison and revive her [1].

Suetonius is the most concise. He writes that Caesar "anxiously wished to save her for his triumph; and when she was supposed to have been bit to death by an asp, he sent for the Psylli to endeavour to suck out the poison" [10]. He does not mention alternative methods.

In summary, all three sources report the asp as the presumed cause, but Plutarch and Cassius Dio explicitly acknowledge the uncertainty and record competing traditions (poisoned needle, hidden poison). The detail of the Psylli being summoned appears in both Cassius Dio and Suetonius. Plutarch alone includes the poignant scene of the dying Charmion adjusting Cleopatra's diadem and the detail of the puncture-marks.

results.question: What do the sources say about Alexander's final illness and death?
results.ideal_answer: Both Plutarch and Arrian follow the Royal Diary: a fever after drinking with Medius, worsening day by day as he continued bathing and sacrificing, until he lay speechless — Arrian preserving the scene of the soldiers filing past while he could only greet them with his eyes — and died. Both record the later poisoning story; Plutarch calls it "a mere made-up story," noting the body stayed uncorrupted for days, and Arrian notes Aristobulus and Ptolemy agree with the diary, adding only the alleged last word that the kingdom would go "to the best." Diodorus, the other surviving ancient narrative, closes the twelve-year reign and reports the same poisoning rumour (Antipater's son Iollas as cup-bearer). Grote, the modern judge, weighs that rumour and concludes it was fomented by the enemies of Antipater and Cassander, above all the rancorous Olympias.
results.answer:
The sources provide a detailed, multi-layered account of Alexander's final illness and death, drawing primarily from the Royal Diary (Ephemerides) kept by his secretary Eumenes, as well as from later historians who discuss poison rumors.

**The Day-by-Day Illness (from the Royal Diary)**

Both Plutarch and Arrian reproduce the court journal's account. Alexander fell ill after a bout of heavy drinking at the house of Medius. He "drank all the next day, and was attacked with a fever" [2]. The fever persisted, and he was carried out daily to perform sacrifices. On the 24th he was "much worse" and had to be carried to the sacrifices; on the 25th he was removed to his palace across the river, where "he slept a little, but his fever did not abate, and when the generals came into his chamber, he was speechless" [2]. The Macedonians, thinking him dead, forced their way in and "let them all pass through unarmed along by his bedside" [5]. Python and Seleucus were sent to the temple of Serapis to ask whether to move him; the god answered "that they should not remove him" [5]. On the twenty-eighth day, in the evening, he died [5]. Arrian's version closely matches this, adding that when his officers entered, "he knew them indeed, but could no longer utter a word, being speechless" [6].

**The Poison Rumors**

Several sources report that a story of poisoning circulated later. Arrian states that "poison was sent for him by Antipater, from the effects of which he died" and that "the poison was procured for Antipater by Aristotle, who was now afraid of Alexander on account of Callisthenes" [7]. It was said to have been conveyed by Cassander "in the hoof of a mule" and administered by his brother Iollas, the royal cup-bearer [7]. Plutarch adds the detail that the poison was "water, deadly cold as ice, distilling from a rock in the district of Nonacris" and kept "in an ass's hoof; for it was so very cold and penetrating that no other vessel would hold it" [5]. However, both Arrian and Plutarch are skeptical. Arrian says he records these statements "rather that I may not seem to be ignorant that they have been made, than because I consider them worthy of credence" [7]. Plutarch notes that "most are of opinion that all this is a mere made-up story," citing as evidence that "during the dissensions among the commanders, which lasted several days, the body continued clear and fresh, without any sign of such taint or corruption, though it lay neglected in a close, sultry place" [5].

**Other Details and Variations**

Grote reports that some writers claimed Alexander swallowed the contents of a large goblet called "the cup of Herakles" and felt very ill after it [4]. He also notes that one of Alexander's last words, when asked to whom he bequeathed his kingdom, was "_To the strongest_" and that he took his signet ring and handed it to Perdikkas [4]. Grote observes that "throughout all the course of this malady no mention is made of any physician as having been consulted" [1], and that the poison report was likely "intentionally fomented, if not originally broached, by the party-enemies of Antipater and Kassander—especially by the rancorous Olympias" [1]. Bury, summarizing the Court Diary, concludes that "such is the punctilious and authentic account... but it is not sufficient to enable us to discover the precise nature of the fatal disease" [3].
