results.question: How do Roman writers describe the Germans' way of fighting?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar, from battlefield experience, describes them rapidly forming a phalanx so dense his soldiers leapt onto it to tear away shields from above. Tacitus, the ethnographer, details the framea — the short, sharp, all-purpose spear used at close quarters or thrown — the wedge-shaped battle line, the doctrine that giving ground to rally again is prudence rather than cowardice, and the carrying off of their dead mid-battle. Tacitus' Annals adds, through Germanicus' battle-speech, that the German goes without cuirass or helmet and carries only a flimsy shield of osiers or painted board. Ammianus, centuries later, shows the Alamanni in action: charging with javelins, hair streaming and eyes blazing, against the locked Roman shield-wall.
results.answer:
Roman writers describe the Germans' way of fighting as centered on infantry, with cavalry supported by agile foot soldiers, and characterized by impetuous charges, wedge formations, and a distinctive set of weapons and tactics.

**Caesar** (mid-1st century BC) describes the Germans' method of battle in detail: they fielded 6,000 horse and an equal number of "very active and courageous foot," each footman selected by a horseman for his own protection. These footmen accompanied the cavalry constantly: "to these the horse retired; these on any emergency rushed forward." If a rider fell wounded, they stood around him; if advance or retreat was needed, "so great, from practice, was their swiftness, that, supported by the manes of the horses, they could keep pace with their speed" [1]. Caesar also notes that the Germans drew up their forces "canton by canton" and surrounded their army with "chariots and waggons" on which they placed their women, to remove all hope of flight [6]. He further records that German matrons would decide by lots and divination whether to give battle, and that they declared "it was not the will of heaven that the Germans should conquer, if they engaged in battle before the new moon" [6].

**Tacitus** (late 1st century AD) provides the most systematic account. He reports that the Germans' principal strength lies in their infantry, so that in battle "these are intermixed with the cavalry" [3]. Their line of battle is "disposed in wedges" [3]. Their weapons: they seldom use swords or broad lances, but carry a spear called the *framea* with "an iron blade, short and narrow, but so sharp and manageable, that, as occasion requires, they employ it either in close or distant fighting" [3]. The foot have multiple missile weapons which "they hurl to an immense distance" [3]. Armor is minimal: "They are either naked, or lightly covered with a small mantle; and have no pride in equipage: their shields only are ornamented with the choicest colors. Few are provided with a coat of mail; and scarcely here and there one with a casque or helmet" [3]. Their cavalry either "bear down straight forwards, or wheel once to the right, in so compact a body that none is left behind the rest" [3]. Tacitus adds that to give ground, provided they rally again, is considered "rather as a prudent strategem, than cowardice," and that the greatest disgrace is to abandon one's shield [3].

**Ammianus Marcellinus** (4th century AD), describing the battle of Strasbourg, portrays the Germans as rushing to combat "with the utmost impetuosity, brandishing their javelins in their right hands, dashed among the squadrons of our cavalry, uttering fearful cries." He notes "their flowing hair bristling with their eagerness, and fury blazing from their eyes" [4]. In the press of battle, "some of the most powerful warriors among the barbarians pressed upon their antagonists with their knees, trying to throw them down; and in the general excitement men fought hand to hand, shield pressing upon shield" [4].

**Cassius Dio** (early 3rd century AD) describes a defensive formation used by Germans against Roman attack: they "opposed their shields on all sides of them and standing upright, apart from the press, proved hard to move by reason of their solidity" [5]. When the Romans attacked, many Germans fell but "were kept upright even when dead by the closeness of their formation" [5].

**Plutarch** (early 2nd century AD), writing of the Teutones and Ambrones (Germanic tribes), describes their wild battle cries: "nothing like the sighs and groans of men, but a sort of wild-beastlike howling and roaring, joined with threats and lamentations rising from the vast multitude, and echoed among the neighboring hills" [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim, quote, and paraphrase is grounded in the provided passages, and the authorial dates and battle name are correct standard glosses with no invented facts or unsupported outcomes. | completeness: The answer thoroughly addresses the broad question by detailing weapons, tactics, formations, armor, and battlefield behavior from multiple Roman writers, and it does not omit any explicitly required component of the question. | attribution: The sources do not disagree but rather provide complementary details from different authors, and the answer correctly attributes each distinct contribution to its respective author in prose without any misattributions.

results.question: How was Caesar's funeral described, and what did it unleash?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius describes the planned pyre in the Campus Martius overtaken by the crowd: two armed men fired the bier before the Rostra and the throng heaped on benches and court furniture; afterwards the populace ran with torches to the houses of Brutus and Cassius and lynched the wrong Cinna. Plutarch adds the cause: Antony's funeral oration, climaxing in the unfolding of Caesar's bloody, pierced garment, after which the mob burned the body on an improvised pyre and ran with the half-burnt brands to fire the murderers' houses. Cassius Dio reports the same inflamed mob and the lynching of the innocent Helvius Cinna in mistake for the praetor of that name. Appian adds the decisive stagecraft: a wax effigy of Caesar revolved on a mechanical device to display all twenty-three wounds, which tipped the crowd into burning the body and the senate-house.
results.answer:
Caesar's funeral, as described by Suetonius and Plutarch, was a spectacular and emotionally charged public event that unleashed violent retribution against the conspirators.

**The Funeral Ceremony**

A gilded tabernacle modeled on the temple of Venus Genitrix was erected before the Rostra, within which was an ivory bed covered with purple and cloth of gold. At the head stood a trophy bearing the bloodstained robe in which Caesar was slain [1]. Directions were given for everyone to carry funeral oblations from the city into the Campus Martius in whatever order they pleased, as the whole day would not have sufficed for a formal procession [1].

Instead of a traditional funeral panegyric, the consul Antony ordered a herald to proclaim the decree of the senate that had bestowed upon Caesar all honours, divine and human, along with the oath the senators had sworn for the defense of his person—and Antony added only a few words of his own [1]. The magistrates and those who had formerly held the highest offices carried the bier from the Rostra into the Forum [1].

At the funeral, to raise pity and indignation, a passage was sung from Pacuvius's tragedy *The Trial for Arms* containing the lines "That ever I, unhappy man, should save / Wretches, who thus have brought me to the grave!" and lines from Attilius's *Electra* to the same effect [1]. Plutarch adds that Antony, finding the multitude moved by his speech, unfolded Caesar's bloody garment, showed how many places it was pierced, and the number of his wounds [5].

**The Spontaneous Cremation**

While some proposed burning the body in the temple of Jupiter Capitolinus or Pompey's senate-house, on a sudden two men with swords and spears set fire to the bier with lighted torches [1]. The crowd immediately heaped upon it dry faggots, the tribunals and benches of the adjoining courts, and anything else at hand [1]. Musicians and players tore off the triumphal dresses they wore and threw them into the flames; veteran legionaries cast in their armor; and most ladies did the same with their ornaments and their children's bullae and mantles [1][3]. A multitude of foreigners also joined the mourning, especially the Jews, who for several nights frequented the spot where the body was burnt [3].

**What the Funeral Unleashed**

The funeral immediately unleashed mob violence. The populace ran from the funeral with torches straight to the houses of Brutus and Cassius, though they were repelled with difficulty [4]. In a grisly case of mistaken identity, they went in quest of Cornelius Cinna (who had spoken against Caesar the day before) but seized Helvius Cinna instead, murdering him and carrying his head about the city on the point of a spear [4]. Plutarch likewise reports that the multitude "snatched the brands that were half burnt out of the pile, and ran about the city to fire the houses of the murderers of Caesar" [5].

In the aftermath, the people erected in the Forum a column of Numidian marble nearly twenty feet high inscribed TO THE FATHER OF HIS COUNTRY, at which they long continued to offer sacrifices, make vows, and decide controversies, swearing by Caesar [4]. Appian adds that an altar was first erected there, which later became the temple of Caesar himself, as he was deemed worthy of divine honors [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided source passages, with no invented facts, quotes, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer thoroughly covers both parts of the question, describing the funeral ceremony and spontaneous cremation in detail and fully explaining the violent mob retribution it unleashed against the conspirators. | attribution: The sources agree and provide complementary details, and the answer accurately attributes the distinct contributions to Suetonius, Plutarch, and Appian in both prose and with citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: What did ancient writers believe about the sources and flooding of the Nile?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus reviews three Greek theories of the summer flood — the Etesian winds, the world-encircling Ocean, and melting snow — refutes them all (offering his own sun-driven explanation), and reports that no Egyptian, Libyan, or Greek professed to know the river's sources except a Sais scribe he didn't believe. Strabo, writing centuries later, says what the ancients only conjectured had become eyewitness knowledge: the Nile owes its rise to summer rains falling in the mountains of Upper Ethiopia. Ammianus, the latest voice, rehearses the same snow-melt, Etesian-wind and Ethiopian-rain theories but judges the river's sources as unknowable to posterity as they were in his own day.
results.answer: Ancient writers held a variety of beliefs about the sources and flooding of the Nile. Herodotus (5th century BC) reports that he could learn nothing from Egyptians or others, but he records three theories put forward by Greeks who "desired to gain distinction for cleverness": (1) the Etesian Winds block the river and cause it to rise; (2) the Nile flows from the Ocean that encircles the earth; (3) the river rises from melting snow. He refutes each: the wind theory fails because the Nile rises even when the winds do not blow and other rivers are not affected; the snow theory is impossible because the river "flows from the hottest parts to those which are cooler" and the inhabitants are black; the ocean theory "carried his tale into the region of the unknown" and cannot be refuted. Herodotus then advances his own explanation: the Sun, in winter driven to "the upper parts of Libya," draws moisture and drives it away to fall as rain in the upper country, feeding the Nile; in summer the Sun draws equally from all rivers, so the Nile is lower in winter because it "bears the burden alone" without rain, while in summer it is drawn less. He also reports the story of the scribe of the sacred treasury at Saïs, who claimed the sources were bottomless springs flowing from between two mountains named Crophi and Mophi, half the water going to Egypt and half to Ethiopia; Psammetichos let down a rope of many thousand fathoms without finding bottom. Herodotus was skeptical [1][6][3][4][8]. Strabo (c. 1st century BC – 1st century AD) notes that earlier writers merely conjectured, but "persons in later times learnt by experience as eye-witnesses, that the Nile owes its rise to summer rains, which fall in great abundance in Upper Ethiopia, particularly in the most distant mountains," a view confirmed by those sailing the Arabian Gulf and by elephant-hunters [7]. Ammianus Marcellinus (4th century AD) records the Etesian wind theory again, and adds that "King Juba, relying on the text of the Carthaginian books, affirms that the river rises in a mountain situated in Mauritania, which looks on the Atlantic Ocean." He also describes the regular inundation: the river increases from the sun's entry into Cancer until Libra, flowing at a great height for one hundred days; too much or too little flood is harmful, and no landowner wishes it to rise more than sixteen cubits [2][5].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The summary is largely accurate but introduces the unsupported specific name 'elephant-hunters' for Strabo's 'those who were sent out to hunt,' a minor invention not found in any provided passage. | completeness: The answer thoroughly addresses what the question asks, covering beliefs about both the sources and flooding of the Nile across Herodotus, Strabo, and Ammianus without omitting any core component. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the various ancient theories and beliefs about the Nile's sources and flooding to their respective authors in prose, with no misattributions or unresolved disagreements.

results.question: How do the sources describe the Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus, the eyewitness, gives the fullest narrative: Titus encamped about the city, and though he resolved to storm the temple, "that house, God had, for certain, long ago doomed it to the fire" — it was burned as the siege reached its terrible climax of famine and slaughter. Tacitus supplies the Roman view, describing how Titus pitched his camp before the walls and drew up his legions in order of battle against the Jews massed beneath their fortifications. Cassius Dio records the storming of the temple precinct and the city's fall — "thus was Jerusalem destroyed on the very day of Saturn, which even now the Jews reverence most."
results.answer:
The Roman siege and destruction of Jerusalem (A.D. 70) is described in greatest detail by Josephus in the *Wars of the Jews*, supplemented by Tacitus, Cassius Dio, and a brief note in Suetonius. While Josephus provides a vivid, often dramatic narrative of the entire campaign, Tacitus concentrates on the strategic situation and the religious character of the defenders, and Dio highlights the final catastrophe and its aftermath.

**Josephus** outlines in his preface that he will relate how Titus besieged and captured the city, how "the temple was burnt, against the consent of Caesar" and how "sacred things ... were snatched out of the fire," as well as "the destruction also of the entire city" [1]. The assault began with the Romans burning the gates and the silver that covered them, which "quickly carried the flames to the wood" and then "spread itself all on the sudden, and caught hold on the cloisters" [6]. The crucial moment came when, without orders, a soldier "snatched somewhat out of the materials that were on fire ... and set fire to a golden window" on the north side of the holy house [10]. Titus, who was resting in his tent, "rose up in great haste, and, as he was, ran to the holy house, in order to have a stop put to the fire," but the soldiers, driven by "passion," could not be restrained: "neither any persuasions nor any threatenings could restrain their violence" [7]. While the temple burned, "ten thousand of those that were caught were slain" without distinction of age, and "the flame was also carried a long way, and made an echo, together with the groans of those that were slain" [4]. After the city fell, Titus ordered the demolition of the entire city and temple, sparing only three great towers (Phasaelus, Hippicus, Mariamne) and the western wall: "for all the rest of the wall, it was so thoroughly laid even with the ground ... that there was left nothing to make those that came thither believe it had ever been inhabited" [2]. Titus himself marveled at the strength of the towers and declared, "We have certainly had God for our assistant in this war" [5].

**Tacitus** concentrates on the siege's beginning and the character of the city and its defenders. He notes that Titus encamped near Jerusalem after receiving three legions and substantial auxiliary forces [11]. The city was defended by two hills of great height fenced by walls "skilfully obliqued or bent inwards," with towers "raised to a height of sixty feet" where the hill aided, and "to a height of one hundred and twenty" where the ground fell [12]. He records portents — "hosts joining battle in the skies," a "voice of more than mortal tone" crying that the Gods were departing, and a belief that ancient prophecies foretold universal empire arising from Judaea [15]. Tacitus also states that "the total number of the besieged, of every age and both sexes, amounted to six hundred thousand" and that "men and women showed equal resolution" [15]. Because of the city's strength, Titus refrained from assault and "determined to proceed by earthworks and covered approaches" [15].

**Cassius Dio** records that the soldiers, due to superstition at first, "would not immediately rush in" to the temple, but Titus forced them; then the Jews fought with extraordinary desperation, "not subdued until a section of the temple was fired," after which many "went to meet death willingly" [3]. He specifies that Jerusalem was destroyed "on the very day of Saturn" (the Sabbath) and that thereafter the conquered Jews were ordered to pay an annual tribute of two denarii to Capitoline Jupiter [3].

**Suetonius** offers only a brief notice: in the final assault, Titus "slew seven of its defenders, with the like number of arrows, and took it upon his daughter's birth-day" [16]. A later editorial footnote confirms that Jerusalem was "taken, sacked, and burnt" after a two-year siege, on the Sabbath, in the second year of Vespasian [17].

Thus, while all sources agree that the city and temple were destroyed by fire and the population slaughtered or enslaved, Josephus uniquely narrates the sequence of events from the Roman assault to the demolition, emphasizing Titus's (unsuccessful) attempt to spare the temple. Tacitus provides a more detached, strategic overview and the religious milieu, and Dio adds the precise day of the destruction and the tribute imposed.

results.question: How do the sources portray the reign and death of Herod the Great?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus gives the fullest portrait. In the Antiquities he records the grand works of the reign — in his eighteenth year Herod "undertook a very great work, that is, to build of himself the temple of God" — alongside his implacable cruelty, and then the loathsome final illness in which "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," read as divine punishment. The parallel narrative in the Wars closes the reign with the bare notice that, having survived the killing of his son by only five days, Herod died, "having reigned thirty-four years."
results.answer:
The sources, primarily Josephus in both the *Antiquities of the Jews* and *The Wars of the Jews*, portray Herod the Great's reign as marked by immense cruelty, family strife, and spectacular building projects, while his death is depicted as a gruesome, divinely-punished end, capped by a final act of calculated barbarity.

**His Reign:** Josephus describes Herod as "a man of great barbarity towards all men equally, and a slave to his passion; but above the consideration of what was right" [4]. His reign was plagued by familial murders — he executed his wife Mariamne, her brother Aristobulus, her mother Alexandra, and several of his own sons, driven by the machinations of his sister Salome and his son Antipater [11]. Yet Josephus also notes that Herod was "favored by fortune as much as any man ever was, for from a private man he became a king" [4], and that he survived extraordinary dangers — for instance, when the upper part of a house collapsed while he was inside, killing no one, "all the people believed that Herod was beloved of God" [10]. His reign also saw severe calamities: a great famine in the thirteenth year brought "perpetual droughts" and a "pestilential disease," and even the king himself was not free from the distress [2].

**His Final Illness and Death:** Both Josephus works give a graphic, nearly identical account of Herod's final disease. In the *Antiquities*, his "entrails were also ex-ulcerated," "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," he had "a difficulty of breathing" with a "loathsome" stench, and convulsions throughout his body [9]. The *Wars* adds that "the diviners said those diseases were a punishment upon him for what he had done to the Rabbins" [12]. Despite his suffering, Herod clung to life: he went to the hot baths at Callirrhoe, and when physicians bathed him in a vessel of warm oil, his eyes failed and he seemed to be dying, but revived at the sound of his servants' lamentation [12][9]. In his final days, he grew "so choleric, that it brought him to do all things like a madman" [9].

**His Final Cruelty:** Knowing the Jews would rejoice at his death, Herod ordered the most illustrious men of the nation to be shut up in the hippodrome at Jericho and commanded that soldiers should slay them all immediately upon his death, so that "all Judea, and every family of them, will weep at it, whether they will or no" [8][7]. He also attempted suicide with a knife while paring an apple, but was stopped by his cousin Achiabus [6]. He died five days after having his son Antipater executed, having reigned thirty-four years since he procured Antigonus to be slain, or thirty-seven since being declared king by the Romans [4].

**Strabo's Geography** adds a brief summary, noting that the chief promoters of Herod's crimes were his sister Salome and his son Antipater, and that he put to death Mariamne, Aristobulus, Alexandra, and his sons Aristobulus and Alexander [11].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Mostly grounded, but it falsely claims Herod was inside the house when it collapsed, whereas source [10] explicitly states the upper part fell 'when nobody was in it' — a minor invented detail. | completeness: The answer thoroughly addresses both parts of the question—how the sources portray Herod's reign (cruelty, family strife, fortune, and calamities) and his death (gruesome illness, divine punishment, final barbarity, and timing)—without omitting any component the question explicitly requires. | attribution: The sources agree in their portrayal of Herod's reign and death, and the answer accurately attributes the specific details to Josephus's works and Strabo without any misattributions.

results.question: How do the sources describe Vespasian's rise to the throne during the civil war?
results.ideal_answer: The sources agree that Vespasian was raised to the throne by the armies of the East while the Jewish war was still in progress. Josephus, present in his camp, says he "desire[d] to obtain that government, in order to corroborate his attempts upon the whole empire," and was proclaimed by the soldiers in Judaea and Egypt. Tacitus reports the news reaching the East — "messengers from Syria and Judaea brought the news that the provinces of the East had sworn allegiance to him." Suetonius records the acclamation against Vitellius: the troops in Moesia and Pannonia revolted, "as did likewise, of the armies beyond sea, those in Judaea and Syria," who swore allegiance to Vespasian as emperor.
results.answer:
The sources describe Vespasian's rise as a blend of spontaneous military acclamation, careful political maneuvering, and divine portents. Tacitus recounts that as Vespasian left his quarters, "a few soldiers … suddenly saluted him as Emperor" and the troops shouted "Caesar and Augustus" (Histories) [1]; Licinius Mucianus immediately "administered … the oath of allegiance" to the soldiers and later addressed the Antiochenes, citing a rumour that Vitellius intended to transfer the Syrian legions to Germany [1]. Suetonius adds that the scheme first arose among Moesian legions who, fearing punishment for earlier plundering, resolved to create an emperor and "extolled Vespasian in high terms"; the attempt was temporarily suppressed, but on the Kalends of July the governor of Egypt, Tiberius Alexander, "first obliged the legions under his command to swear obedience to Vespasian", and on 28 July the army in Judaea "also swore allegiance to him" (Life of Vespasian) [2]. Suetonius also mentions a (possibly forged) letter of Otho urging vengeance and a report that Vitellius planned to swap the German legions' quarters for warmer stations [2]; Mucianus, putting aside his jealousy, promised the Syrian army, while the Parthian king "offered … a reinforcement of forty thousand archers" [7].

Josephus provides the most detailed eastern narrative. After news of Vitellius' excesses reached Vespasian at Caesarea, he was "much indignation" but restrained by the winter season [9]. At the exhortation of Mucianus and the other commanders, Vespasian focused on gaining Egypt, "knowing that Egypt was of the greatest consequence, in order to obtain the entire government, because of its supplying of corn" [6]. He sent to Tiberius Alexander, who "readily obliged the legions and the multitude to take the oath of fidelity" [3]; thereafter the legions in Moesia and Pannonia also took the oath, and Mucianus arrived to announce the people's enthusiasm [3]. Vespasian then sent Mucianus to Italy with a substantial army and himself went to Egypt [5]; after his rule was confirmed, he left for Rome and dispatched Titus to finish the Jewish war [10].

Cassius Dio notes the role of portents: a comet, two eclipses, two suns, and huge footprints on the Capitol, as well as dreams and omens that had long pointed to Vespasian [8]. While Vespasian hesitated, his soldiers "surrounded his tent and hailed him as emperor"; Mucianus urged him on, and Vespasian likewise sent Mucianus to Italy while he went to Egypt for money and grain [8].

Across all accounts, the pivotal factors are the spontaneous acclamation by the troops, the crucial support of Mucianus and the eastern legions, the control of Egypt, and the favourable omens that legitimised his bid.

results.question: How do the sources narrate the year of the four emperors that followed Nero?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus gives the connected narrative, opening his Histories "with the time when Servius Galba was consul for the second time with Titus Vinius for his colleague" — the start of the convulsion that ran Galba to Otho to Vitellius to Vespasian. Plutarch's surviving lives carry the same rapid turnover: the senate, swearing in Otho's name the oath it had just sworn and broken to Galba, "conferred on him the titles of Caesar and Augustus." Suetonius' per-emperor lives record each fall in turn — Galba "perished in the seventy-third year of his age, and the seventh month of his reign," cut down in the Forum.
results.answer:
The sources narrate the Year of the Four Emperors with different purposes, levels of detail, and moral emphases.

**Tacitus** (the *Histories*) offers the most dramatic and psychologically penetrating account. His preface famously declares: "I am entering on the history of a period rich in disasters, frightful in its wars, torn by civil strife … Four emperors perished by the sword; there were three civil wars" [c20263]. His narrative dwells on the horror of civil conflict—the sack of Cremona, for instance, is described with graphic intensity: the storming of the camp, the massacre, and the town being set alight while soldiers joked that the bath-water "would soon be warm enough" [22][21][20]. Tacitus also tracks the shifting loyalties of legions and the psychology of commanders such as Antonius Primus [23][26].

**Suetonius** (*The Twelve Caesars*) focuses on the emperors' private lives and character flaws. Otho is depicted as a riotous youth who "run about in the night-time, and seize upon any one he met … and toss him in a blanket" [c5021]; Vitellius's nativity was cast at birth and foretold that he would "be dragged to execution by the hands of the public executioner" [c5025]; his rule is marked by gluttony and coarseness. Galba's noble ancestry is noted but also his stinginess [4]. Suetonius thus explains the political chaos through personal vice and omens.

**Plutarch** (in his *Lives* of Galba and Otho) writes a moralising biography that stresses character, political misjudgement, and divine signs. He details how Galba, upon learning that Vitellius had been proclaimed emperor in Germany, suddenly adopted Piso as his successor, bypassing Otho; this caused Otho's resentment and the ominous thunder and lightning that accompanied the adoption ceremony [3]. Later, omens such as the Tiber's flooding and the statue of Caius Caesar turning from west to east accompany Vespasian's rise [11].

**Cassius Dio** (Book 64–65 of his *Roman History*) provides a concise, annalistic narrative that moves chronologically through each emperor's reign. He lists the consuls of 69 [5], summarises the main events (Galba's adoption of Piso, Otho's usurpation, the battle of Cremona, Otho's suicide, Vitellius's excesses and death) [9][6], and inserts moral observations, such as the fickleness of the Roman populace who praised Otho one day and cursed him the next [5].

**Josephus** (*The Jewish War*) covers the year only insofar as it impinges on Vespasian's Jewish campaign. He explicitly declines to give a full account because the events "are well known by all, and they are described by a great number of Greek and Roman authors" [2]. He summarises the sequence—Nero's death, Galba's murder, Otho's rise and fall, Vitellius's troubles, the Capitol fight, and the victory of Antonius Primus and Mucianus—and then concentrates on how Vespasian postponed the Jerusalem expedition while waiting to see where the empire would settle [2][15]. The final downfall of Vitellius is described briefly: he was "drawn along through the multitude, and abused with all sorts of torments, had his head cut off in the midst of Rome" after reigning eight months and five days [7].

**William Smith** (*A Smaller History of Rome*) distills the events into a modern scholarly summary, providing dates and a clear chain of causation: Galba murdered in the Forum in January 69, Otho's defeat at Bedriacum and suicide, Vitellius's gluttony, Vespasian's proclamation by the governor of Egypt, and the final battle in Rome where the Capitoline Temple was burned and Vitellius killed on December 20 [1].

In sum, the ancient sources vary from full-dress historical narrative (Tacitus), to biographical anecdote (Suetonius, Plutarch), to annalistic summary (Dio), to a peripheral mention driven by an external narrative (Josephus), while modern scholarship (Smith) synthesises them into a compact outline.

results.question: How do the sources assess the character and generalship of Hannibal?
results.ideal_answer: The sources agree on his extraordinary gifts while dividing on his vices. Livy's introductory sketch grants him a genius equally fit to command and obey, fearless and tireless, "by far the first of the cavalry and infantry" — but balances it with "inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy." Polybius marvels at the generalship that held a polyglot army together "for sixteen continuous years" in Italy. Appian preserves the famous conversation in which Scipio asked Hannibal whom he thought the greatest general, ranking himself only behind Alexander and Pyrrhus. Mommsen, the modern judge, notes that "the Romans charged him with cruelty, the Carthaginians with covetousness," yet finds the noble image of the commander unmarred.
results.answer: The ancient sources offer sharply divergent assessments of Hannibal's character and generalship. Livy presents a Janus-faced figure: Hannibal possessed extraordinary courage, prudence, and endurance, yet these “high virtues” were balanced by “inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy, no truth, no reverence for things sacred, no fear of the gods” [3]. Polybius acknowledges that some regarded him as “extraordinarily cruel” or “exceedingly grasping of money,” but cautions against simplistic judgments, arguing that circumstances may force men to act contrary to their real principles [2]; meanwhile, Polybius praises Hannibal’s “consummate prudence and strategical ability” in exploiting the character of Flaminius [5]. Cassius Dio’s appraisal is almost uniformly laudatory: he emphasizes Hannibal’s quick comprehension, careful planning, endurance, and skill in both immediate action and long-term foresight, with no hint of vice [4]. By contrast, Appian enumerates a litany of atrocities—forcing captives to fight each other, using bodies as bridges, and mass slaughter—attributing them to the “practice of cruelty” [6]. Diodorus Siculus likewise records barbaric acts such as compelling soldiers to duel their own relatives, while also noting Hannibal’s “warlike spirit” and long military experience [7]. Mommsen, a modern historian, observes that Romans charged him with cruelty, Carthaginians with covetousness, but concludes that these accusations “have not been able to mar the pure and noble image which it presents” and highlights his “inventive craftiness,” “discretion and enthusiasm, caution and energy” [1]. Thus, the sources range from outright condemnation (Appian, Diodorus, and Livy’s vice list) to more balanced or even admiring assessments (Polybius, Cassius Dio, Mommsen), with his strategic brilliance universally acknowledged.

results.question: How do the sources describe the final destruction of Carthage?
results.ideal_answer: Appian gives the fullest narrative of 146 BC: as the city of 700 years came to its end, Scipio "is said to have shed tears and publicly lamented the fortune of the enemy," reflecting on the mutability that would one day overtake Rome itself. Polybius, his tutor and an eyewitness, records the same reflection on the fall of "the once mighty empires of the Assyrians, Medes, Persians" and Macedon, with Scipio quoting Homer on the doom of Troy. The epitome of Livy registers the bare outcome — Scipio "at length took the city by storm, in the seven hundredth year after its foundation."
results.answer: The sources describe the final destruction of Carthage in spring 146 BC as a prolonged, horrifying assault that moved street by street and ended in a vast conflagration. After Scipio Aemilianus's forces broke into the city, they fought for six days through the three narrow streets leading to the Byrsa citadel [9][11]. The huge six-story houses were taken one by one, with soldiers crossing on beams laid between roofs while fighting raged both above and in the streets below [9][11]. Appian stresses the chaos: the soldiers pulled down buildings in heaps, and as fire spread the crashing was awful; the streets were filled with corpses, and street-cleaners with axes and forks threw the dead and still-living into trenches, horses trampling faces and skulls [5]. When Scipio reached the Byrsa, he ordered the three streets to be set on fire, and the city burned fiercely [11]. Mommsen reports that the ruins burned for seventeen days, and later excavations uncovered a layer of ashes four to five feet deep [1]. The remaining population—30,000 men and 25,000 women—surrendered from the citadel and were granted bare life, though most were sold into slavery [8]. The Roman deserters (900 in number) and the Carthaginian general Hasdrubal had thrown themselves into the temple of Aesculapius. When the deserters set the temple on fire, Hasdrubal secretly surrendered to Scipio, but his wife, seeing him pleading at the victor's feet, reproached him and then cast her children and herself into the flames [8][2][10][6]. Appian records that as Scipio watched the city burn, he wept and, reflecting on the mutability of human affairs, recited the Homeric lines foreseeing the fall of Troy, telling Polybius that he feared a similar fate for Rome [3]. After the city was taken, the senate—after a debate—ordered that Carthage be razed to the ground, the site ploughed over, and a curse pronounced upon anyone who should rebuild there [1][7][4].

results.question: How do the sources recount Rome's long wars against Mithridates of Pontus?
results.ideal_answer: Appian's dedicated narrative traces the whole struggle to its end, when Pompey brought the king's dominions "and the adjoining territory as far as the river Euphrates, under the pretext and impetus of the Mithridatic war, under Roman sway." Plutarch follows the commanders: the people "unanimously voted [Lucullus] to the Mithridatic war," which Pompey later took over. Livy's epitome records the war's terrible opening — Mithridates seized Asia, chained the proconsul and the general, "and ordered all the Romans in Asia to be massacred on the same day."
results.answer:
The sources recount Rome's long wars against Mithridates of Pontus as a series of three major conflicts, spanning the first century BC, in which the ambitious king repeatedly challenged Roman power in the east but was eventually crushed. The principal narratives differ in scope and emphasis.

Appian’s *Foreign Wars* provides the most continuous and detailed account of the Mithridatic campaigns. He presents Mithridates as a relentless and capable opponent who, despite being "vanquished by Sulla, Lucullus, and Pompey," also "several times got the better of them" [1]. Appian describes the outbreak of the war after the massacre of Romans in Asia (the "Asiatic Vespers"), the peace negotiations in which Sulla demanded that Mithridates "give up Cappadocia" [4], and the final destruction of his kingdom by Pompey, who "brought ... the whole of his dominions, and the adjoining territory as far as the river Euphrates, under Roman sway" [5]. He also records Mithridates’ cruel end, perishing through a conspiracy he had pardoned [1].

Plutarch, by contrast, treats the Mithridatic Wars through the lives of the Roman generals who fought him. In the *Life of Sulla* he dramatizes the personal confrontation between Sulla and Mithridates, quoting Sulla’s brusque words to the king: "ought not the petitioner to speak first, and the conqueror to listen in silence?" [6]. The *Life of Lucullus* and *Life of Pompey* further recount the latter stages; for example, Plutarch notes that Pompey pursued Mithridates relentlessly, finding his camp empty after a forty-five-day siege and later overtaking him near the Euphrates [8].

William Smith’s *A Smaller History of Rome*, a later scholarly summary, condenses the entire sequence into a concise timeline: the First Mithridatic War (88-84 BC), sparked by Mithridates’ invasion of Asia and his defeat at Chaeronea and Orchomenus; the brief Second War, ended when "Mithridates defeats Murena"; and the Third or Great Mithridatic War (74-61 BC), which saw Lucullus and then Pompey finally destroy his power [2][3].

In sum, the ancient and secondary sources together portray the Mithridatic Wars as a protracted struggle that tested Rome’s best commanders and ended only when Pompey completed what Sulla and Lucullus had begun.

results.question: How do the sources treat the reforms and deaths of the Gracchi brothers?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch preserves the moral force of the cause in Tiberius Gracchus's speech on the landless soldiers of Italy, who though they fought for Rome had no "houses of their own, or hearths of their ancestors to defend." Appian narrates the legislative struggle, ending when a substitute tribune was chosen "and the agrarian law was enacted." Livy's epitome summarizes the reform itself — Gracchus's agrarian law that "no person should hold more than five hundred acres of the public lands" — and the violence its passage unleashed.
results.answer:
The sources in this corpus treat the reforms and deaths of the Gracchi brothers with a mix of narrative detail, moral reflection, and political analysis. The principal ancient accounts — Livy's Epitome, Plutarch's Lives, and Appian's Civil Wars — present the reforms as well-intentioned measures aimed at redressing social and economic distress, but also as politically explosive acts that provoked violent aristocratic backlash. Mommsen (the 19th-century historian) provides a more analytical, critical perspective, judging the reforms as the opening of the Roman revolution.

**Reforms of Tiberius Gracchus (133 BC)**

Livy's Epitome reports that Tiberius, as plebeian tribune, proposed an agrarian law limiting possession of public land to five hundred acres, and "deprived his colleague, Marcus Octavius, of his authority by a law which he made" when Octavius vetoed it [1]. Plutarch adds that Tiberius was moved by the depopulation of Italy, where "there being hardly any free husbandmen or shepherds, but for the most part only barbarian, imported slaves" [8]. Appian gives Tiberius's speech, in which he asked "whether it was not just to divide among the common people what belonged to them in common" and warned that Rome's future conquests depended on "having plenty of brave men" rather than a landless populace [10]. Mommsen sees the agrarian law as legally sound — "the state was confessedly the owner of the occupied land" — but politically reckless, calling Tiberius "a tolerably capable, thoroughly well-meaning, conservative patriot, who simply did not know what he was doing" [12][13].

Tiberius also proposed distributing the treasure of Attalus of Pergamum to the new landholders, and pressed for further reforms including shortening military service and extending the franchise to Italian allies [21]. William Smith notes the revolutionary character: the law "affected only Public Lands, but it was no less a revolutionary measure" because long possession had made the land seem like private property [11].

**Death of Tiberius Gracchus**

The ancient sources agree that Tiberius was killed while seeking re-election. Livy's Epitome states he was "slain in the Capitol, by the chief nobles, by the advice of Publius Cornelius Nasica, after having been beaten first with the fragments of the seats, and was thrown, without the rites of sepulture, into the river" [1]. Appian likewise has Nasica leading the senators, who "wrested clubs out of the hands of the Gracchans themselves, or with fragments of broken benches" beat them; Tiberius "was slain at the door close by the statues of the kings" and "all the bodies were thrown by night into the Tiber" [32]. Plutarch emphasizes the posthumous cruelty: "they would not suffer his own brother, though he earnestly begged the favor, to bury him in the night, but threw him, together with the other corpses, into the river" [5]. Mommsen calls it an "infamous butchery" that "condemns itself, as it condemns the aristocratic faction whence it issued" but adds that "the glory of martyrdom, with which it has embellished the name of Tiberius Gracchus, came in this instance, as usually, to the wrong man" [13]. After Tiberius's death, the Senate did not oppose the land division, and Publius Crassus was elected as a replacement commissioner [5].

**Reforms of Gaius Gracchus (123–122 BC)**

Gaius's reforms were far more sweeping. Plutarch lists them: distribution of public lands to the poor, clothing soldiers at public charge, extending the franchise to all Italians, selling corn to the poor at a lower rate, and reforming the courts by adding three hundred equestrians to the senatorial jury pool [16]. William Smith enumerates the principal laws: extension of the agrarian law via new colonies in Italy and the provinces, a state corn provision (the first of the Leges Frumentariae) selling corn below market value, and equipping soldiers at state expense [15]. Mommsen characterises Gaius's programme as "nothing else than an entirely new constitution" with the corn distribution designed to attach the urban proletariat to its leader, the judicial reform transferring power from senators to equites, and the colony programme (including one at Carthage) extending Roman settlement overseas [17][19]. W. Warde Fowler notes that Gaius's corn law organised supply, transport, warehousing, and finance, selling corn at "six and one-third asses, or rather less than half the normal market-price" [22].

**Death of Gaius Gracchus**

Gaius's downfall came in 121 BC. William Smith records that while Gracchus was absent founding the colony at Carthage, his opponents weakened his popularity; on his return both he and Flaccus failed re-election, and the consul Lucius Opimius resolved to crush them [26]. An accident — a servant of Opimius was stabbed after insulting Gracchus — gave the Senate a pretext. Opimius was "invested with extraordinary power to protect the commonwealth and suppress all tyrants" [24]. Flaccus and Gracchus seized the Aventine; Opimius attacked. Appian reports that Gracchus "fled across the river by the Sublician bridge, with one slave, to a grove" where the slave killed him [31]. Plutarch says the slave "first killed his master and then himself" [27] and that his head was cut off: "Septimuleius, therefore, having fixed Caius's head upon the top of his spear, came and presented it to Opimius. They presently brought the scales, and it was found to weigh above seventeen pounds" — the reward having been its weight in gold [28]. Plutarch adds that "the bodies of these two persons, as well as of the rest who were slain, to the number of three thousand men, were all thrown into the river; their goods were confiscated, and their widows forbidden to put themselves into mourning" [28]. Opimius then built a temple of Concord, prompting the graffito: "Folly and Discord Concord's temple built" [25]. Mommsen judges that "the aristocracy conceived the plan of offering battle to the author of the corn-largesses and land-assignations on his own ground" through the rival tribune Drusus, and that Opimius's armed suppression was the culmination of a deliberate strategy to destroy Gaius [20].

In sum, the ancient sources portray the Gracchi as reformers driven by genuine concern for the Roman poor, but also as men whose methods — deposing a tribune, seeking re-election, arming followers — broke constitutional norms and provoked a violent senatorial reaction that ended in their brutal deaths. Mommsen adds a layer of structural analysis, seeing the Gracchi as the unwitting inaugurators of a century of revolution.

results.question: How do the sources describe the war against Jugurtha of Numidia?
results.ideal_answer: The sources frame the war as much by Roman corruption as by arms. Appian preserves Jugurtha's famous verdict on the venal city — that "the whole city of Rome could be bought if a purchaser could be found for it." Plutarch follows the endgame through the Numidian kings: after Jugurtha fled and "in his distress came to [Bocchus] as his last hope," he was received as a suppliant and then betrayed. Livy's epitome records the capture plainly — Bocchus, beaten and unwilling to fight on, "delivered up Jugurtha in chains to Marius."
results.answer:
The sources in the corpus describe the war against Jugurtha of Numidia from several distinct perspectives, ranging from brief annalistic summaries to detailed narrative and scholarly analysis.

**Livy's Epitomes** (primary, fragmentary/periochae) provide a terse timeline of events. Book LXIV records that Jugurtha attacked Adherbal, besieged him in Cirta, and put him to death contrary to the Senate's commands, leading to the declaration of war; Consul Calpurnius Bestia made peace with Jugurtha without authority; Jugurtha came to Rome under a safe-conduct and is supposed to have bribed many senators, and on leaving is reported to have said, "O venal city! doomed to quick perdition, could but a purchaser be found!" [8]. Book LXV notes Metellus defeated Jugurtha in two battles and ravaged Numidia, while Book LXVI recounts that Marius drove Jugurtha out of Numidia, and that Bocchus, king of the Moors, delivered him up in chains; in this Sulla distinguished himself [3]. The epitomes thus focus on the sequence of commanders and the capture.

**Plutarch** (primary, *Life of Marius*) concentrates on the final stage of the war and its political fallout. He describes how Bocchus, after pretending to support Jugurtha, privately determined to betray him and sent for Sulla. After hesitation, Bocchus "fixed upon his former treachery, and put Jugurtha alive into Sylla's possession" [9]. Plutarch emphasizes that this betrayal sparked the bitter rivalry between Marius and Sulla: many attributed the success to Sulla, who had a seal engraved showing Bocchus betraying Jugurtha to him, which irritated the hot and jealous temper of Marius [9]. The account thus serves to explain the origins of the later civil conflict, more than to narrate the war itself.

**Appian** (primary, *The Foreign Wars*) supplies details not found in other sources. He records that Jugurtha, when summoned to trial, uttered the famous saying that "the whole city of Rome could be bought if a purchaser could be found for it" [16]. Appian describes Metellus' harsh discipline: he executed Turpilius, a Roman prefect, for suspicion of collusion, and burnt alive Thracian and Ligurian deserters [16]. He also recounts the negotiations: when Marius sent Manlius and Sulla to Bocchus, Manlius told the Moorish king that the Romans had taken the contested territory from Syphax by the law of war and given it to Masinissa, and that an enemy like Jugurtha could not claim a gift made to a friend [16]. The final betrayal is narrated succinctly: Bocchus, under pretence of a conference, deceived Jugurtha's friend Apsar and had Sulla come to his camp, leading to Jugurtha's seizure [15].

**William Smith** (secondary, *A Smaller History of Rome*) provides the fullest narrative, covering the origins, the corruption of Roman commanders, the campaigns of Metellus and Marius, and Jugurtha's capture and death. Smith traces the dynastic conflict among Micipsa's heirs, Jugurtha's assassination of Hiempsal and execution of Adherbal, and the repeated bribery of Roman officials [1][6][10][7]. He details the military operations: Metellus' siege of Thala, Marius' reduction of the remaining strongholds, and the climactic desert ambush in which Marius repelled the united forces of Jugurtha and Bocchus [5]. Smith notes that Sulla's later claim of credit for the betrayal was symbolized by a signet ring depicting the surrender [11], and that Marius led Jugurtha in triumph in January 104 BC, after which the king was thrown into a dungeon and starved to death [11].

**Theodor Mommsen** (secondary, *The History of Rome*) offers a critical, interpretative account. He stresses that Jugurtha, after defeating Albinus, was "regarded as the deliverer of Libya from the rule of the hated foreigners" and that Metellus was determined not to end the war without the king's death, even instigating assassination attempts [12]. Mommsen provides a detailed description of the battle on the Muthul, where Jugurtha's tactical positions forced the Romans into a difficult advance [12]. He portrays Bocchus as playing a duplicitous game, and the negotiations as a tense standoff, with Sulla undertaking a "perilous task" that ultimately secured Jugurtha's betrayal and surrender [13][2]. Mommsen evaluates the credit for victory: Metellus conquered Numidia up to the desert, Sulla captured the king, and Marius "played a part somewhat compromising the dignity of an ambitious upstart" [2]. He also records Jugurtha's death in the Tullianum, calling it the "bath of ice" as the African crossed the threshold [2].

In summary, the sources collectively depict the Jugurthine War as a conflict characterized by Roman corruption and military incompetence in its early phase, redeemed by the determined campaigns of Metellus and Marius, and concluded through the betrayal of Bocchus. While the epitomes and Smith offer straightforward narratives, Plutarch and Mommsen analyze the political rivalries that the war engendered, and Appian adds distinctive anecdotal details about bribery and the treatment of deserters.

results.question: How do the sources tell the rise and fall of Theban supremacy under Epaminondas?
results.ideal_answer: The sources trace Theban hegemony as the personal achievement of Epaminondas — and its collapse with his death. Xenophon, contemporary and cool toward Thebes, ends his history on the inconclusive note that after Mantinea "the effective result of these achievements was the very opposite of that which the world at large anticipated." Diodorus makes the dependence explicit: while Epaminondas lived "he gained the sovereign power for his country; but, by his death they lost it again" — "and this was the end of Epaminondas, a man honoured and esteemed of all." Pausanias records his monument, whose elegiac lines tell "how he restored Messene, and how the Greeks got their freedom through him." Plutarch supplies the Theban view of the bond with Pelopidas, for whom Epaminondas "singly fought a multitude, resolving rather to die than forsake his helpless" friend.
results.answer:
The sources present the rise and fall of Theban supremacy under Epaminondas as a dramatic arc beginning with the liberation of Thebes in 379 B.C., peaking with the victory at Leuctra in 371 B.C., and collapsing abruptly with his death at Mantinea in 362 B.C. — a trajectory summarised by George Grote: "with him the dignity and commanding influence of Thebes both began and ended" [2].

**The Rise: Liberation and Leuctra**

Grote traces the origins of Theban resurgence to the revolution of 379 B.C., which expelled the Spartan garrison from the Cadmeia and forced Epaminondas, previously a philosopher, into public life by "the strongest obligations both of duty and interest" [13]. Military training — including the creation of the Sacred Band — and steady campaigning against pro-Spartan oligarchies in Boeotia built Theban strength [10].

The decisive turning point was the battle of Leuctra (371 B.C.). Diodorus Siculus reports that Epaminondas, "chiefly by his valour and prudence, he had routed those commanders of Greece, who were never before conquered," inflicting four thousand Spartan casualties against only three hundred Boeotian dead [5]. Grote describes the psychological shock: "All the prestige and old associations connected with her long-established power vanished" [4]. J. B. Bury notes that after Leuctra "the parts hitherto played by the two states are reversed; Thebes now becomes the invader of the Peloponnese, as Sparta before had been the invader of Boeotia" [3].

**The Zenith: Hegemony and Invasions**

In the aftermath, Epaminondas led a victorious invasion of Laconia, ravaging Spartan territory, liberating Messene, and founding Megalopolis as an anti-Spartan bulwark [9]. Grote emphasises that this amounted to "a partial reconstitution of the peninsula"; the western half of Laconia was "wrested from them" and the Arcadians were "emancipated from their dependence" [9]. Theban influence extended into Thessaly, Phocis, and Euboea [6]. Xenophon's *Hellenica* — though notably hostile to Thebes — records Epaminondas's Achaean campaign (367 B.C.), where he personally insisted on sparing the aristocrats and securing pledges of fealty, a policy later reversed by the home government [18].

**The Fall: Mantinea**

The Theban ascendancy crumbled at Mantinea in 362 B.C. Grote summarises Xenophon's description of the battle: Epaminondas led a night-march against Sparta, was thwarted, then engaged the enemy near Mantinea; his tactical dispositions — a massive left-wing column — proved "complete success" and the Thebans were victorious [8]. But Epaminondas was mortally wounded. Bury writes: "As he pursued the retreating foe, at the head of his Thebans, Epaminondas received a mortal thrust from a spear. When the news spread through the field, the pursuit was stayed and the effect of the victory was undone; the troops fell back like beaten men" [15]. Diodorus confirms: "if this man had lived some time longer, he had gained for the Thebans the sovereign command both at sea and land. But not long after, being killed at the battle at Mantinea ... all the prosperity of the Thebans presently died (as it were) with him" [12].

**Source Biases**

Grote explicitly flags the problem with the chief contemporary witness: "the philo-Laconian Xenophon finds neither misdeeds nor omissions to reveal in the capital enemy of Sparta — mentions him only to record what is honorable — and manifests the perverting bias mainly by suppressing or slurring over his triumphs" [2]. Bury adds a further critical note: while Epaminondas's military genius is undisputed, "his political faculty was mediocre" and he "failed to solve the fundamental problems which demanded solution at the hands of a statesman who aimed at making his country great" [15]. Thus the sources agree on the outline — meteoric rise, spectacular victories, and collapse upon the hero's death — but differ in their judgments, with Xenophon's hostile silence contrasting with the open admiration of later writers from Polybius and Cicero down to Grote himself.

results.question: How do the sources narrate the Athenian expedition to Sicily and its destruction?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides gives the canonical narrative of the catastrophe, ending with the survivors crammed into the Syracusan quarries, "each man during eight months having only half a pint of water and a pint of corn given him daily." Plutarch's life of the commander follows Nicias to his end, noting the dispute in the sources over how he died — that "Demosthenes and Nicias did not die, as Thucydides and Philistus have written, by the order of the Syracusans," but perhaps by their own hand.
results.answer:
The sources narrate the Athenian expedition to Sicily as a doomed enterprise from the start, culminating in total destruction. Plutarch records that the fleet departed under ill omens: the women celebrated the death of Adonis with images of dead men, and Socrates' daimonion presaged ruin [11]. Nicias, who had opposed the expedition, advocated a cautious display of force before returning home [11]. 

The arrival of Demosthenes with a splendid fleet—5,000 men‑at‑arms and 3,000 light‑armed—initially terrified the Syracusans [12], but the turning point came with the naval battles in the Great Harbour. Thucydides describes a fight in which the Syracusans first defeated the Athenian centre, then killed the general Eurymedon after he was pinned against the shore, and drove the whole fleet to land [3]. The Syracusans, now confident, determined to block the harbour mouth so the Athenians could not escape [2]. 

Plutarch details another sea‑fight where the Athenians, having lost Eurymedon, were compelled to retreat by land; the Syracusans immediately closed the harbour entrance [8]. Further naval engagements saw the Athenians suffer from the enemy’s tactics: using light nimble ships and stones, a stratagem taught by the Corinthian pilot Ariston, who fell in the battle [8]. 

An eclipse of the moon, initially interpreted by Nicias’ soothsayers as a reason to delay departure, proved disastrous [5]. When the Athenians finally attempted to break out, the Syracusans had barricaded the harbour with a chain‑linked line of vessels [5]. In the climactic battle, the Athenians fought with laden heavy ships and were pelted with stones [8]; they were utterly defeated, and the survivors were thrown into despair [1]. 

The retreat on land was a catastrophe. In a night action the Athenians became confused by the moon casting their own shadows, mistaking friends for foes, and were routed with great slaughter [7]. Nicias blamed Demosthenes, who urged an immediate retreat, but the army was forced to abandon the sick and wounded [7]. The retreating host split into two divisions. Demosthenes’ rear division was overtaken and surrendered; Nicias’ division, after terrible thirst, was captured at the river Asinarus [10]. Thus the whole armament—once the pride of Athens—was destroyed.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is largely grounded, but it misattributes the Athenians' compelled retreat by land to Plutarch's sea-fight in [8] rather than the later land retreat in [10], and cites [7] for abandoning the sick when that detail appears in [10]. | completeness: The answer comprehensively covers how both Thucydides and Plutarch narrate the expedition and its destruction, from the ill-omened departure and harbour battles through the final retreat, surrender, and annihilation, without omitting any part the question explicitly requests. | attribution: The sources agree and provide complementary details about the expedition, and the answer correctly attributes the distinct contributions to their respective authors in prose without any misattributions.

results.question: How do the sources describe the Spartan system of government attributed to Lycurgus?
results.ideal_answer: The sources credit Lycurgus with a mixed, balanced constitution. Plutarch singles out the central institution: "the first and of greatest importance was the establishment of the senate," a council of twenty-eight elders whose power, equal to the kings', acted "like ballast in a ship" between monarchy and democracy. Herodotus gives the early Greek account of the same transformation — once Lycurgus had reformed their institutions "the Spartans had good laws," and after his death "they erected a temple" to him and paid him great worship.
results.answer: The sources describe the Spartan system of government attributed to Lycurgus as a carefully balanced mixed constitution designed to prevent any one element from becoming dominant. Plutarch, in his *Life of Lycurgus*, provides the most detailed account. He reports that Lycurgus's "first and of greatest importance was the establishment of the senate" (the Gerusia), consisting of twenty-eight elders plus the two kings, making thirty in total [1][3]. This senate had "a power equal to the kings' in matters of great consequence" and acted as a stabilising force: "the twenty-eight always adhering to the kings so far as to resist democracy, and, on the other hand, supporting the people against the establishment of absolute monarchy" [1][3]. Plutarch likens it to "ballast in a ship, which always kept things in a just equilibrium" [1]. The popular assembly met in the open air between Babyca and Cnacion; the people could only ratify or reject proposals put before them by the king or senate, not debate them [3][2]. Later, the ephorate was added — "a bit in its mouth," as Plato put it — to check the oligarchical element, established about 130 years after Lycurgus [2]. Polybius, in his *Histories*, emphasises the same principle of checks and balances. He writes that Lycurgus "combined together all the excellences and distinctive features of the best constitutions, that no part should become unduly predominant" [8]. The royal power was checked by fear of the people, the people were restrained by fear of the Gerusia, and the Gerusia, being selected on merit, always supported justice, so that "the party placed at a disadvantage by its conservative tendency was always strengthened and supported by the weight and influence of the Gerusia" [8]. Polybius credits this constitution with enabling the Lacedaemonians to retain their freedom "for the longest period of any people with which we are acquainted" [8]. He also notes that Lycurgus was "the first to construct a constitution — that of Sparta — on this principle" of mixing kingship, aristocracy, and democracy [9].

results.question: What do the sources report about the oracle at Delphi — its setting, procedure, and authority?
results.ideal_answer: The sources present Delphi as the most authoritative and richest of Greek shrines. Strabo records its divine charter — that "Apollo, by the aid of Themis, established this oracle with a view to benefit the human race." Pausanias preserves its ancient history, that "in the most ancient times it was the oracle of Earth" before it passed to Apollo. Diodorus shows the other side of its fame, the immense treasure that made it a target: in the Sacred War the Phocian leader's "brother and successor Onomarchus converted much of those treasures" to pay his troops, the sacrilege that defined the conflict.
results.answer:
The ancient sources provide a detailed picture of the Delphic oracle’s setting, procedure, and authority.

**Setting**  
Diodorus Siculus reports that the oracle was discovered when goats grazing near “an opening or gulph in the earth in that place, now called the Adytum of the Temple” began to “leap and dance in a wonderful manner” and make unusual noises [3]. A shepherd who investigated was likewise “inspired with a spirit of prophecy.” The vapour rising from the chasm was the source of inspiration, and to prevent people from leaping in, a “machine” — the tripod — was constructed so that a woman could sit over the chasm “without any danger” and deliver answers [3]. Pausanias adds that some accounts attribute the first oracles to Earth, then to Themis, and finally to Apollo, and he also mentions “shepherds meeting with the oracle, and becoming inspired by the vapour, and prophesying through Apollo” [4].

**Procedure**  
Diodorus describes how the Pythian prophetess sat on the tripod to prophesy [3][1]. Goats were “most commonly sacrificed by the Delphians when they come to consult the oracle” [3]. Originally young virgins served, but after one was raped by a visitor (Echecrates), the Delphians decreed that “no young virgin for the future, but a grave woman of fifty years of age (in a virgin's dress, to keep up the memory of the ancient mode in divination) should preside, and return the answers” [1]. The Pythia’s utterances were delivered in hexameter verse [6]. Herodotus details a consultation: Croesus sent envoys to Delphi; they “entered the sanctuary of the temple to consult the god” and the Pythia spoke her answer in hexameter, which was then “caused to be written down” and brought back [8]. Pausanias also notes that the first priestess, Phemonoe, was the first “who recited the oracles in hexameters” [4].

**Authority**  
Herodotus demonstrates the oracle’s authority through Croesus’s famous test: he sent to multiple oracles asking what he was doing on a specific day; only the Delphic Pythia correctly answered in hexameter verse, identifying the “smell of a strong-shelled tortoise boiling in caldron of bronze, and the flesh of a lamb mingled with it” [8]. Croesus then judged that “the Oracle at Delphi was the only true one” and sent lavish offerings of gold — half-plinths, a golden lion, bowls, and other treasures [7][10]. In return the Delphians granted Croesus and the Lydians “precedence in consulting the Oracle and freedom from all payments, and the right to front seats at the games” [9]. Grote sums up the wider authority: Delphi was “universally venerated” from the earliest times, its mandate guiding “the earliest Grecian colonies” and answering questions from “Lydians, Phrygians, Etruscans, Romans” — so much so that “the question was put and the answer treasured up” with “complete faith” [6].

results.question: How do the sources explain the fall of Nineveh and the end of the Assyrian empire?
results.ideal_answer: The modern reconstruction and the ancient notice agree that Nineveh fell to a coalition led by the Medes. Rawlinson's Assyria judges that the empire "fell, not so much from any inherent weakness, or from the effect of gradual decay, as by an unfortunate combination of circumstances" — a barbarian inroad coinciding with the rise of a warlike neighbour. His Media traces that neighbour's role: after the siege "the fall of the city was followed by a division of the spoil between the two principal conquerors," Cyaxares the Mede taking Assyria proper. Herodotus gives the early Greek account — the Medes "had rule over the same nations as before; and they also took Nineveh" and made the Assyrians subject, except Babylon.
results.answer: The sources offer several explanations for the fall of Nineveh and the end of the Assyrian empire, combining divine, military, natural, and political factors. The prophet Nahum, as reported by Josephus, foretold that Nineveh would become "a pool of water in motion" and that its people would flee, a prophecy fulfilled 115 years later [2]. Similarly, the biblical Jonah warned "Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown" at a time when the empire had already declined and enemies were on the offensive [3]. In terms of military agency, the ancient historians differ: Herodotus attributed the conquest to the Median king Cyaxares alone, while Ctesias and others held that the Medes and Babylonians together destroyed the city [1]. Ctesias’s account, preserved by Rawlinson, adds that a heavy rainy season swelled the Tigris, bursting into the city and sweeping away more than two miles of the wall, which could not be repaired; seeing resistance hopeless, the Assyrian king (Sardanapalus/Saracus) burned himself with his wealth in his palace [5]. Rawlinson further links this flood to Nahum’s words: "The gates of the rivers are opened, and the palace is dissolved" [4]. Long‑term causes are also cited: a devastating Scythian invasion that ravaged and depopulated Assyria, leaving it weak and exhausted, so that it fell easily to a permanent conqueror [6]. Finally, political betrayal played a role: the Assyrian king Saracus dispatched his general Nabopolassar to confront the Babylonian enemy, but Nabopolassar instead allied with the Median king Cyaxares, married his daughter to his son Nebuchadnezzar, and joined the Medes in besieging Nineveh, leading to Saracus’s suicide by fire [7].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, including the correct gloss identifying the unnamed prophet as the biblical Jonah, with no invented facts or unsupported details. | completeness: The answer comprehensively covers how the sources explain the fall of Nineveh, addressing divine prophecy, military agency, natural disaster, long-term decline, and political betrayal without omitting any explicit requirement of the question. | attribution: The answer correctly synthesizes multiple sources, explicitly surfacing and attributing the disagreement between Herodotus and Ctesias in prose, while accurately crediting the other distinct contributions to their respective authors.

results.question: What do the sources say about the walls, size, and wonders of Babylon?
results.ideal_answer: The sources concur on Babylon's vast scale. Rawlinson's modern survey calls it "probably the largest and most magnificent city of the ancient world." Herodotus describes its plan — "there are two divisions of the city; for a river whose name is Euphrates parts it in the middle" — within its enormous square of wall. Strabo gives the measurements: the wall was 385 "stadia in circumference, and 32 feet in thickness," with towers rising to 60 cubits — the defenses that, with the hanging garden, made the city one of the wonders of the world.
results.answer:
The ancient sources present Babylon as a city of extraordinary magnitude and splendor, though they disagree on its exact dimensions. Herodotus, who claims to have seen the city himself, describes its ground plan as a square, "each face measures one hundred and twenty furlongs," giving a circuit of 480 furlongs (about 14 miles each side, 56 miles total) [3]. He reports the outer wall as "fifty royal cubits in thickness and two hundred cubits in height" and notes a moat, a hundred bronze gates, and chambers atop the wall with space for a four-horse chariot to turn [3][8]. Inside, houses of three and four stories lined straight streets, and a second inner wall enclosed a smaller area [8]. Strabo gives a smaller circuit of 385 stadia, a wall thickness of 32 feet, and a height of 50 cubits between towers (60 cubits for the towers themselves); he adds that the roadway on the wall allowed "chariots with four horses when they meet to pass each other with ease" [1].

Later historians of Alexander, such as Clitarchus, Diodorus, and Q. Curtius, give circuits of 365–385 stadia [12]. Rawlinson summarizes the conflicting height estimates: Herodotus gives 200 royal cubits (about 335 English feet), Ctesias 50 fathoms (300 Greek feet), while Clitarchus and Strabo give only 75 feet [4]. Rawlinson concludes that the numbers were either rough guesses or intentional exaggerations by native guides, but that the walls were still of "vast height—seventy or eighty feet at least in the time of Alexander" [4]. Grote similarly defends Herodotus's higher numbers, arguing that the destruction by Darius was not complete and that Herodotus could have seen substantial remains [11][10][9].

Among the wonders, Strabo notes that "among the seven wonders of the world, are reckoned this wall and the hanging garden" [1]. He describes the hanging garden as a square of four plethra per side, consisting of vaulted terraces on cube-shaped pillars, filled with earth to grow large trees, with water raised from the Euphrates by engines [1]. Rawlinson adds that the garden was "erection to the whim of a woman," a square of 400 Greek feet per side, rising to at least 75 feet on tiers of open arches, watered by a screw, and lined with lead to prevent moisture damage [2]. He also confirms that the walls were considered one of the seven wonders [5]. Herodotus additionally describes the temple of Zeus Belos (Belus), an eight-tiered tower with a square base of two furlongs each way, topped with a large couch and golden table [8]. The tomb of Belus, a quadrangular pyramid of baked brick one stadium in height and one stadium each side, was destroyed by Xerxes [1]. Thus the sources collectively portray Babylon as a city of immense walls, vast enclosed area, and remarkable constructions—the hanging gardens, the temple-tower, and the walls themselves—all celebrated as wonders of the ancient world.

results.question: How do the sources describe Rome's wars against the Sassanid Persian empire?
results.ideal_answer: The sources mark the Sassanid dynasty as a renewed and formidable threat to Rome on the eastern frontier. Cassius Dio records its alarming birth — "Artaxerxes, a Persian, having conquered the Parthians in three battles and killed their king," then menaced the Roman provinces of Mesopotamia. Rawlinson's Sassanian volume traces the long struggle over the frontier fortresses, noting how Sapor "rightly conceived from the first the importance of Nisibis." Ammianus, the eyewitness of Julian's invasion, shows the Persians' method against the retreating Romans — having "learnt by their frequent defeats to shun pitched battles," they "laid secret ambuscades on our road."
results.answer:
The sources describe the Rome-Sassanid wars as a prolonged and exhausting struggle, marked by large-scale battles, frequent sieges, and shifting fortunes. According to Rawlinson, the conflict under Shapur II (Sapor) “languished” for years, with the Persians “constantly victorious in the open field” but hamstrung by an “inability to conduct sieges to a prosperous issue” [1]. The war consisted of numerous pitched battles in which Constantius was repeatedly defeated, yet the fortified towns of Mesopotamia—especially Nisibis—repeatedly held out [2][14]. Shapur II finally succeeded in capturing Nisibis after a twenty-seven-year contest, during which he “fought numerous pitched battles with the Romans, and was never once defeated” [4].

Ammianus Marcellinus provides vivid battle narratives, such as the Persian storming of a city through a neglected defensive tower, where seventy archers climbed in and “shot their arrows among the citizens with prodigious skill” [8]. He also records Julian’s Persian campaign, including the storming of Maogamalcha and the hard-fought victory before Ctesiphon, where the Romans claimed to have slain “two thousand five hundred” of the enemy for the loss of only seventy-five of their own men [10].

Gibbon, drawing on Ammianus and others, recounts Julian’s grand invasion of 363 AD with sixty-five thousand men [11]. After initial successes, Julian burned his fleet and advanced into the interior, where Sapor’s scorched-earth tactics left the Romans starving and harassed [15][16]. In the subsequent retreat, a fierce battle saw the Roman rear-guard broken by elephants, but the legionaries eventually repulsed the Persians [17]. Following Julian’s death, Jovian signed an “ignominious treaty” ceding territories to Persia, after which Sapor overran Armenia and Iberia [13][19].

Tactically, the sources emphasise the Persian reliance on archers behind large shields, heavy cavalry, and elephants [9][18], and note that by the later Sassanian period, Persian armies had overcome their earlier weakness in sieges, taking cities like Amida and Antioch with relative speed [5]. Overall, the sources portray the wars as a gruelling test of Roman endurance against a powerful and resilient eastern monarchy.
