results.question: How do the sources describe Rome's catastrophic defeat by the Parthians at Carrhae?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch's life of Crassus gives the fullest narrative of the disaster — the Romans, after the battle, had "a sad night of it," unable to bury their dead or tend their wounded, "everyone bewailed his own fate." Cassius Dio supplies the end at the parley: lured to a conference by Surena, Crassus "fell among the rest, whether he was slain by one of his own men to prevent his capture alive, or whether by the enemy." Rawlinson's Parthia explains the military cause from the victors' side — to attack a cavalry power "with a body of foot-soldiers, supported by an insignificant number of horse, must be at all times rash and dangerous."
results.answer:
The sources portray the Roman defeat at Carrhae as a disaster resulting from Parthian tactical innovation, treacherous guides, and the incompetence of Crassus. The battle was fought in the open plains of Mesopotamia, where the Parthians relied on mounted archers and heavy cavalry. Mommsen describes the Parthian system: the heavy cavalry formed a line while the “bow which was national in the east and was handled with masterly skill” allowed them to shower the densely packed legions with arrows from a distance [8]. The Romans, drawn up in a dense square, were outflanked and overwhelmed; the hope that the enemy would run out of arrows vanished at the sight of “the endless range of camels laden with arrows” [8]. Smith similarly notes that the Parthians “worried the densely-marshaled Romans with showers of arrows; and by feigned retreats, during which they continued to discharge their arrows, they led the Romans into disadvantageous positions” [7].

A critical moment was the death of Publius Crassus, the son of the commander. Mommsen recounts that Publius advanced with a select corps to prevent being surrounded, but his force was cut off and annihilated; Publius, wounded in the sword-arm, “caused himself to be stabbed by the hand of his shield-bearer” rather than be captured [5]. His head was later displayed before his father’s eyes, triggering a renewed and hopeless assault on the main army [5].

After nightfall, the Romans attempted a retreat, but it was chaotic. Plutarch says, “strange confusion and disorder, with an outcry and lamentation, seized the camp” as the disabled were left behind [1]. The retreat was further sabotaged by the guide Andromachus, whom Crassus had “infatuated as to choose him for his guide.” Andromachus, in the pay of the Parthians, “led him hither and thither, and at last conveyed him into the midst of morasses and places full of ditches” [3]. Cassius Dio confirms that Crassus “trusted him” while “distraught by his private misfortune and the public calamity” [10].

The final act came when the Parthian general Surena offered a truce and invited Crassus to a personal conference. The sources agree this was a trap. Plutarch writes that Surena “betook himself to his usual craft” and had prisoners set free to circulate false talk of peace [2]. At the meeting, a richly adorned horse was presented to Crassus; when the Parthians attempted to hurry him away, a scuffle broke out, and Crassus was killed—whether by his own men to prevent capture or by the Parthians is uncertain [6][9][10]. Cassius Dio reports that “the barbarians took him forcibly and threw him on his horse,” and in the ensuing fight Crassus fell [10]. Smith adds the famous detail that his head was taken to King Orodes, who “caused melted gold to be poured into the mouth, saying, 'Sate thyself now with that metal of which in life thou wert so greedy'” [7].

The losses were staggering. Mommsen states: “Of above 40,000 Roman legionaries, who had crossed the Euphrates, not a fourth part returned; the half had perished; nearly 10,000 Roman prisoners were settled by the victors in the extreme east of their kingdom—in the oasis of Merv” [6]. Smith gives similar figures: “Twenty thousand Roman troops were slain, and ten thousand taken prisoners” [7]. Rawlinson likewise says “one half of the whole number perished” and the prisoners were settled in Margiana [9]. Only a small remnant, including Cassius, escaped to Syria. Thus the sources unanimously present Carrhae as one of the most catastrophic defeats in Roman history, comparable to Cannae and Arausio [6].

results.question: What do the sources reveal about Roman dining, banquets, and table luxury?
results.ideal_answer: The sources show both the ordinary custom and its imperial excess. Fowler describes the shape of the Roman day around its chief meal — "the dinner, cena, was in fact the principal private event of the day," coming when all business was over. Suetonius supplies the extreme of imperial gluttony, the emperor Vitellius who "always made three meals a day, sometimes four: breakfast, dinner, and supper, and a drunken revel after all," sustained by habitual vomiting. Dill, the modern voice, weighs the moralists' charges, noting that "modern criticism has laboured hard to correct some of the harsher judgments on the luxury and self-indulgence of the period of the early Empire."
results.answer: The sources reveal a Roman dining culture that evolved from sober family meals to elaborate, often extravagant banquets, driven by new wealth, Greek influence, and a passion for display. In the early Republic, the family dined together in the atrium, with the husband reclining while the wife and children sat—a sign that dinner was merely a break from work [10]. After the Second Punic War, dedicated dining-rooms (*triclinia*) appeared, designed for comfort and conversation, with guests reclining on three couches arranged around a table [3][1]. The typical arrangement accommodated nine guests (three per couch), and placing more than that on a single couch was considered bad form; Cicero criticized Calpurnius Piso for crowding five onto a couch while he lounged alone [1]. The dinner itself consisted of three courses (*gustatio*, *cena*, and dessert of pastry and fruit), but these could be expanded indefinitely by skilled slave-cooks from Greece and the East [4]. Luxury manifested in every detail: silver plate, bronze-mounted couches, fine carpets, and rare foreign delicacies. Mommsen notes that from the mid-second century BC, “Asiatico-Hellenic luxury” flooded Rome, bringing “empty refinement” to the table; hot dishes now appeared at lunch, professional cooks replaced household women, and baked goods became a separate trade [11]. A pontifical banquet given by Mucius Lentulus Niger around 63 BC included sow’s udder, sea-hedgehogs, oysters, boar, ducks, and pastries, illustrating the elaborate menus of the elite [9]. The same author records that a table of African cypress-wood might cost a million sesterces, and that silver kitchen utensils and pearl-encrusted furniture became common [7]. The literary satire of Petronius, described by Dill, presents the most vivid picture of vulgar excess. Trimalchio, a freedman *Sevir Augustalis*, hosts a dinner that is “a long series of surprises”: a boar with sweetmeat baskets hanging from its tusks, a Zodiac-themed course, a ceiling that opens to lower a tray of fruit, and hundred-year-old Falernian wine [6][5]. The entertainment is as coarse as the food—rope-dancers, Homeric scenes recited by the host, and discordant singing [8]. Yet luxury was not limited to private homes; the wealthy were expected to provide public banquets. Julius Caesar feasted the people at 22,000 tables after his triumph in 46 BC, and a certain P. Lucilius of Ostia once set out 217 couches for a civic banquet [12]. In sum, Roman dining combined leisure, social display, and a constant tension between old-fashioned simplicity and ever-increasing extravagance.

results.question: According to Tacitus, what did the Germans consider the greatest disgrace in battle?

results.question: What was the principal building material of Chaldaea, and why was stone so little used?
results.ideal_answer: The principal building material of Chaldaea was brick, made from the abundant clay of the alluvial plain (sun-dried, or kiln-baked for greater hardness). Stone was scarcely used because the alluvium was destitute of it — any stone had to be imported from adjacent countries — so the Chaldaeans built with clay brick in its default.

Stone was little used because the alluvium was "wholly destitute" of it, and though sandstone could be obtained from the nearby Arabian border, "in primitive times, before many canals were made, the difficulty of transporting this weighty substance across the soft and oozy soil of the plain would necessarily have prevented its adoption generally" [1]. As a result, "stone was never adopted in Babylonia as a building material, except to an extremely small extent" [1], and the Chaldaeans relied instead on sun-dried and kiln-baked bricks [1][2].

results.question: What did Maharbal say to Hannibal after the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: When Hannibal hesitated to march at once on Rome after Cannae, his cavalry commander Maharbal — who had urged that within five days Hannibal might dine in the Capitol — rebuked him: "You know how to conquer, Hannibal, but you do not know how to make use of your victory." results.question: What did Scipio Africanus say to the crowd on the anniversary of his victory when prosecuted, before leading them to the Capitol?
results.ideal_answer: Brought to trial on the very anniversary of the battle, Scipio reminded the assembly that on that day he had fought and beaten Hannibal and the Carthaginians in Africa (at Zama); declaring it unfit to spend such a day in litigation, he went up to the Capitol to thank the gods — and the whole assembly left the tribunes and followed him.
results.answer: According to Livy, when Scipio Africanus was prosecuted by the tribunes Petillii, he appeared on the anniversary of his victory at Zama and addressed the crowd as follows: “Tribunes of the people, and you, Romans, on the anniversary of this day I fought a pitched battle in Africa, with Hannibal and the Carthaginians, with good fortune and success. As, therefore, it is but decent that a stop be put, for this day, to litigation and wrangling, I will immediately go to the Capitol, there to return my acknowledgments to Jupiter the supremely good and great, to Juno, Minerva, and the other deities presiding over the Capitol and citadel, and will give them thanks for having, on this day, and at many other times, endowed me both with the will and ability to perform extraordinary services to the commonwealth. Such of you also, Romans, as it suits, come with me and beseech the gods that you may have commanders like myself; since, from my seventeenth year to old age, you have always anticipated my years with honours, and I, your honours with services.” He then went up to the Capitol, and the whole assembly followed him [1].

results.question: Which famous maxims of the sages were inscribed in the forecourt of the temple of Apollo at Delphi?

results.question: How did a Roman patron's clients begin his day, in the morning ceremony at his house?

In the early hours after daybreak, clients and callers gathered at his house to greet him and pay their respects before he went down to the Forum, often escorting him there in a train; under the Empire they thronged the reception hoping for an invitation to dinner or a dole (the sportula).
results.answer: The clients began their day by arriving at the patron's house in the early morning. They would gather in the *atrium*, sometimes waiting for a considerable time, while the patron attended to legal or other business within [3]. According to an old tradition, it was once a source of pride for a Roman to "open up the house at early morning and attend to the legal needs of the clients" (*reclusa mane domo vigilare, clienti promere iura*) [4] [1]. Once the patron was ready to leave, the clients who had been waiting outside would accompany him in a kind of procession to the Forum, some going ahead to clear the way and others following [2]. Thus the morning ceremony involved the clients presenting themselves at the house, waiting in the *atrium* for the patron's attention, and then serving as a public escort as he began his public day.

results.question: What city did Alexander found in memory of the horse he had tamed as a boy?
results.ideal_answer: Bucephala, on the Hydaspes. As a boy Alexander tamed the horse Bucephalas (turning him toward the sun after noticing he feared his own shadow); when the horse died of age and toil after the battle with Porus, Alexander founded the city of Bucephala in his memory.
results.answer: Alexander founded the city of **Bucephala** (also called Bucephalia) in memory of his horse Bucephalus. Arrian reports that the city was founded "on the spot whence he started to cross the river Hydaspes" and named Bucephala in memory of his horse Bucephalas, which died there of toil and old age [1]. Plutarch likewise writes that Alexander "built a city, which he named Bucephalia, in memory of him, on the bank of the river Hydaspes" [2]. Strabo's Geography also mentions the city: "one of these cities he called Bucephalia, from the horse Bucephalus, which was killed in the battle with Porus" [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including the city’s name, its commemorative purpose, and its location on the Hydaspes—is directly supported by the cited passages, with no invented facts or unsupported details. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies the city as Bucephala/Bucephalia and explicitly links it to Alexander's horse Bucephalus, fully satisfying the question's requirements without omitting any asked-for facts. | attribution: The answer correctly identifies the city and clearly attributes the specific details and quotes from each of the three sources in prose.

results.question: How did the wife of the emperor who added Britain to the empire meet her end?
results.ideal_answer: The emperor is Claudius, who invaded Britain to gain a triumph; his wife Messalina, after publicly marrying her lover Silius, was ordered to answer for her conduct, failed in her attempts at suicide in the gardens of Lucullus, and was run through with a sword by a tribune.
results.answer: The wife of Claudius (the emperor who added Britain to the empire) was Messalina. According to the sources, she met her end after a scandalous affair with Gaius Silius, which led to Claudius ordering her death. Cassius Dio reports that Claudius "slew Messalina after she had retreated into the gardens of Asiaticus" [1]. Tacitus provides a more detailed account: Messalina was in the gardens of Lucullus, "struggling for life" and writing letters of entreaty, while Narcissus, fearing Claudius might relent, rushed to have her killed. When the executioners arrived, she "put her hand to a dagger" and in terror was applying it ineffectually to her throat and breast, "when a blow from the tribune drove it through her" [2][3]. Her body was then given up to her mother. Claudius, still at a banquet, was told she was dead but did not inquire whether it was by her own or another's hand [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every detail of Messalina's death is supported by the provided passages, and the answer introduces no unsupported facts, quotes, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by identifying Claudius and Messalina and thoroughly explaining how she died, including the affair, the order for her death, her failed suicide attempt, and the fatal blow by a tribune. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the distinct contributions and details from both Cassius Dio and Tacitus in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: Who tutored the son of the king who crushed the Greeks at Chaeronea?
results.ideal_answer: Aristotle. The king is Philip of Macedon, victor at Chaeronea (where his son Alexander led the charge against the Theban Sacred Band); Philip had earlier sent for Aristotle, the most celebrated philosopher of the age, to instruct Alexander, rewarding him by rebuilding his native Stagira.
results.answer: The son of the king who crushed the Greeks at Chaeronea was Alexander the Great. He was tutored by Aristotle. Philip II expressly invited Aristotle to undertake the education of his son [1][2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims are directly supported by the source passages, including that Philip’s son Alexander was tutored by Aristotle whom Philip expressly invited. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies Aristotle as the tutor, fully satisfying the question's requirement without omitting any asked-for facts. | attribution: The sources agree that Aristotle tutored Alexander the Great at the invitation of his father Philip, and the answer correctly presents this information without any misattribution.

results.question: Who avenged the Roman commander killed at Carrhae?
results.ideal_answer: Ventidius (Publius Ventidius Bassus), Antony's lieutenant. The commander killed at Carrhae was Crassus, slain at a treacherous parley after the defeat by the Parthians; Ventidius later defeated the Parthians in three battles, killing prince Pacorus, a victory Plutarch says "fully avenged" the defeats under Crassus.
results.answer: The Roman commander killed at Carrhae, Marcus Licinius Crassus, was avenged by Publius Ventidius, a lieutenant of Mark Antony. Ventidius defeated the Parthians and slew Pacorus, the son of the Parthian king. According to Plutarch, this victory was "one of the most renowned achievements of the Romans, and fully avenged their defeats under Crassus" [11]. Cassius Dio reports that the Romans believed Ventidius "had paid the Parthians in full through the death of Pacorus for the disasters that Roman arms had incurred in the time of Crassus" [12].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer—that Ventidius, serving under Antony, avenged Crassus by defeating the Parthians and killing Pacorus—is directly supported by the cited Plutarch and Cassius Dio passages, and no substantive new facts, quotes, or outcomes are invented. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies Publius Ventidius as the avenger of Crassus and fully addresses what the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree on the answer, and the model correctly attributes the specific details and quotes to Plutarch and Cassius Dio in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: Why did the Greek army Xenophon served with have to fight its way home?
results.ideal_answer: Their employer Cyrus the Younger was killed at Cunaxa charging his brother King Artaxerxes (struck by a javelin under the eye), leaving the Greeks victorious but stranded deep in Persia; then Tissaphernes treacherously seized their five generals at a conference and cut down the captains, leaving the Ten Thousand leaderless and forced to fight their way to the sea.
results.answer:
The Greek army that Xenophon served with—the Ten Thousand—had to fight its way home primarily because, after the death of Cyrus at the Battle of Cunaxa, the Persian satrap Tissaphernes **treacherously seized and killed their generals**, leaving the Greeks leaderless and deep in hostile territory with no option but to fight their way back to Greece.

After Cyrus fell, Tissaphernes negotiated a truce, promising to guide the Greeks safely home through friendly country with provision markets [9][3]. The Greeks accepted and marched with him as far as the Great Zab river. There, however, Tissaphernes invited the Greek generals—Clearchus, Proxenus, Menon, Agias, and Socrates—into his tent, along with twenty captains and about two hundred soldiers. At a signal, "those within were seized and those without cut down" [8]. The generals were sent to the Persian court and executed [4][7].

As Grote notes, Tissaphernes seemingly planned to draw the Greeks far from the center of the Persian empire and then "open his schemes of treasonable hostility" [5]. After the generals were killed, the Persians summoned the Greek army to surrender, but the Greeks refused indignantly. Leaderless and surrounded by enemies, they elected new commanders—including Xenophon—and "resumed their northward march" [4], fighting off Persian cavalry and hostile peoples all the way to the Black Sea.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer—including Cyrus's death at Cunaxa, Tissaphernes's negotiated truce, the treacherous seizure and execution of the five generals, the election of Xenophon, and the fighting retreat—is directly supported by the provided passages, with no invented facts, names, or quotes. | completeness: It fully answers the question by explaining that Cyrus's death at Cunaxa left the Greeks deep in hostile territory and Tissaphernes' treacherous seizure of their generals left them leaderless, forcing them to fight their way home. | attribution: The sources agree on the historical narrative, and the answer accurately attributes its claims to the correct sources in prose and with citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: How did Alexander react on finding the tomb of the founder of the Persian empire desecrated?
results.ideal_answer: Finding the tomb of Cyrus (whose epitaph read "I am Cyrus, son of Cambyses, who founded the empire of the Persians") dug through and pillaged at Pasargadae, Alexander was grieved; he commissioned Aristobulus to restore the tomb and its furnishings, seal the entrance, and he had the Magian guards arrested and tortured to find the culprits.
results.answer: Alexander was grieved by the outrage committed upon the tomb of Cyrus [3]. He commissioned Aristobulus to restore the tomb, to put back the surviving parts of the body, repair the damaged coffin and couch, replace the ornaments, and seal the entrance with the royal seal [1]. He arrested the Magian guards and put them to the torture to discover the perpetrators, but they confessed nothing and were released [2]. According to Plutarch, he also executed a Macedonian named Polymachus as the robber [4]. After reading the tomb’s inscription, Alexander was sensibly touched and caused it to be recut in Greek characters below the original [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the cited passages, with no invented facts or unsupported details. | completeness: The answer fully covers Alexander’s emotional grief and every responsive action the question asks about—restoring the tomb, sealing it, and arresting and torturing the guards—while extra details do not reduce the completeness score. | attribution: The answer draws on multiple sources but only attributes Plutarch in prose, relying on bare citation markers for the contributions from Arrian.

results.question: Which Spartan commanded the Greeks at the land battle fought the same day as the sea battle at Mycale?
results.ideal_answer: Pausanias, son of Cleombrotos (regent for Leonidas' young son Pleistarchos). Herodotus reports that the victory at Plataea and the fight at Mycale fell on the same day, and that Pausanias was appointed to lead the Spartan army that won at Plataea.
results.answer: The Spartan commander of the Greek land forces at the battle of Mycale was Leotychides. Herodotus records that "first Leotychides, sailing along in his ship and coming as near to the shore as he could, made proclamation by a herald to the Ionians" before the battle [3]. Pausanias also confirms that "Leotychides, who became king in his place, shared with the Athenians and their General Xanthippus, the son of Ariphron, in the action at Mycale" [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer correctly identifies Leotychides from the cited sources but invents the specific role of 'commander of the Greek land forces,' since the passages only place him on a ship making proclamations and sharing in the action, without explicitly naming him commander of the land battle. | completeness: It incorrectly names Leotychides, who commanded at Mycale, rather than Pausanias, who led the Greeks at Plataea—the land battle fought the same day. | attribution: The sources agree that Leotychides was the Spartan commander at the battle of Mycale, and the answer correctly attributes the supporting details to both Herodotus and Pausanias in prose.

results.question: How did the statesman who championed Athens' Long Walls die?
results.ideal_answer: Pericles — who, as Socrates attested, proposed the long wall to the people — died of the plague of Athens, in his case not with violent fits but as a dull, lingering distemper that slowly wasted his body and mind.
results.answer: The statesman who championed Athens' Long Walls was Pericles (the project was part of his foreign policy [1]). He died from the plague, which seized him "not with sharp and violent fits ... but with a dull and lingering distemper, attended with various changes and alterations, leisurely, by little and little, wasting the strength of his body" [11]. According to Grote, he succumbed to a "slow and wearing fever" that undermined his strength [12].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims—that Pericles championed the Long Walls, that the plague seized him with a lingering distemper, and that Grote records a slow and wearing fever—are directly supported by the cited passages [1], [11], and [12]. | completeness: The answer identifies Pericles and fully describes his death from the plague as a slow, lingering distemper that wasted his body, completely addressing what the question asks. | attribution: The answer surfaces the disagreement between the sources regarding whether Pericles died of the plague or a different fever, but it fails to name Plutarch in prose, relying on a bare citation marker instead.

results.question: How did the two leading assassins of Caesar die?
results.ideal_answer: Both died at Philippi. Cassius, mistakenly believing the battle lost, bared his neck to his freedman Pindarus and was beheaded; in the second battle Brutus, defeated, fell on his sword — held, by some accounts, by his friend Strato — and died.
results.answer:
The two leading assassins of Caesar, Brutus and Cassius, both died by suicide after their defeat at the Battle of Philippi.

Cassius, believing his side was losing and unaware that Brutus had been victorious on the other wing, ordered his freedman Pindarus to kill him. Plutarch says, "pulling up his mantle over his head, he made his neck bare, and held it forth to Pindarus, commanding him to strike" [2]; Appian also reports that he "presented his throat" and was slain [1]. Some sources note the alternative that Pindarus killed him without orders, then disappeared [1][2]. Cassius died on his birthday [1].

Brutus, facing final defeat, asked his friends to help him die. When they refused, he turned to Strato, an old comrade from his studies. According to Plutarch, Brutus "taking hold of the hilt of his sword and directing it with both his hands, he fell upon it, and killed himself," though others say Strato held the sword at Brutus's entreaty [5]. Appian states Strato "thrust his sword into the side of Brutus, who did not shrink or turn away" [4]. Both accounts agree he died by the sword with Strato's assistance.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is largely grounded but invents the unsupported claim that Brutus asked his friends to help him die and they refused, a narrative detail found in none of the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully covers the deaths of both leading assassins, describing how Cassius and Brutus each died at Philippi, the specific circumstances, and who assisted them, omitting nothing the question requires. | attribution: The sources agree or provide complementary alternative accounts, and the answer correctly and explicitly attributes the specific details and quotes to their respective sources in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: What revolt broke out in Britain under the emperor who was the last of his dynasty?
results.ideal_answer: The revolt of Boudicca (Buduica), queen of the Iceni, broke out under Nero — whom Dio calls the last of the descendants of Aeneas and Augustus. While the governor Paulinus was away at Mona she sacked two Roman cities with enormous slaughter before being defeated.
results.answer: The revolt of Boudicca (also spelled Boadicea or Buduica), queen of the Iceni, broke out in Britain under the emperor Nero, who was the last of the Julio-Claudian dynasty. Cassius Dio reports that "a terrible disaster had taken place in Britain" with two cities sacked and eighty thousand Romans and allies perishing, and that "all this ruin was brought upon them by a woman" [1]. Suetonius likewise records that the Britons rose "under the conduct of Boadicea, queen of the Iceni, who had been treated in the most ignominious manner by the Roman tribunes" [3]. Dio adds that Buduica herself led an army of about 230,000 and rode into battle on a chariot [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer correctly identifies the revolt and its leader from the sources but invents unsupported details—an army of 230,000 and Boudicca riding a chariot—that appear in none of the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully identifies the revolt of Boudicca and correctly names Nero as the last of the Julio-Claudian dynasty, covering every element the question asks for. | attribution: The answer misattributes the claim that Boudicca led an army of 230,000 and rode into battle on a chariot to Cassius Dio, as these details are not present in the provided source passages.

results.question: How did the brother of Hannibal who marched an army into Italy to reinforce him meet his end?
results.ideal_answer: Hasdrubal, Hannibal's brother, crossed the Alps into Italy to reinforce him but was defeated and killed by the consuls Marcus Livius and Claudius Nero at the river Metaurus (207 BC); his severed head, carried south, was thrown before the outposts of Hannibal's camp.
results.answer: Hasdrubal, Hannibal's brother, was killed in the Battle of the Metaurus in 207 BC. Appian states that he 'was slain' along with his scattered army [1]. Livy's summary records that he was 'vanquished at the Metaurus, and slain' and that his head was thrown into Hannibal's camp [2]. Cassius Dio writes that the Romans 'destroyed Hasdrubal along with many others' and that Nero 'fixed Hasdrubal's head on a pole nearby' [3]. William Smith adds that after the battle was lost, he 'rushed into the midst of the enemy, and fell, sword in hand' [4]. Mommsen similarly notes that he 'sought and found ... an honourable soldier's death' [5].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim, including the date, battle name, manner of death, and post-battle treatment of Hasdrubal's head, is directly supported by the provided passages with accurate quotations and no invented content. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies Hasdrubal and thoroughly describes how he died at the Battle of the Metaurus in 207 BC, fully addressing the question's inquiry about his fate without omitting any explicitly requested information. | attribution: The sources agree on the core facts of Hasdrubal's death in battle, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details and phrasing from each respective source in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: How did the Carthaginian commander who led elephants across the Alps eventually die, long after his war with Rome?
results.ideal_answer: Hannibal, who led his army with its elephants across the Alps into Italy in the Second Punic War, died decades later in exile: cornered at the court of king Prusias by a Roman demand for his surrender, he drank the poison he had long kept ready.
results.answer: Hannibal, the Carthaginian commander who crossed the Alps with elephants, died long after the Second Punic War by suicide, taking poison that he had long kept ready for such an emergency. After being forced to flee from Antiochus, he found refuge at the court of King Prusias of Bithynia. When the Roman envoy Titus Quinctius Flamininus arrived and pressured Prusias, the king sent soldiers to surround Hannibal's house. Finding all exits blocked, Hannibal called for the poison and drank it, famously saying, "Let us release the Romans from their long anxiety, since they think it too long to wait for the death of an old man" [1]. Cassius Dio similarly reports that "being unable to escape he committed suicide" [3]. Plutarch adds that while the dominant version has him drinking poison, some say he had a servant strangle him with his cloak, and others claim he drank bull's blood "after the example of Themistocles and Midas" [4]. Mommsen summarizes that when Hannibal saw his house beset by assassins, he took poison, having long been prepared to do so [2]. Livy's epitome confirms he "swallows poison" while at the court of Prusias [6].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is fully supported by the provided passages, including the manner of death by poison, the circumstances at Prusias's court, and the alternative accounts; any minor citation mismatches do not constitute invented content. | completeness: The answer fully covers how Hannibal died long after the war—by poison at Prusias’s court after Roman pressure—without omitting any element the question explicitly asks for. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces the differing historical accounts of Hannibal's death and explicitly attributes the alternative versions to Plutarch in prose while attributing the primary account to the other sources.

results.question: Who commanded the Roman army that razed Carthage to the ground, and what did he reportedly weep over as the city burned?
results.ideal_answer: Scipio Aemilianus (Scipio the Younger), who destroyed Carthage in the Third Punic War (146 BC). As the city burned he shed tears and, asked by Polybius what he meant, admitted he frankly named his own country — fearing that one day the same fate would befall Rome.
results.answer: The Roman army that razed Carthage in 146 BC was commanded by Scipio Aemilianus (also called Scipio Africanus the Younger). As the city burned, he reportedly wept and reflected on the rise and fall of great powers. Appian writes that “Scipio, beholding this spectacle, is said to have shed tears and publicly lamented the fortune of the enemy,” meditating on the fates of Troy, the Assyrians, Medes, Persians, and Macedonians [1]. Polybius, who was present, records that “at the sight of the city utterly perishing amidst the flames Scipio burst into tears, and stood long reflecting on the inevitable change which awaits cities, nations, and dynasties” [2]. When asked by Polybius what he meant, Scipio replied that he feared for his own country’s fate [1]. The historian William Smith notes that Scipio “is said to have repeated the lines of the Iliad over the flames of Carthage: 'The day shall come when sacred Troy shall perish, and Priam and his people shall be slain'” [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims are directly supported by the provided passages, and the full name "Scipio Aemilianus" (Africanus the Younger) is a correct modern gloss of the commander described in the sources. | completeness: The answer fully identifies Scipio Aemilianus as the commander and thoroughly addresses what he reportedly wept over, including his fear for Rome's eventual downfall. | attribution: The sources agree on the core facts and provide complementary details, and the answer correctly attributes the specific quotes and details to their respective sources in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: Which Theban general broke Spartan power in a great pitched battle, and where did he fall in his last victory?
results.ideal_answer: Epaminondas of Thebes, who shattered Spartan supremacy at Leuctra (371 BC) and fell mortally wounded at Mantinea (362 BC), dying in the very hour of his victory.
results.answer: The Theban general who broke Spartan power in a great pitched battle was Epaminondas. He achieved this at the Battle of Leuctra in 371 BC. In his last victory, the Battle of Mantinea (362 BC), he fell mortally wounded. Diodorus Siculus records that Epaminondas “was killed at the battle at Mantinea, where he obtained a famous victory for his country by his own fall” [9], and describes his final moments after being brought back to camp [2]. Grote likewise states that Epaminondas “was mortally wounded” at Mantinea and died after having his spear‑head withdrawn [4], adding that the Mantineans later showed Pausanias “a spot called Skiopê near the field of battle” where the wounded general had been carried off [5]. Thus, Epaminondas fell on the field of Mantinea, with tradition pointing to the specific location of Skiopê.

results.question: Which Achaean coastal city was swallowed by the sea in a single night, and what was blamed for the catastrophe?
results.ideal_answer: Helike (Helice) in Achaea, which an earthquake and an inundation of the sea overwhelmed in a single night (373 BC). The catastrophe was blamed on the wrath of Poseidon, whose suppliants the people of Helike had dragged from his temple and slain.
results.answer: The Achaean coastal city that was swallowed by the sea in a single night was Helice (Helike). The catastrophe was blamed on the anger of the god Neptune (Poseidon). Strabo reports that the sea was "raised to a great height by an earthquake, and overwhelmed both Helice and the temple of the Heliconian Neptune," and that "this calamity happened in consequence of the anger of Neptune" because the Heliceans refused to give the Ionians either the image of Neptune or the model of the temple [1][2]. Diodorus Siculus similarly states that the destruction was "the effect of the anger of the gods, for the impious violation of the rights of religion" [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including the identification of Helice, the night-time inundation, the anger of Neptune/Poseidon, and the quoted passages—is supported by the sources, and the glosses Helike/Poseidon are correct standard equivalents. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies Helice (Helike) as the city and attributes the catastrophe to the anger of Neptune/Poseidon, fully satisfying both parts of the question while extra supporting detail does not reduce the score. | attribution: The sources agree on the core facts, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details and quotes to Strabo and Diodorus Siculus in clear prose without any misattribution.

results.question: How did the Spartan regent who led the Greeks to victory at Plataea fall from power and die?
results.ideal_answer: Pausanias the regent, who commanded the victorious Greeks at Plataea (479 BC), afterwards intrigued with Persia to make himself master of Greece; detected, he fled to the temple of Athena of the Brazen House, where the ephors roofed him in, barricaded the doors and starved him, bringing him out only as he was about to expire.
results.answer: Pausanias, the Spartan regent who commanded at Plataea, fell from power after his arrogant and Persian-like conduct at Byzantium aroused suspicion that he was plotting with the barbarians. Recalled a second time under threat of being declared a public enemy, he was initially imprisoned by the ephors but secured his release for lack of irrefutable proof [1]. Later, the ephors obtained decisive evidence by overhearing his conversation with a messenger to Artabazus; when they moved to arrest him in the street, Pausanias fled to the sanctuary of Athena of the Brazen House [3]. The ephors "took off the roof of the chamber, and having made sure that he was inside, shut him in, barricaded the doors, and staying before the place, reduced him by starvation" [3]. They brought him out while he was still breathing, and he died immediately upon leaving the temple [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is largely faithful to the sources but introduces the name 'Artabazus,' which appears in no provided passage, constituting a minor invention. | completeness: The answer fully addresses both parts of the question, explaining how Pausanias fell from power through suspicion of Persian intrigue, recall, and detection, and how he died by being barricaded and starved in the temple of Athena. | attribution: The sources agree on the narrative of Pausanias's fall from power and death, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details to the relevant passages without any misattribution.

results.question: The empire whose supreme god was Asshur fell when its great capital was stormed — who destroyed that capital?
results.ideal_answer: Assyria — whose national god was Asshur and whose capital was Nineveh — fell when Nineveh was stormed by the Medes under Cyaxares allied with the Babylonians under Nabopolassar; the last Assyrian king, his means of resistance exhausted, burned himself in his palace.
results.answer: The capital, Nineveh, was destroyed by the combined forces of Cyaxares, the king of the Medes, and Nabopolassar, a Babylonian general who betrayed the Assyrian king Saracus. According to George Rawlinson, "Cyaxares and Nabopolassar then joined their efforts against Nineveh; and Saracus, unable to resist them, took counsel of his despair, and, after all means of resistance were exhausted, burned himself in his palace" [3]. The supreme god Asshur is identified as the head of the Assyrian pantheon in the same volume [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the source passages, including the fall of Nineveh to Cyaxares and Nabopolassar, the betrayal of Saracus, and the role of Asshur, with no invented facts or quotes. | completeness: It fully answers what the question asks by identifying the capital as Nineveh and naming both destroyers—Cyaxares (king of the Medes) and Nabopolassar (Babylonian)—while also correctly linking the supreme god Asshur to Assyria. | attribution: The passages agree as they are from the same author and address different parts of the prompt without contradiction, and the answer correctly attributes the information to George Rawlinson with appropriate citations.

results.question: How did the founder of the Persian empire meet his death on campaign?
results.ideal_answer: Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian empire, was killed on campaign beyond the Araxes against the Massagetae and their queen Tomyris; the greater part of his army was destroyed and Cyrus himself fell there, after a reign of twenty-nine years.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian Empire, met his death on campaign against the Massagetae, a people on his north-eastern frontier ruled by Queen Tomyris. After Cyrus used a stratagem involving a banquet with wine to defeat and capture a portion of the Massagetae army — including Tomyris's son Spargapises, who subsequently took his own life — Tomyris gathered all her forces and joined battle with Cyrus. Herodotus describes it as "of all the battles fought by Barbarians I judge to have been the fiercest" [2]. The two sides first shot arrows at one another, then engaged in close combat with spears and daggers. Ultimately "the Massagetai got the better in the fight: and the greater part of the Persian army was destroyed there on the spot, and Cyrus himself brought his life to an end there, after he had reigned in all thirty years wanting one" [2]. After the battle, Tomyris had the Persian dead searched for Cyrus's corpse; when she found it, "she let his head down into the skin" — a skin she had filled with human blood — "and doing outrage to the corpse" declared, "I according to my threat will give thee thy fill of blood" [2][3]. Herodotus notes that "as regards the end of the life of Cyrus there are many tales told, but this which I have related is to my mind the most worthy of belief" [3]. A conflicting account, preserved by Ctesias and summarized by Rawlinson, holds that Cyrus was mortally wounded in battle against the Derbices (a nation bordering India, assisted by Indian allies with elephants) and died of his wound on the third day, though his army ultimately prevailed [1].

results.question: Which Jewish prisoner predicted that his Roman captor would become emperor, and did the prophecy come true?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus, captured at Jotapata, predicted to Vespasian that he would become Caesar and emperor. The prophecy came true when the eastern legions proclaimed Vespasian emperor (AD 69) — Tiberius Alexander first swearing the legions of Egypt to him on 1 July — and Vespasian, recalling the prophecy, freed Josephus.
results.answer:
The Jewish prisoner was Josephus (the historian Flavius Josephus), and his Roman captor was Vespasian. When Josephus was brought before Vespasian after being captured at Jotapata, he declared to him: "Thou, O Vespasian, art Caesar and emperor, thou, and this thy son" [2]. At the time Vespasian dismissed this as a trick to save his life, but later "he remembered what Josephus had said to him when he ventured to foretell his coming to the empire while Nero was alive" [1]. Cassius Dio records a slightly different version: Josephus laughed and said, "You may imprison me now, but a year later when you become emperor you will release me" [3]. 

Yes, the prophecy came true. Vespasian did become emperor, and once he did, he recalled Josephus's prediction, declared it "Divine," and set Josephus at liberty — even cutting his chains to pieces so that he would "be like a man that had never been bound at all" [1].

results.question: Which king rebuilt the Jerusalem temple on a grand scale, and how did he die?

results.question: How did the reign of the emperor Vitellius come to its end?
results.ideal_answer: Vitellius's army was crushed by the Flavian forces at the second battle of Cremona (Bedriacum); when Rome fell, Vitellius was dragged from hiding through the streets to the Gemonian stairs, tortured and killed, and his body cast into the Tiber.
results.answer: After the forces of Vespasian entered Rome, Vitellius attempted to flee but was captured. He was dragged from his hiding place, bound, and paraded through the streets to the Forum and then to the Scalae Gemoniae (the Gemonian Stairs), where he was tortured and killed. Suetonius states that he was 'dragged half-naked into the Forum, with his hands tied behind him, a rope about his neck, and his clothes torn' and 'upon the Scalae Gemoniae, he was tormented and put to death in lingering tortures, and then dragged by a hook into the Tiber' [1]. Cassius Dio adds that after being abused and struck, he exclaimed 'Yet I was once your emperor!' before they took him to the top of the Scalae Gemoniae where they struck him down and cut off his head, which was 'carried about all over the city' [4]; his wife later saw to his burial [4]. Tacitus similarly records that he was 'driven along till they reached the Gemoniae' and 'fell under a shower of blows', remarking that 'the mob reviled the dead man with the same heartlessness with which they had flattered him when he was alive' [3]. Josephus briefly notes that he was 'drawn along through the multitude, and abused with all sorts of torments, had his head cut off in the midst of Rome' [5]. Thus his reign ended in violent death amid public humiliation.

results.question: After the catastrophe at Cannae, what feature of Rome's system of government was credited with explaining her swift recovery?
results.ideal_answer: Rome's mixed, balanced constitution — the three elements of consuls (monarchic), senate (aristocratic) and people (democratic), each checking the others — which Polybius describes precisely at the epoch of Cannae as the source of Roman stability and resilience.

results.question: What naval catastrophe destroyed the great Athenian expedition to Sicily, and what became of its captured general?
results.ideal_answer: The Athenian armament was annihilated at Syracuse — beaten decisively in the Great Harbour and then destroyed "with a total destruction" in the retreat (413 BC). results.answer: The great Athenian expedition to Sicily was destroyed in a final, desperate naval battle in the Great Harbor of Syracuse. After the battle, the surviving Athenians attempted to retreat by land but were eventually forced to surrender. The captured general Nicias, along with his colleague Demosthenes, was put to death by the Syracusans. Thucydides reports that "Nicias and Demosthenes were butchered, against the will of Gylippus" [5]; Grote adds that they were ordered executed by a decree of the Syracusan assembly, though Hermocrates secretly enabled them to die by their own hands [4].

results.question: Why is Roman concrete so durable compared to modern concrete?
results.answer: Roman concrete owes its exceptional durability to the use of volcanic ash (pozzolana), particularly from the region of Baiae near Naples. Cassius Dio describes how this volcanic earth, when mixed with water and sand, undergoes a chemical reaction: it "grow[s] into a solid piece" and "hardens and petrifies," ultimately becoming "indissoluble" [2]. This pozzolanic reaction creates a material that actually strengthens over time, especially in seawater, unlike modern Portland-cement concrete which can degrade. The lasting quality of this mortar was already noted by Mommsen, who praised the "everlasting mortar of their buildings" as a testament to Roman engineering [1].

To address what the sources actually say about Hannibal's tactics at Cannae: he chose ground where a strong wind would blow dust into the Romans' faces [2][1][7]; he arranged his infantry in a crescent formation with the centre advanced, so that when the Romans pushed forward they were enveloped on both flanks by his Libyan heavy infantry [2][5][4]; he placed his best cavalry on the wings, with Hasdrubal breaking the Roman right wing before wheeling to attack the rear [6]; and he used ambuscades and a simulated desertion ploy [1][3]. results.question: What were the symptoms of the epidemic that devastated Athens?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides (who had the disease himself) describes the "distemper" thus: people in good health were suddenly attacked by violent heats in the head, redness and inflammation of the eyes, a bloody throat and tongue with fetid breath, then sneezing, hoarseness, chest pain and a hard cough, stomach upset and retching with violent spasms. Most died around the seventh to ninth day; in survivors the disease could descend into the bowels (fatal diarrhoea), or cost them fingers, toes, eyes, or memory.
results.answer: According to Thucydides, who had the disease himself and observed it in others, the plague that devastated Athens began with sudden violent heats in the head, and redness and inflammation in the eyes; the inward parts, such as the throat or tongue, became bloody and emitted an unnatural and fetid breath [1]. These symptoms were followed by sneezing and hoarseness, after which the pain soon reached the chest and produced a hard cough [1]. When it fixed in the stomach, it caused upset and discharges of bile of every kind, accompanied by very great distress, and in most cases ineffectual retching followed, producing violent spasms [1]. Externally the body was not very hot to the touch, nor pale, but reddish, livid, and breaking out into small pustules and ulcers; but internally it burned so that the patient could not bear to have on him clothing or linen even of the very lightest description, or to be otherwise than stark naked [2]. They suffered from unquenchable thirst and a miserable feeling of not being able to rest or sleep [2]. The body did not waste away while the distemper was at its height, and most succumbed on the seventh or eighth day to the internal inflammation [2]. If they passed this stage and the disease descended further into the bowels, inducing violent ulceration accompanied by severe diarrhoea, this brought on a weakness which was generally fatal [2]. Even where it did not prove mortal, it still left its mark on the extremities: it settled in the privy parts, the fingers, and the toes, and many escaped with the loss of these, some too with that of their eyes; others were seized with an entire loss of memory on their first recovery, and did not know either themselves or their friends [2].

results.question: What role did mass kidnapping play in early Rome's search for wives?
results.ideal_answer: Rome under Romulus was short of women and neighboring peoples refused intermarriage, so Romulus staged games (the Consualia) and invited the neighbors; at a signal the Roman youths rushed in and carried off the unmarried women — the "rape of the Sabine women." The ensuing war with the Sabines ended with the women themselves brokering peace and the two peoples merging.
results.answer:
Mass kidnapping was the foundational solution to early Rome's lack of women. According to both Livy and Plutarch, Romulus's new city was filled with men—many of them fugitives and outcasts—but the neighboring communities refused to grant them intermarriage, contemptuously asking whether Rome had "opened any asylum for women also" [6]. Romulus therefore resolved to obtain wives by force. He staged games in honor of the god Consus (the Consualia) and invited the surrounding peoples, especially the Sabines, who came with their wives and children [6][2]. At a prearranged signal—Romulus rising and wrapping his purple robe around him—the Roman youth drew their swords, fell upon the guests, and "ravished away the daughters of the Sabines" while the parents fled [2].

The scale of the abduction is disputed: Plutarch reports that some sources say only thirty were taken, while Valerius Antias gives five hundred and twenty-seven, and Juba six hundred and eighty-three [2]. Livy simply says "a great number were carried off at hap-hazard" [6]. Plutarch notes that the Romans took only one married woman, Hersilia, and that unknowingly—"which showed they did not commit this rape wantonly, but with a design purely of forming alliance with their neighbors by the greatest and surest bonds" [2]. Romulus himself went among the captives to reassure them, promising lawful wedlock, shared property, citizenship, and children, arguing that "from injuries love and friendship often arise" [5].

The kidnapping directly caused wars with the Sabines and neighboring Latin towns, but the abducted women—now wives and mothers—ultimately intervened on the battlefield, throwing themselves between the armies and begging their fathers and husbands to make peace [7][4]. The result was a treaty uniting Romans and Sabines into a single state, with the Sabine women "still dearer to their husbands and parents, and above all to Romulus himself" [7]. The thirty curiae (voting divisions) were even named after the Sabine women [7][8]. Thus the mass kidnapping not only supplied Rome with wives but, by forcing a union with the Sabines, doubled the city's population and secured its future.

results.question: How did the Sabine commander Mettius Curtius end up in a swamp during the battle for Rome?
results.ideal_answer: During the battle between Romans and Sabines on the future site of the Forum, Mettius Curtius' horse took fright at the noise of his pursuers and carried him headlong into a morass; he barely escaped (Plutarch says he abandoned the mired horse and saved himself). The spot was afterwards called the Curtian Lake.
results.answer: According to Plutarch, shortly before the battle the river had overflowed and left behind in the plain (where the forum later stood) "a deep blind mud and slime, which, though it did not appear much to the eye, and was not easily avoided, at bottom was deceitful and dangerous" [1]. As the Sabines advanced, Curtius, "a gallant man, eager of honor, and of aspiring thoughts, being mounted on horseback, was galloping on before the rest, and mired his horse here, and, endeavoring for awhile by whip and spur and voice to disentangle him, but finding it impossible, quitted him and saved himself" [1]. Thus he ended up in the swamp when his horse became stuck in the hidden slime; he survived by abandoning the animal.

results.question: What omens were believed to have foretold Claudius' death?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius lists as the chief presages a comet, lightning striking his father Drusus' monument, and the deaths of magistrates of all ranks that year. Cassius Dio adds a shower of blood, a thunderbolt on the Praetorian standards, the temple of Jupiter Victor opening of its own accord, and bees swarming in the camp.
results.answer: According to Suetonius, the chief presages of Claudius' death were "the appearance of a comet, his father Drusus’s monument being struck by lightning, and the death of most of the magistrates of all ranks that year" [2]. Cassius Dio offers a more detailed list of prodigies: "the comet star, which was seen over a wide expanse of territory, by the shower of blood, by the bolt that descended upon the standards of the Pretorians, by the opening of its own accord of the temple of Jupiter Victor, by the swarming of bees in the camp, and by the fact that one representative of each political office died" [1]. Thus both sources agree on the comet and the widespread deaths of magistrates; Dio additionally records a shower of blood, a lightning bolt on the Praetorian standards, the spontaneous opening of the temple of Jupiter Victor, and a swarm of bees in the camp as omens foretelling Claudius’ death.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims, quotations, and paraphrases are directly and accurately supported by the cited passages, with no invented facts or unsupported details. | completeness: The answer fully covers all omens mentioned in the reference from both Suetonius and Cassius Dio, including the comet, lightning striking Drusus' monument, deaths of magistrates, shower of blood, thunderbolt on the Praetorian standards, temple of Jupiter Victor opening, and bees swarming in the camp. | attribution: The sources provide complementary lists of omens without making incompatible claims, and the answer correctly attributes each source's specific contributions in clear prose.

results.question: Does Thucydides describe anything like a tsunami?
results.ideal_answer: Yes. He reports that during the earthquakes of 426 BC the sea at Orobiae in Euboea retired from the coast and returned in a huge wave, flooding part of the town and leaving some of it permanently under water, killing those who could not reach high ground (with a similar inundation at Atalanta). He even gives the correct causal explanation: the earthquake drives the sea back, and it recoils with redoubled force.
results.answer: Yes. Thucydides describes a tsunami during the sixth year of the Peloponnesian War. He reports that after numerous earthquakes, the sea at Orobiae in Euboea "retiring from the then line of coast, returned in a huge wave and invaded a great part of the town... such of the inhabitants perishing as could not run up to the higher ground in time." A similar inundation occurred at Atalanta off the Opuntian Locrian coast, "carrying away part of the Athenian fort and wrecking one of two ships" drawn up on the beach. He explicitly offers a causal explanation: "The cause, in my opinion, of this phenomenon must be sought in the earthquake. At the point where its shock has been the most violent, the sea is driven back and, suddenly recoiling with redoubled force, causes the inundation" [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim, including the modern gloss of the earthquake-induced inundation as a tsunami, is directly supported by the cited passage [1]. | completeness: It fully answers the question by confirming Thucydides describes tsunami-like events and provides all key details: the inundations at Orobiae and Atalanta, the retreat and return of the sea, the damage and casualties, and the explicit causal explanation linking the phenomenon to earthquakes. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes all information to the single provided source without any misattribution.

results.question: Which Persian king was said to suffer from epilepsy from birth?
results.ideal_answer: Cambyses. Herodotus reports that he was said to have suffered from birth from a grievous malady "called by some the sacred disease" (the ancient name for epilepsy), and remarks it was no surprise that with so diseased a body his mind was not sound either.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, the Persian king Cambyses suffered from epilepsy from birth. He describes it as "a certain grievous malady, that which is called by some the 'sacred' disease" and states that Cambyses "had from his birth" this condition [1].

results.question: How were the pontoon bridges across the Hellespont built?
results.ideal_answer: After a storm destroyed the first pair (built by Phoenicians with flax cables and Egyptians with papyrus cables), the rebuilt bridges rested on rows of anchored fifty-oared galleys and triremes — 360 under one bridge and 314 under the other — over which cables of white flax and papyrus were stretched tight with wooden windlasses; planks, brushwood, and stamped earth were laid on top, with barriers so the animals would not see the sea.
results.answer: The pontoon bridges across the Hellespont, built on Xerxes' orders, were constructed in the following manner. Two separate lines of ships — fifty‑oared galleys and triremes — were moored breastwise across the strait, 360 vessels for the bridge nearest the Euxine Sea and 314 for the other [2]. The ships were held in place by very large anchors both towards the Pontus and towards the Aegean, and a gap was left between the ships in three places to allow small vessels to sail through [2]. Over each line of ships six massive cables were stretched and tightened with wooden windlasses; after an earlier attempt that used the materials separately — the Phoenicians employing flax and the Egyptians papyrus [3] — the rebuilt bridges used two cables of white flax and four of papyrus together for each bridge [1]. Across the taut cables were laid logs of wood sawn to match the breadth of the bridge, and these were fastened down with a second layer of ropes [1]. Upon this foundation brushwood was spread, then earth stamped down firmly [1]. Finally, a barrier was built along each side high enough to keep the baggage‑animals and horses from seeing the sea below [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer accurately restates nearly all construction details from Herodotus but introduces an unsupported specific number by claiming the ships left gaps 'in three places,' whereas the source only mentions an opening for passage without specifying three. | completeness: The answer thoroughly covers all aspects of the bridge construction asked for—vessels, anchoring, cables, decking, and barriers—without omitting any part of the question. | attribution: The sources provide complementary, non-contradictory details about the different phases of the bridge construction, and the answer correctly attributes each specific detail to the corresponding source without any misattribution.

results.question: What solar eclipse marked the departure of Xerxes' army, and how was it interpreted?
results.ideal_answer: As the army set out from Sardis in spring, the sun left its place in a perfectly clear sky and day became night. Xerxes, concerned, asked the Magians, who declared the god was foreshowing to the Greeks the abandonment of their cities — since the sun was the foreshower of events for the Greeks, the moon for the Persians. Xerxes marched on greatly pleased.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, as Xerxes' army was setting out from Sardis at the beginning of spring, "the Sun left his place in the heaven and was invisible, though there was no gathering of clouds and the sky was perfectly clear; and instead of day it became night" [2]. Xerxes was troubled by this, but when he consulted the Magians, they declared that "the god was foreshowing to the Hellenes a leaving of their cities, saying that the Sun was the foreshower of events for the Hellenes, but the Moon for the Persians" [2]. Thus the eclipse was interpreted as an auspicious sign for the Persian campaign, and Xerxes continued on his way "with very great joy" [2]. (Note: the scholar J. B. Bury comments that the eclipse actually took place two years later, but the tradition followed by Herodotus transposed its date "to a more impressive and significant occasion" [1].)
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim, quotation, and interpretive summary in the answer is directly supported by the provided source passages, with no invented facts or unsupported details. | completeness: The answer fully covers both parts of the question by describing the solar eclipse at Sardis and detailing the Magians' interpretation that it foretold the Greeks abandoning their cities, without omitting any required information. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the historical narrative and the Magians' interpretation to Herodotus, and the scholarly context regarding the eclipse's actual date to J. B. Bury, using clear prose attribution without any misattributions.

results.question: How did the Persian royal postal relay system work?
results.ideal_answer: According to Herodotus, men and horses were stationed along the route, one for each day's journey; the message was carried by the first rider to the second, the second to the third, and so on, like the Greek torch-race. Neither snow, rain, heat, nor darkness of night stopped them — nothing mortal travelled faster. The Persians called it the angareion.
results.answer:
The Persian royal postal relay system, instituted by Darius I, worked by placing post-houses at regular intervals along the main routes of the empire, each spaced according to how far a horse could gallop at top speed without stopping. At each station the state maintained couriers and multiple relays of fresh horses. When a dispatch arrived, a courier would take it and ride at full speed to the next station, where it was handed to a new courier on a fresh horse, and so on until it reached its destination [1]. According to Xenophon, the messengers traveled by night as well as by day, and the speed was so great that some compared it to the flight of birds [1].

Herodotus describes the system vividly: “there is nothing mortal which accomplishes a journey with more speed than these messengers, so skilfully has this been invented by the Persians: for they say that according to the number of days of which the entire journey consists, so many horses and men are set at intervals, each man and horse appointed for a day’s journey. These neither snow nor rain nor heat nor darkness of night prevents from accomplishing each one the task proposed to him, with the very utmost speed. The first then rides and delivers the message with which he is charged to the second, and the second to the third; and after that it goes through them handed from one to the other, as in the torch-race among the Hellenes” [3][2]. He adds that the Persians called this relay of horses “angareion” [2].

The royal road itself, from Sardis to Susa, was measured out in stages (111 in all, totaling 450 parasangs or 13,500 furlongs), with resting-places along the way, so that a traveler covering 150 furlongs a day would take 90 days [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer—from Darius's establishment of post-houses, the courier-and-relay process, Xenophon's and Herodotus's descriptions, the term 'angareion', to the royal road's stages and measurements—is directly supported by the provided source passages. | completeness: The answer thoroughly covers the relay mechanics, station spacing, fresh-horse handoffs, continuous day-and-night operation in all weather, the torch-race analogy, and the name angareion, fully addressing how the system worked. | attribution: The sources agree and provide complementary details about the postal system, and the answer correctly attributes all claims to their respective sources in prose and with citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: Which people beyond Scythia practiced cannibalism?
results.ideal_answer: The Androphagoi ("man-eaters"). Herodotus describes them as nomads dwelling north of Scythia with their own language and the most savage manners of all human beings, acknowledging no law — and alone of all these nations they are man-eaters.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, the Androphagoi (meaning 'man-eaters') were the people beyond Scythia who practiced cannibalism: 'The Androphagoi have the most savage manners of all human beings… and alone of all these nations they are man-eaters' [1].
