results.question: What celibate Jewish brotherhood that held all property in common and despised private wealth is described at length?
results.ideal_answer: The Essenes. Josephus devotes long passages to this Jewish sect: they reject pleasure, neglect marriage, hold riches in contempt, and own nothing privately — by their law all property is thrown into a common stock, "so that a rich man enjoys no more of his own wealth than he who hath nothing at all," with about four thousand living this way.
results.answer:
The brotherhood is the **Essenes** (also spelled **Essens**), described at length by Josephus in both *The Wars of the Jews* and *The Antiquities of the Jews*.

Josephus identifies them as one of the three philosophical sects among the Jews, alongside the Pharisees and Sadducees, but notes the Essenes "pretend to a severer discipline" [2]. He writes that they "reject pleasures as an evil, but esteem continence" and "neglect wedlock" (i.e. they are celibate), choosing instead to adopt other people's children and raise them in their own way [2].

Regarding wealth, Josephus states: "These men are despisers of riches, and so very communicative as raises our admiration. Nor is there any one to be found among them who hath more than another; for it is a law among them, that those who come to them must let what they have be common to the whole order,—insomuch that among them all there is no appearance of poverty, or excess of riches, but every one's possessions are intermingled with every other's possessions; and so there is, as it were, one patrimony among all the brethren" [2]. In the *Antiquities* he similarly notes that their institution "will not suffer any thing to hinder them from having all things in common; so that a rich man enjoys no more of his own wealth than he who hath nothing at all" and that they "neither marry wives, nor are desirous to keep servants" [1].

Josephus also notes that there is "another order of Essens" who do marry (after a three-year trial), but they are a distinct subset; the main body remains celibate [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the provided passages, and 'Essenes' is a correct standard modern gloss for Josephus's 'Essens'. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies the Essenes and fully covers every element the question asks for: celibacy, communal property, contempt for private wealth, and being described at length by Josephus. | attribution: The sources agree on the description of the Essenes, and the answer correctly attributes the information to Josephus and his specific works without any misattribution.

results.question: What stepped, multi-tiered temple-tower did the Babylonians raise at the center of their cities?
results.ideal_answer: The ziggurat — a solid temple-tower built in receding stages (square set upon square), the most essential feature of a Babylonian temple, with a shrine at its summit. The number of stages ranged from two up to seven or eight; the great temple of Belus at Babylon was the famous example.
results.answer: The Babylonians raised a stepped, multi-tiered temple-tower called a **ziggurat** at the centre of their cities. Rawlinson describes it as “a *ziggurat*, or tower, which was either square, or at any rate rectangular, and built in stages, the smallest number of such stages being two, and the largest known number seven” [2]. The great temple of Belus at Babylon, for instance, rose in seven stages, each coloured to represent one of the seven planets—black for Saturn, orange for Jupiter, red for Mars, gold for the Sun, pale yellow for Venus, blue for Mercury, and silver for the Moon—so that “the building rose up in stripes of varied colour” [4]. The temple-tower at Borsippa (the Birs-i-Nimrud) followed the same pattern: a square base of 272 feet, with each successive stage smaller and set back, reaching a total height of about 150 feet [1][3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is fully grounded in the sources but wrongly attributes the seven-stage planetary colour scheme to the temple of Belus at Babylon when the passages describe those colours for the temple of Nebo at Borsippa (the Birs-i-Nimrud). | completeness: The answer correctly identifies the ziggurat and affirms its stepped, multi-tiered form, fully addressing the question; extra detail about colors and specific sites does not lower the score. | attribution: The answer misattributes the specific planetary color scheme described in passage 4, which applies to the Birs-i-Nimrud, to the great temple of Belus at Babylon.

results.question: What surprise attack on the beached Athenian fleet brought the long war between Athens and Sparta to an end?
results.ideal_answer: The battle of Aegospotami (405 BC). With the Athenian crews scattered ashore in search of food, Lysander rowed across the Hellespont and caught the fleet drawn up on the open beach; all but a handful of ships (Conon escaped with nine) were taken without a fight. The loss of the fleet left Athens unable to continue the Peloponnesian War, and she surrendered the next year.
results.answer: The surprise attack was the **Battle of Aegospotami** (405 BC), in which the Spartan admiral Lysander caught the Athenian fleet of 180 ships beached and largely unmanned on an open shore at Aegospotami ("Goat's River") in the Hellespont. For four days the Athenians had sailed out to challenge Lysander, who refused battle; each day the crews returned and dispersed along the beach to find provisions, growing increasingly careless [3][1][2]. On the fifth day, Lysander's scout ships watched the Athenians disembark and then "hoist a bright shield as a signal" [2]. As soon as the signal was given, "the whole Peloponnesian squadron… rowed rapidly across the strait and found the Athenian fleet defenceless" [1]. Xenophon reports that "some of the vessels had only two out of their three banks of rowers, some only a single one, while others again were completely empty" [6]. Out of 180 Athenian triremes, only Conon's squadron of nine ships managed to escape; the remaining "one hundred and seventy-one vessels were captured by Lysander on the beach" [6]. Between three and four thousand Athenian prisoners were put to death [1]. The disaster was decisive—"a retribution for what they had themselves inflicted"—and directly led to Athens' blockade and surrender, bringing the long Peloponnesian War to an end [5].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including the battle details, ship counts, shield signal, casualties, and decisive outcome—is directly supported by the provided passages, and standard modern glosses such as "Peloponnesian War" and "Goat's River" are correct equivalents for source content. | completeness: The answer fully names the Battle of Aegospotami, describes the surprise attack on the beached Athenian fleet, and states it ended the Peloponnesian War, covering everything the question asks despite including extra detail. | attribution: The sources agree on the identity and nature of the surprise attack, and the answer correctly attributes all specific details and quotes to their respective supporting passages without misattribution.

results.question: How were the victorious Athenian commanders punished for failing to rescue survivors after the sea battle at Arginusae?
results.ideal_answer: After winning at Arginusae (406 BC) the generals were accused of failing to pick up the shipwrecked crews (they blamed a storm). The Athenian assembly, illegally trying them collectively rather than one by one, condemned the eight; the six who had returned to Athens were executed. The city soon repented of the act.
results.answer: After the battle of Arginusae, the eight Athenian generals who had been present were condemned to death and confiscation of property by the Assembly, which tried them collectively rather than individually [1]. Six of them—including Thrasyllus and Pericles, son of the great statesman—were executed; the other two avoided execution by keeping out of the way [1]. The generals had been suspended from command and summoned home to account for their failure to rescue the drowning sailors [1]. At the Assembly, Theramenes acted as their principal accuser, arguing that they could and should have saved the men on the disabled ships, and that the storm was not a sufficient excuse [6]. The generals were initially blamed, then they shifted blame onto the trierarchs, who in turn accused the generals of not issuing rescue orders until the storm made it impossible [1]. The proceedings were marked by a violation of customary legal safeguards: the Assembly condemned all the accused together instead of judging each separately, and the prytanes' opposition (except for Socrates) was overruled [1][2]. The people later repented of the execution [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including the collective death sentence and confiscation, the six executions, Theramenes's accusation, the blame-shifting, and the procedural violations involving Socrates—is directly supported by the provided source passages with no invented facts or quotes. | completeness: The answer fully covers how the commanders were punished, including their collective condemnation, execution of six, confiscation of property, and the city's repentance, and the inclusion of extra detail does not lower the score. | attribution: The sources agree and complement each other, and the answer accurately attributes all claims to the correct sources without any misattributions or un-surfaced disagreements.

results.question: How was a Roman army's overnight fortified camp laid out, with its regular streets and gates?
results.ideal_answer: On a fixed, repeatable plan. The general's tent (praetorium) was set first, then the lines of the tribunes' and legions' quarters measured off from it; the whole camp formed a square crossed by regular streets (viae) "like a town", with four gates, a market-place, and a broad clear space inside the rampart — so familiar that each soldier could find his quarters as in his own city.
results.answer:
A Roman army's overnight fortified camp was laid out as a regular square, "with streets and other constructions regularly planned like a town" [2]. The camp was divided by a grid of streets (viae) and had four gates, one on each side [5].

The general's tent (Praetorium) was placed in the center, marked by a white flag [7]. From this central square, the tents of the Tribunes were pitched in a straight line, and the legions' quarters were arranged in blocks separated by roads [9]. The main streets included the Via Principalis (running across the front of the Principia) and the Via Quintana (running at right angles to the other streets, parallel to the Tribunes' tents) [3]. The camp had four gates: the Porta Principalis Dextra and Porta Principalis Sinistra on the sides, and the Porta Decumana at the rear [1][4].

When the legions arrived, each soldier knew exactly which via and gate to use to reach his quarters, "because he knows the direction and the quarter of the town in which home lies" [7]. Josephus similarly notes that the camp was divided into streets, with four gates, and "appears to be a city built on the sudden" [5].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is supported by the provided passages: the square layout with regular streets [2], four gates [5], central Praetorium marked with a white flag [6][7][9], Tribune tents in a line and legion blocks separated by roads [8][9], Via Quintana parallel to the Tribunes' tents [3], and the named gates shown in the diagrams [1][4] are correctly glossed with their standard names and positions (Via Principalis, Porta Decumana at the rear). | completeness: The answer fully covers how the camp was laid out from the central praetorium, its grid of regular streets, and its four gates, satisfying everything the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree on the layout of the Roman camp, and the answer correctly uses bare citation markers to attribute the details without any misattribution.

results.question: What strategy of refusing open battle and wearing the invader down by delay was used against Hannibal in Italy?
results.ideal_answer: The cautious, delaying strategy of the dictator Quintus Fabius Maximus, who shadowed Hannibal from the heights, cut off his supplies and refused a pitched battle — earning the surname Cunctator ("the Delayer"/"Lingerer") and, at first, scorn at Rome, before it was recognised as the policy that saved the state.
results.answer: The strategy was the famous **Fabian strategy** (from Fabius Maximus’ epithet *Cunctator*, “the Delayer”). After the disasters of Lake Trasimene and the Trebia, the dictator Fabius Maximus deliberately avoided pitched battle with Hannibal’s superior force. He kept his army on high ground where the Carthaginian cavalry could not attack [1], shadowed the enemy closely but refused any general engagement [2], and counted on Rome’s inexhaustible reserves of men and supplies to wear Hannibal down by attrition [5]. Polybius writes that Fabius “made up his mind to incur no danger and not to risk a battle, but to make the safety of his men his first and greatest object” [9]. Plutarch states that his design was “wearing out and wasting the vigor of his arms by lapse of time, of meeting his want of resources by superior means” [1]. Cassius Dio records that because he “would not fight, but quietly accompanied him” and frustrated the enemy’s foraging, he was “even given the name of The Delayer” [4]. Livy notes that Hannibal recognized this “deliberate prudence” and tried in vain to provoke a fight [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim and quote is directly supported by the provided passages, and the conventional battle names (Lake Trasimene, Trebia) and the label 'Fabian strategy' are correct glosses of the events and tactics described in the sources. | completeness: The answer fully identifies the Fabian strategy, names Fabius Maximus Cunctator, and explains the refusal of pitched battle and war of attrition/delay, covering everything the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree on the details of the Fabian strategy, and the answer correctly attributes the complementary details to the respective authors in prose without any misattributions.

results.question: How did Hannibal's men break apart the rock blocking a pass while crossing the Alps?
results.ideal_answer: They split it by fire and vinegar: piling and burning timber against the rock to heat it, then pouring vinegar on the heated stone to make it soft and crumbling, after which they cut a way through with iron tools.
results.answer: Hannibal's men broke apart the rock by first piling up timber and setting it on fire to heat the stone, then pouring vinegar (and water, according to Appian) onto the heated rock to weaken it, and finally breaking it with iron tools. Livy writes that "having felled and lopped a number of large trees... they make a huge pile of timber; and... set fire to it, and, pouring vinegar on the heated stones, they render them soft and crumbling" and then "open a way with iron instruments through the rock" [1]. Appian adds that "he cut down and burned wood, quenched the ashes with water and vinegar, and thus rendering the rocks brittle he shattered them with iron hammers" [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is fully supported by the cited passages, including the use of fire, vinegar (and water per Appian), and iron tools, with accurate quotations and no invented content. | completeness: The answer fully describes the complete process—heating the rock with fire, pouring vinegar (and water) to weaken it, and using iron tools to break it—thereby satisfying everything the question asks. | attribution: The sources agree on the method used to break the rock, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details and quotes to Livy and Appian without any misattribution.

results.question: What secret nocturnal religious society did the Roman senate outlaw as a criminal conspiracy?
results.ideal_answer: The Bacchanalia — the secret night-rites of Bacchus. After they spread from Etruria through Italy and into Rome, the senate in 186 BC treated them as a dangerous conspiracy: thousands were tried and many executed, the meeting places demolished, and a decree forbade the rites in Rome or Italy except under strict, tiny, senate-licensed exceptions.
results.answer: The Roman senate outlawed the **Bacchanalian rites** (the nocturnal orgies of Bacchus, also referred to as the Bacchanals). Livy reports that after the consul Postumius laid the matter before the senate, they decreed "an inquiry out of the common course, concerning the Bacchanals and their nocturnal orgies" [1]. The senate forbade any initiated persons from assembling for the rites and ordered searches for the priests and participants throughout Rome and Italy [1]. Subsequently, they decreed "that no Bacchanalian rites should be celebrated in Rome or in Italy" and that all places where the Bacchanalians had held meetings were to be demolished [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, with no invented facts or unsupported details. | completeness: The answer correctly names the Bacchanalia/Bacchanalian rites and fully covers the senate's outlawing of these secret nocturnal gatherings, satisfying everything the question asks. | attribution: The source passages agree and are both from Livy, and the answer correctly attributes the information to Livy in prose without any misattribution.

results.question: What intoxicating vapor rising from a cleft in the ground was believed to put the priestess at Delphi into a prophetic frenzy?
results.ideal_answer: An exhalation rising from a deep cleft or cavern at Delphi. The Pythian priestess sat on a tripod set over the opening, breathed in the rising exhalation, and, seized by a divine frenzy, delivered the oracle's response.
results.answer: The intoxicating vapor was an **exhalation** (or **vapour**) rising from a cleft in the ground at Delphi. Strabo describes the site as "a deep hollow cavern" from which "rises up an exhalation which inspires a divine frenzy," and over this cleft the Pythian priestess sat upon a tripod to receive the vapour before prophesying [4]. Diodorus Siculus relates the discovery of the chasm: goats and a shepherd who looked into it were seized by an "enthusiasm" and "a spirit of prophecy" [1]. Pausanias similarly records that shepherds were "inspired by the vapour" and began to prophesy [3], and Samuel Dill notes that "Exhalations from secret chasms, as at Delphi" produced a "physical excitement akin to madness" [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the cited passages, with no invented facts, quotes, names, or outcomes. | completeness: The answer correctly identifies the intoxicating vapor as an exhalation or vapour rising from the cleft at Delphi and fully addresses its effect on the Pythian priestess, covering everything the question asks without omitting any required element. | attribution: The sources agree on the presence of an intoxicating vapor or exhalation at Delphi, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details to each respective source in prose without any misattributions.

results.question: What Greek coastal city was swallowed by the sea after an earthquake, leaving its ruins visible beneath the water?
results.ideal_answer: Helike, in Achaea. One winter night (373 BC) a violent earthquake struck and the sea simultaneously rushed in, submerging the city and its people; for a long time afterwards its ruins could be seen under the water, growing fainter as the sea wore them away.
results.answer: The city was **Helice** (also spelled Helike), an Achaean city on the Corinthian Gulf. According to Strabo, "Helice was overwhelmed by the waves two years before the battle of Leuctra" [4], and he notes that "of Bura and Helice, one has been swallowed by an earthquake, the other covered by the waves" [1]. Diodorus Siculus records that "two cities of Achaia, one called Helice, and the other Bura, chiefly suffered by this sad accident" — the sea "swallowed up them and their houses together" after an earthquake struck at night [2]. Pausanias provides the most detailed account: "the sea encroached over much of the district and quite flooded Helice with water" and states that the "ruins of Helice" were still visible underwater, though "effaced by the action of the sea" over time [3]. He also notes that the earthquake was divine vengeance from Poseidon after the Achaeans killed suppliants at his temple there [5].

results.question: What great temple on the Capitol was burned down during the street fighting in Rome in the civil war of AD 69?
results.ideal_answer: The temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus (the Capitol) was burned to the ground during the fighting between the Vitellians and the supporters of Vespasian. Tacitus calls it the most deplorable disgrace to befall Rome since the city's foundation — the seat of Jupiter, untouched by Porsenna or the Gauls, openly besieged and set on fire.
results.answer: The temple burned down during the street fighting in Rome in AD 69 was the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus on the Capitoline Hill — the great temple of the Capitol. Tacitus describes it as "the seat of Jupiter Supremely Good and Great" and records that "the Capitol, with its gates shut, neither defended by friends, nor spoiled by a foe, was burnt to the ground" during the clash between Vitellian and Flavian forces [2]. He calls the event "the most deplorable and disgraceful event that had happened to the Commonwealth of Rome since the foundation of the city" [1]. Plutarch likewise confirms that the temple was "demolished in the Vitellian sedition" [3].

results.question: How did the soldiers turn against the emperor Galba and kill him in the forum?
results.ideal_answer: Bought over by Otho's promises, the praetorians abandoned Galba; as he was carried into the Forum his bearers panicked and tipped him from his litter near the Lacus Curtius, where the soldiers cut him down — most reports saying a legionary named Camurius severed his throat — and then beheaded and mutilated the old emperor, consul and high priest, before the senators and crowd.
results.answer: The soldiers turned against Galba after being alienated by his stinginess and won over by Otho. When the armed force approached, the standard‑bearer of Galba’s escort “tore off and dashed upon the ground Galba’s effigy,” signalling the troops’ switch to Otho [3]. Galba was lured from the palace by false reports that the mutiny was over; once in the Forum he was abandoned by his attendants [4]. In the Forum “horsemen and footsoldiers met him and then and there cut down … the old man” [2]. Tacitus reports that Galba was thrown from his litter near the Lacus Curtius and that a soldier of the 15th legion “completely severed his throat by treading his sword down upon it”; afterwards the rest “foully mutilated his arms and legs … even on the headless trunk” [3]. Cassius Dio adds that he was “struck by a javelin hurled into the very chair in which he was being carried,” his head was cut off and stuck on a pole [2]. Suetonius simply states that the knights appointed to dispatch him “galloping up to him … they put him to death” in the Forum [4]. Thus the soldiers’ defection, fueled by resentment over withheld donatives [5], culminated in the violent and public execution of the emperor.

results.question: On what furniture did well-to-do Romans recline while eating the main meal of the day?
results.ideal_answer: They reclined on couches (lecti) rather than sitting. The cena (dinner) was the chief meal of the day, and after the Punic wars special dining rooms — the triclinia, named for the three couches arranged around the table — were built for the comfort of reclining guests, with no more than three to a couch as good manners.
results.answer: Well-to-do Romans reclined on couches called **lecti** (singular *lectus*) while eating the main meal (*cena*). These were arranged in a special dining-room called a *triclinium*: three *lecti* were placed around a central table (*mensa*), each couch accommodating up to three guests [2][1]. The host occupied a specific couch, and the most important guest was given the *lectus consularis* [1]. In earlier times, the husband reclined on a *lectus* while his wife sat beside him and children sat on stools [3].

results.question: How does Plutarch portray Antony's ruin through Cleopatra?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch frames Cleopatra as "the last and crowning mischief" that befell Antony — a love that awakened his dormant passions and corrupted what goodness and judgment remained. She kept him captive through ceaseless charm (dicing, drinking, hunting with him, never letting him escape by day or night); the passion rekindled "like Plato's restive horse" when he returned east and showered her with provinces; and after Actium it left him a misanthrope playing Timon of Athens in a sea-hut, before the final catastrophe in Alexandria.
results.answer:
Plutarch portrays Antony's ruin through Cleopatra as a progressive moral and psychological collapse, in which she is the ultimate agent of his undoing. He introduces her as "the last and crowning mischief that could befall him" — a love that "awaken and kindle to fury passions that as yet lay still and dormant in his nature, and to stifle and finally corrupt any elements that yet made resistance in him, of goodness and a sound judgment" [1].

From this point, Plutarch traces a steady erosion of Antony's character and position:

- **Distraction from duty**: Cleopatra mockingly tells him, "Leave... the fishing-rod, general, to us poor sovereigns of Pharos and Canopus; your game is cities, provinces, and kingdoms" [10], implying he is neglecting his real responsibilities for trivialities with her.
- **Public humiliation**: Antony is reported to have "risen up and rubbed her feet" at a banquet, "suffered the Ephesians to salute her as their queen," and left the tribunal mid-pleading to follow her chair [4]. His friends are driven away by "the insolent usage they had from Cleopatra's flatterers" [4].
- **Territorial prodigality**: He gives her vast territories — "Phoenicia, Coele-Syria, Cyprus, great part of Cilicia, that side of Judaea which produces balm, that part of Arabia where the Nabathaeans extend to the outer sea" — gifts that "much displeased the Romans" [2].
- **Emotional manipulation**: Cleopatra's creatures "so melted and unmanned him" that he abandons his Median expedition, "fully believing she would die if he forsook her" [3].
- **The will and burial**: Antony's will orders that even if he died in Rome, his body should be sent to Cleopatra at Alexandria — a detail Caesar uses to inflame public opinion against him [6].

The climax is Actium. When Cleopatra's sixty ships flee, Antony "showed to all the world that he was no longer actuated by the thoughts and motives of a commander or a man, or indeed by his own judgment at all" — he abandons his fighting men and follows her, "as if he had been born part of her, and must move with her wheresoever she went" [5]. Plutarch explicitly calls her "she that had so well begun his ruin and would hereafter accomplish it" [5].

After the defeat, Antony cries out "that Cleopatra had betrayed him to the enemies he had made for her sake" [7]. She then tricks him into believing she is dead; he stabs himself, saying, "it distresses me that so great a general should be found of a tardier courage than a woman" [7], and dies in her arms at her monument.

In his final comparison with Demetrius, Plutarch makes the moral explicit: "Antony... was over and over again disarmed by Cleopatra, and beguiled away, while great actions and enterprises of the first necessity fell, as it were, from his hands, to go with her to the seashore of Canopus and Taphosiris, and play about. And in the end, like another Paris, he left the battle to fly to her arms; or rather, to say the truth, Paris fled when he was already beaten; Antony fled first, and, to follow Cleopatra, abandoned his victory" [8]. The entire arc is one of a great commander progressively stripped of his judgment, his friends, his army, and finally his life — all through his enslavement to Cleopatra.

results.question: How does Arrian portray Alexander's bond with his soldiers?
results.ideal_answer: As intense mutual devotion that survived even open conflict. At the Hyphasis the army's silent, grieved resistance forced the invincible king to turn back; when he nearly died among the Malli, the troops wept, cheered, and crowded to touch his hands, knees, and clothes as he showed himself alive; and at Opis the mutiny dissolved when he called every Macedonian his kinsman, sealing the reconciliation with kisses and a vast common banquet.
results.answer: Arrian portrays Alexander's bond with his soldiers as one of intense mutual affection and shared sacrifice, though not without moments of tension. Alexander himself emphasizes that he has never asked his men to endure hardships he did not share: he claims that no part of his body "in front at any rate" is free from wounds, and lists injuries from sword, arrows, and missiles, insisting he still leads them as conquerors [1]. He also asserts that he sleeps less and eats worse than they do, remaining awake to watch over them [2]. When Alexander is critically wounded among the Mallians and a false report of his death spreads, the entire army erupts in lamentation, then becomes spiritless and perplexed about who could lead them home safely, feeling "bereft of Alexander" and surrounded by impassable rivers and hostile nations [3]. The depth of feeling is confirmed at his recovery: as his ship approaches, the men are at first incredulous, but when he stretches out his hand they raise a cheer, many shed "involuntary tears at the unexpected sight," and when he mounts his horse the whole army echoes with loud clapping; soldiers crowd to touch his hands, knees, or clothes, and throw garlands and flowers upon him [4]. In battle, the bond is shown by the Macedonians' desperate scramble to reach their fallen king, breaking ladders in their fear for his safety and fighting fiercely around his body, with men like Peucestas and Leonnatus shielding him until they are themselves wounded [6]. There is also strain: at Opis the Macedonians feel insulted by his Persianizing policies and his announced discharge of veterans, and Alexander has thirteen ringleaders arrested and executed [5]; yet when they later throw down their weapons and beg for his pity, Alexander is moved to tears, and a poignant reconciliation follows as he weeps and the soldiers continue their importunate entreaties, led by the aged cavalry captain Callines [7]. Overall, Arrian depicts a bond forged in common danger, marked by fierce loyalty and deep emotion on both sides, and capable of surviving even serious estrangement.

results.question: How does Livy portray the struggle between patricians and plebeians?
results.ideal_answer: As the defining, recurring affliction of the early Republic — "the discord among the several orders... the bane of this city." Driven by debt and oppression, the commons twice seceded (the first ended by Menenius Agrippa's fable of the belly and the members, the second on the Sacred Mount against the decemvirs); each crisis extracted new concessions — tribunes, appeal, laws — while Livy moralizes on how hard moderation is when each side's pursuit of power or liberty makes it an object of dread to the other.
results.answer:
Livy portrays the struggle between patricians and plebeians as a protracted and often bitter conflict that threatened the very survival of the Roman state, yet one in which compromise was periodically achieved through the intervention of conciliatory figures and the practical recognition that the two orders depended on each other. He embeds his analysis in dramatic set-pieces—speeches, fables, and exemplary deaths—that often tilt the reader’s sympathy in multiple directions, though a recurring note is the weariness of the patricians under what they see as endless plebeian demands.

The most famous emblem of this struggle appears in the fable of the belly and the limbs, delivered by Menenius Agrippa during the first secession of the plebeians to the Sacred Mount. Livy recounts how the plebeians, resenting that “every thing was procured for the belly by their care, labour, and service,” conspired to starve it, only to find that “the members themselves and the entire body were reduced to the last degree of emaciation” [3]. The fable’s moral—that the patrician senate, like the belly, receives nourishment but returns it as blood distributed to all—persuaded the commons to negotiate. The result was the creation of the tribunes of the plebs, magistrates with “inviolable privileges” who could bring assistance against the consuls [3]. Agrippa himself is celebrated as “the mediator and umpire in restoring concord among his countrymen,” a man beloved by both orders, and Livy notes with pathos that the people paid for his funeral by contributions when he died in poverty [4].

Yet Livy also shows how concord was fragile and how each side manoeuvred against the other. After the banishment of the patrician Caeso, the younger patricians adopted a strategy of alternating intimidation and charm: on days when the tribunes pressed an unpopular law, they would attack with a “numerous body of clients”; on other days they would “salute the plebeians courteously, enter into conversation, and invite them home.” Livy remarks that “by soothing and managing the commons they gradually rendered them tractable” and the law was evaded for a whole year [2].

Patrician resentment at tribunician power surfaces repeatedly in Livy’s speeches. Consuls facing prosecution warn the younger nobles that “the consular fasces, the praetexta and curule chair” are “nothing else than the decorations of a funeral,” and that the consul must act “at the nod and command of the tribune … as if he were a tribune’s beadle.” They invoke the fates of Coriolanus and the younger Menenius—the son of Agrippa—who, though defended by the senate, was crushed by the tribunes and fined, dying “of the disgrace, and … anguish of mind” [6][5]. Livy presents the tribunes’ prosecution of the younger Menenius as an instance of their “insolence,” especially since it came despite the memory of his father’s service [5].

Another patrician spokesman, late in the conflict, catalogues the concessions already made: “the aid of tribunes, right of appeal to the people, the acts of the commons made binding on the patricians under the pretext of equalizing the laws, the subversion of our privileges.” He then laments, “What termination is there to be to our dissensions? when shall it be allowed us to have a united city? when to have one common country?” [1]. The speech frames the plebeians as perpetual aggressors—seizing the Aventine and the Sacred Mount, taking up arms against the patricians, yet failing to defend the city against foreign enemies.

Throughout, Livy’s narrative acknowledges the real economic distress and political exclusion that drove the plebeians—famine after the secession, debt, and the harshness of patrician creditors—but his most vivid and extended passages tend to give voice to patrician exasperation and to celebrate the heroic mediators (like Agrippa) who temporarily restored harmony. The struggle is presented less as a story of plebeian triumph than as a recurring cycle of grievance, concession, and resentment, in which the survival of the state depended on the willingness of both sides to pull back from the brink.

results.question: How did the Romans become a naval power during the First Punic War, according to Polybius?
results.ideal_answer: Through what Polybius calls extraordinary audacity. Having never thought of naval war and lacking even the knowledge to build quinqueremes, they used a wrecked Carthaginian ship as their model, trained rowers on benches set up on dry land, and invented the "crow" boarding-bridge that turned sea battles into land fights (first proven at Mylae); after catastrophic storm losses they persisted, rebuilt with lighter ships and trained crews, and won the decisive battle of Aegusa.
results.answer:
According to Polybius, the Romans became a naval power during the First Punic War through a combination of audacity, a stroke of luck, and systematic preparation.

The war had been dragging on, and the Romans saw that "while Italy was repeatedly ravaged by the naval force, Libya remained permanently uninjured" — so they resolved to meet the Carthaginians at sea [1]. They decided to build a fleet of one hundred quinqueremes and twenty triremes, but faced a major obstacle: their shipbuilders "were entirely unacquainted with the construction of quinqueremes, because no one in Italy had at that time employed vessels of that description" [1].

Their breakthrough came by chance. Earlier, when the Romans were first ferrying troops to Messene using borrowed ships from Tarentum, Locri, Elea, and Neapolis, a Carthaginian decked vessel attacked them in the Strait, "charged so furiously that it ran aground, and falling into the hands of the Romans served them as a model on which they constructed their whole fleet" [2]. Polybius emphasises that without this captured ship, "they would have been completely hindered from carrying out their design by want of constructive knowledge" [2].

While the ships were being built, the Romans also tackled the problem of untrained crews. They "taught them to row on dry land" by making men sit on rowers' benches arranged in the same order as on a real vessel, with a Celeustes (time-keeper) in the middle to coordinate their strokes — "to get back and draw in their hands all together in time, and then to swing forward and throw them out again" [2]. Once the ships were completed, the crews had only "a brief preliminary practice of real sea-rowing" before setting out [2].

Polybius presents this as a remarkable feat of daring: the Romans, with no prior experience of naval warfare, "without so much as a preliminary trial, took upon themselves there and then to meet the Carthaginians at sea, on which they had for generations held undisputed supremacy" [1].

results.question: How does Thucydides describe the moral collapse caused by civil war?
results.ideal_answer: In his analysis of the Corcyra revolution, Thucydides argues that war is "a rough master" that levels men's characters to their fortunes. Words lost their ordinary meanings (recklessness became courage, moderation cowardice); religion was honored by neither side while fair phrases served guilty ends; the moderates perished between the factions; the "ancient simplicity" of honor was laughed out of existence and no man trusted his fellow. The concrete horror at Corcyra — fathers killing sons, suppliants slain at altars — set the pattern the whole Hellenic world then exceeded.
results.answer:
Thucydides describes the moral collapse caused by civil war — using the Corcyraean stasis as his prime example — as a complete inversion of values, language, and human decency that spread across the entire Hellenic world.

**The immediate horrors at Corcyra:** During the revolution, death "raged in every shape" — "sons were killed by their fathers, and suppliants dragged from the altar or slain upon it; while some were even walled up in the temple of Dionysus and died there" [3]. Thucydides notes this was "one of the first to occur" and that later "the whole Hellenic world was convulsed" [3].

**The general analysis of revolution's effects:** He observes that "in peace and prosperity, states and individuals have better sentiments" but "war takes away the easy supply of daily wants, and so proves a rough master, that brings most men's characters to a level with their fortunes" [2].

**The perversion of language:** The most famous passage is his account of how "Words had to change their ordinary meaning and to take that which was now given them" [2]. Specifically:
- "Reckless audacity came to be considered the courage of a loyal ally; prudent hesitation, specious cowardice; moderation was held to be a cloak for unmanliness; ability to see all sides of a question, inaptness to act on any" [2].
- "Frantic violence became the attribute of manliness; cautious plotting, a justifiable means of self-defence" [c1215].
- "The advocate of extreme measures was always trustworthy; his opponent a man to be suspected" [c1215].

**The breakdown of social bonds:** "Blood became a weaker tie than party, from the superior readiness of those united by the latter to dare everything without reserve" [c1215]. Members' confidence in each other "rested less on any religious sanction than upon complicity in crime" [c1215]. "Revenge also was held of more account than self-preservation" [c1215]. Oaths of reconciliation "only held good so long as no other weapon was at hand" — when opportunity came, the first to seize it "thought this perfidious vengeance sweeter than an open one" [c1215].

**The root cause:** "The cause of all these evils was the lust for power arising from greed and ambition; and from these passions proceeded the violence of parties once engaged in contention" [c1215]. The leaders in the cities "each provided with the fairest professions" — on one side "the cry of political equality of the people," on the other "of a moderate aristocracy" — but in reality "sought prizes for themselves in those public interests which they pretended to cherish" [4].

**The collapse of religion and trust:** "Religion was in honour with neither party; but the use of fair phrases to arrive at guilty ends was in high reputation" [4]. "The ancient simplicity into which honour so largely entered was laughed down and disappeared; and society became divided into camps in which no man trusted his fellow" [4]. "Neither promise to be depended upon, nor oath that could command respect" remained [4].

**The psychology of the actors:** In this contest "the blunter wits were most successful" — they feared being out-debated by cleverer opponents and "so at once boldly had recourse to action," while their more sophisticated adversaries "arrogantly thinking that they should know in time" often "fell victims to their want of precaution" [4].

**The role of envy:** Thucydides adds that "revenge would not have been set above religion, and gain above justice, had it not been for the fatal power of envy" [1]. Men are "readier to call rogues clever than simpletons honest, and are as ashamed of being the second as they are proud of being the first" [c1215].

results.question: How does Thucydides contrast the Athenian and Spartan national characters?
results.ideal_answer: Largely through speeches. The Corinthians tell Sparta that the Athenians are addicted to innovation, swift in conception and execution, adventurous beyond their power, never at home — "born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" — while the Spartans keep what they have, invent nothing, and never go far enough. Archidamus answers by defending Spartan slowness as disciplined wisdom: warlike from self-control and honor, wise because educated too little to despise the laws.
results.answer:
Thucydides presents the contrast most explicitly through the Corinthians' speech at Sparta, where they declare that the Spartans have little perception of "the great contrast between the two national characters" [1]. The Athenians are "addicted to innovation, and their designs are characterized by swiftness alike in conception and execution," while the Spartans have "a genius for keeping what you have got, accompanied by a total want of invention, and when forced to act you never go far enough" [1]. The Corinthians go on to elaborate a series of antitheses: Athenians are "adventurous beyond their power, and daring beyond their judgment," whereas Spartans "attempt less than is justified by your power, to mistrust even what is sanctioned by your judgment" [1]; Athenians display "promptitude" against Spartan "procrastination"; "they are never at home, you are never from it: for they hope by their absence to extend their acquisitions, you fear by your advance to endanger what you have left behind" [1]; and the Athenians "toil on in trouble and danger all the days of their life," such that "one might truly say that they were born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" [2].

Archidamus, the Spartan king, acknowledges and re-frames the same contrast. He defends the Spartan traits that are "most assailed by their criticism" — "slowness and procrastination" — as "really nothing but a wise moderation" [3]. He argues that Spartans are "both warlike and wise" because their self-control breeds honour and bravery, and they are educated with "too severe a self-control to disobey the laws" and are "taught to consider that the schemes of our enemies are not dissimilar to our own, and that the freaks of chance are not determinable by calculation" [5]. Where Athenians trust in dash and enterprise, Spartans base their preparations "on the assumption that his plans are good" — trusting not in enemy blunders but "on the soundness of our provisions" [5].

Thucydides himself later reflects that this characterological contrast had strategic consequences: "the slowness and want of energy of the Lacedaemonians as contrasted with the dash and enterprise of their opponents, proved of the greatest service, especially to a maritime empire like Athens" [4].

results.question: What does Herodotus find most remarkable about Babylon?
results.ideal_answer: Its unmatched magnificence and its customs. He marvels at the city's scale — the deep moat and walls fifty royal cubits thick and two hundred high, and the temple of Zeus Belos with its eight stacked towers — and singles out two customs: the annual bride auction, where money from the beautiful maidens dowered the plain ones (in his opinion their wisest), and the carrying of the sick into the marketplace, since they use no physicians and every passer-by must offer advice.
results.answer: Herodotus states that, after the city of Babylon itself, the greatest marvel in the land is the Babylonians' boats. He writes: "But the greatest marvel of all the things in the land after the city itself, to my mind is this which I am about to tell" [1]. The boats that travel down the Euphrates to Babylon are round, made of leather stretched over willow ribs, and shaped "like a shield" rather than pointed at either end — essentially coracles. They are then packed with straw and cargo and floated downstream [1].

results.question: How does Suetonius chart Caligula's descent from popular prince to monster?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius structures the Life around an explicit pivot: "Thus far we have spoken of him as a prince. What remains to be said of him, bespeaks him rather a monster than a man." His accession brought genuine joy — the soldiers' beloved camp-child, heir to Germanicus' virtues — though Tiberius had already predicted he was "rearing a hydra for the people of Rome." After the pivot comes the monster: claiming divine majesty, beheading the gods' statues to mount his own head, the temple to his own divinity, and cruelties without limit.
results.answer:
Suetonius charts Caligula’s descent from popular prince to monster by first showing the overwhelming affection he enjoyed at the start of his reign and then cataloguing the atrocities that later characterised him, explicitly noting a sudden change between the two phases.

At his accession, Caligula was greeted with universal joy. The Roman people, remembering his father Germanicus, hailed him with endearing nicknames such as “their star,” “their chick,” “their pretty puppet,” and “bantling” [1]. Within three months of his entering Rome, over 160,000 victims were said to have been sacrificed in thanksgiving, and when he fell ill citizens vowed to risk their lives for his recovery [4]. Suetonius also details his early popular acts: he honoured his family, restored exiles, granted an amnesty for past offenses, and performed public acts of filial piety [3].

Later, Suetonius describes a starkly different emperor. Caligula’s cruelty becomes relentless: he fed criminals to wild beasts to save on the cost of meat, branded high-ranking men with hot irons, compelled parents to witness the execution of their sons, and ordered victims struck with light repeated blows so that they might “feel himself die” [6][10]. Even at leisure his savagery did not relent – at a banquet he threw guests into the sea, and he stabbed a gladiator who yielded to him [7]. His language matched his deeds, boasting of his “inflexible rigour” (adiatrepsia), reminding his grandmother that “all things are lawful for me,” and famously wishing the Roman people had a single neck [8][10].

Suetonius marks the pivot explicitly: “The commencement, however, of his reign was such as by no means prognosticated its subsequent transition,” and speaks of “the sudden change of his conduct, the astonishing mixture of imbecility and presumption, of moral turpitude and frantic extravagance” [2]. Thus the biography juxtaposes the loved prince of the early chapters with the monster of the later ones, framing the descent as an abrupt and inexplicable transformation.

results.question: How does Tacitus characterize German marriage and the position of women?
results.ideal_answer: With pointed admiration. The marriage gifts — oxen, a bridled horse, shield, spear, and sword — admonish the wife that she comes as a partner in toils and dangers, "to suffer and to dare equally with him, in peace and in war... thus she is to live; thus to die." The Germans live "fenced around with chastity": adultery is extremely rare and instantly punished, no one calls seduction the way of the world, and in the strictest tribes a woman takes one husband "as one body and one life."
results.answer: Tacitus characterizes German marriage as a solemn and equal partnership. In the marriage ceremony, the husband does not give frivolous gifts but rather "oxen, a caparisoned steed, a shield, spear, and sword," and the wife in turn presents arms to her husband — this is viewed as "the firmest bond of union" and the "conjugal deities" [1]. By this ritual the woman is "admonished ... that she comes to her husband as a partner in toils and dangers; to suffer and to dare equally with him, in peace and in war" [1]. German women live "fenced around with chastity," uncorrupted by seductive spectacles or secret correspondence; adultery is "extremely rare" and is punished instantly by the husband, who cuts off the offender's hair, strips her, expels her from his house, and drives her through the village with stripes [1]. No indulgence is shown to a prostitute: "Neither beauty, youth, nor riches can procure her a husband" [1]. In some states, only virgins marry, so that a wife takes "one husband as one body and one life" and loves him as her marriage itself [1]. Further, Tacitus notes that virgins are not hurried into marriage but wait for equal maturity, so "the sexes unite equally matched and robust; and the children inherit the vigor of their parents" [2]. Every mother suckles her own children rather than handing them to servants or nurses [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including the marriage gifts, reciprocal armament, partnership in toils and dangers, chastity, adultery punishments, and child-rearing practices—is directly supported by the cited passages with accurate quotations and fair paraphrases. | completeness: The answer thoroughly addresses both German marriage and the position of women, covering equal partnership, marriage gifts, shared duties, chastity, adultery, monogamy, and maternal roles without omitting any part of the question. | attribution: The passages agree as they are consecutive excerpts from the same source, and the answer correctly attributes the information to Tacitus with accurate citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Suetonius portray the character of Tiberius?
results.ideal_answer: As a dissimulating tyrant whose vices, long imperfectly concealed, flowered once he withdrew to Capri. Even Augustus reportedly pitied the Roman people for being "ground by the jaws of such a slow devourer." The soldiers' nickname Biberius marked his drunkenness from youth; at Capri, "remote from the observation of the people of Rome, he abandoned himself to all the vicious propensities which he had long but imperfectly concealed"; and the translator's verdict is of an "execrable tyrant" whose indiscriminate cruelty spared neither age nor sex.
results.answer: Suetonius portrays Tiberius as a man of innate cruelty and moroseness who long concealed his true nature behind a facade of austere virtue. From boyhood he displayed a “cruel and sullen temper” that his rhetoric master likened to “Mud mixed with blood” [4]. As emperor he at first affected modesty, but once freed of the restraints of his mother and Sejanus he “abandoned himself to all the vicious propensities which he had long but imperfectly concealed” [5]. These included excessive drinking — he was nicknamed “Biberius” — and all‑night feasts with naked girls; he promoted drinking companions to high office [5]. After Sejanus’ death he became “cruel in the extreme” [3], with daily executions even on holidays, the killing of entire families, and the suppression of any critical writing [3]. He devised ingenious tortures: after learning of his son Drusus’ murder he tortured and executed indiscriminately, invented a torment that tied a victim’s limbs with harp‑strings while forcing them to drink, and, on Capri, had condemned men beaten by sailors with poles and oars before being thrown from a cliff [1]. His pettiness appeared in the abuse of a fisherman whose face he lacerated with a fish and a crab for approaching him unexpectedly [2]. Suetonius sums him up as having “from his earliest years, a moroseness of disposition, which counterfeited the appearance of austere virtue,” and in his later years he “launched forth into excesses, of a kind the most unnatural and most detestable” [7]. Overall, Suetonius’ Tiberius is a deeply dissimulating tyrant — cruel, drunken, lecherous, and consumed by fear and suspicion.

results.question: How does Josephus portray the factional strife among the Jews that doomed Jerusalem?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus presents the fall of Jerusalem as a self-inflicted catastrophe of civil war within the walls. The zealots fractured into rival factions — Eleazar son of Simon making "the first separation of the zealots from the people" and seizing the inner temple, against John of Gischala and, in the city, Simon — until there were "three treacherous factions" fighting one another even as Titus besieged them. In that warfare they burned the storehouses, so that almost all the corn "which would have been sufficient for a siege of many years" was destroyed and the people were "taken by the means of the famine" their own leaders had prepared. The common people were "their prey on both sides," and Josephus moralizes that no age ever bred "a generation more fruitful in wickedness" — the Jews destroyed themselves.
results.answer: Josephus portrays the factional strife as a self‑destructive madness that doomed Jerusalem from within, even before the Romans breached the walls. He emphasizes that the sedition split into three factions—those of Eleazar, John, and Simon—who fought each other relentlessly. While the Romans were still outside, these factions burned the city’s stores of grain: “they set on fire those houses that were full of corn … as if they had, on purpose, done it to serve the Romans … cutting off the nerves of their own power” [1]. Consequently, “they were taken by the means of the famine, which it was impossible they should have been, unless they had thus prepared the way for it by this procedure” [1]. Josephus describes Eleazar’s initial split from John as driven by ambition: “he being desirous of gaining the entire power and dominion to himself, revolted from John” [2]. He sums up the whole convulsion with a grim metaphor: the sedition was “like a wild beast grown mad, which, for want of food from abroad, fell now upon eating its own flesh,” and he calls the three‑way partition “the effect of Divine justice” [3]. Thus, in Josephus’s account, the Jews’ internal hatreds, not Roman might, were the true engine of Jerusalem’s destruction.

results.question: How does Josephus characterize the reign of Herod the Great?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus draws Herod as a king of dazzling success abroad and monstrous cruelty at home. He stood so high in Rome's favour that "he arrived at that pitch of felicity" of being, after Agrippa, the man Caesar valued most, and he crowned his reign with vast building works, "undertaking a very great work, that is, to build of himself the temple of God," alongside Caesarea and his fortresses. Yet the suspicion that secured his throne consumed his own household: he had his beloved wife tried and executed — "thus was Mariamne led to execution" — and later his sons. His end matched his cruelty: a loathsome disease in which "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," which Josephus reads as divine punishment.
results.answer:
Josephus characterizes Herod the Great as a ruler of striking contradictions, whose reign combined immense magnificence with extreme cruelty. He explicitly states that those who observe Herod's public benefactions are forced to confess "he had a nature vastly beneficent," yet when one considers his punishments and injuries even to his own family, one must allow "he was brutish, and a stranger to all humanity" [1]. Josephus himself explains that both tendencies sprang from a single cause: "being a man ambitious of honor, and quite overcome by that passion," Herod spent lavishly to secure a lasting reputation, and because "his expenses were beyond his abilities, he was necessitated to be harsh to his subjects" [1]. 

This ambition drove Herod to magnificent building projects — he rebuilt the temple in Jerusalem as "the most glorious of all his actions" [6], erected cities like Caesarea, and showered gifts on cities across Syria, Greece, and the Olympic games [5]. Yet these same ambitions led him to oppress his own people: he imposed heavy taxes, set spies everywhere, forbade private gatherings, and executed any who resisted his rule [4]. He transgressed Jewish customs and laws, though he excused such innovations as done to please Caesar and the Romans, while Josephus insists the real motive was "to leave great monuments of his government to posterity" [2]. 

In sum, Josephus portrays Herod's reign as a blend of glorious public works and harsh, often barbaric, tyranny — a reign that left the nation impoverished and filled with fear, even as it was adorned with splendid buildings and spectacles.

results.question: How does Tacitus portray the role of the armies in making and unmaking emperors in the civil war?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus makes the legions the true power in the state. The convulsion of 69 laid bare "that secret of the empire, that emperors could be made elsewhere than at Rome" — sovereignty now belonged to whichever army would proclaim its general. That loyalty was bought: the city soldiery turned on Nero and were inclined to change "when they found that the donative promised in Galba's name was withheld," and under each claimant discipline dissolved — "Much however as Vitellius indulged his generals, his soldiers enjoyed yet greater licence." The praetorians embodied the menace, bursting in arms upon Otho's banquet and threatening the Senate. Throughout, emperors are made and unmade by the greed, licence, and violence of the soldiery.
results.answer:
Tacitus portrays the armies as the decisive force that both made and unmade emperors during the civil war of AD 69, emphasizing their power, fickleness, and susceptibility to bribes. 

- In Lower Germany, the legions swore allegiance to Galba only after "long delay" and with "absolute silence" from most troops, while the 1st and 5th legions even "threw stones at the images of Galba" [5]. Within days, the soldiers of Upper Germany had broken those same images and, "to seize the doubtful fortune of the hour," resolved to offer an emperor—Vitellius [1]. Fabius Valens then formally saluted Vitellius as Emperor with the cavalry, and all the legions followed with "prodigious zeal" [4].

- The army's power is stated explicitly in a speech Tacitus assigns to Mucianus: "That an Emperor can be created by the army, Vitellius is himself a proof" [6]. This maxim is borne out repeatedly. Galba, addressing the troops in person, failed to quiet the mutiny because he offered no donative; Tacitus remarks that "he was ruined by his old-fashioned inflexibility" and that even "the very smallest liberality" might have won them over [3]. 

- Otho's coup is shown as the work of common soldiers: "Thus two soldiers from the ranks undertook to transfer the Empire of Rome, and actually transferred it" [8]. Later, Vespasian's elevation likewise begins when a few soldiers spontaneously salute him as Emperor outside his chamber, and the movement then sweeps through the eastern legions [2]. 

- Tacitus further illustrates the troops' lawless independence when they murder their own commander, Hordeonius Flaccus, dragging him from bed and killing him after a drunken night, and attempt to do the same to Vocula [7]. Throughout the Histories, Tacitus depicts the armies not as loyal instruments of the state but as the ultimate arbiters of imperial power, whose support could be bought, whose loyalties were fleeting, and who repeatedly unmade one emperor only to create another.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including the quotations, the sequence of mutinies, the murders of commanders, and the interpretation of armies as kingmakers—is directly supported by the provided passages, with AD 69 being a correct standard historical gloss. | completeness: The answer fully addresses how Tacitus portrays armies as the decisive force in both making and unmaking emperors, covering bought loyalty, mutiny, fickleness, and specific examples from the civil war. | attribution: All source passages are from the same author (Tacitus) and agree on the theme, and the answer accurately summarizes and cites the passages without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Xenophon portray the rule and downfall of the Thirty Tyrants at Athens?
results.ideal_answer: Xenophon shows the Thirty beginning with popular approval and ending in bloody tyranny and overthrow. At first they prosecuted the informers and base men of the democracy, and "the new senate gladly recorded its vote of condemnation against them"; but they soon turned to plunder and murder, passing a resolution "empowering each of the committee to seize on one of the resident aliens apiece, to put his victim to death, and to confiscate his property." Their rule grew so murderous that in eight months they had "slain almost more men than the Peloponnesians together in ten years of warfare." When Theramenes resisted Critias he was struck off the roll and "dragged through the Agora" to his death. Finally Thrasybulus and the exiles from Phyle prevailed, and the Athenians "reappointed the several magistrates; the constitution began to work afresh, and civic life was recommenced."
results.answer: Xenophon portrays the rule of the Thirty Tyrants as a descent into lawless tyranny. He notes that after the Thirty appointed a senate and magistracies arbitrarily, they first prosecuted "informers" under the democracy to public approval, but soon sought absolute control. They secured a Spartan garrison under Callibius, then used it to "lay hands on whom they would", no longer only base folk but also prominent citizens who might oppose them [5]. The narrative highlights internal conflict: Critias, eager for "wholesale carnage", clashed with Theramenes, who argued it was unjust to kill those who had merely been honoured under the democracy [2]. When the Thirty needed money for their guards, they each seized a resident alien to execute and confiscate property; Theramenes' refusal led Critias to engineer his condemnation by the senate [3]. After the armed removal of Theramenes from the altar and his execution by hemlock [1][8], the Thirty "feeling that they had it in their power to play the tyrant without fear", issued an order excluding from the city all not on the list of Three Thousand, and drove many into exile [10].\n\nThe downfall begins with Thrasybulus and about seventy followers seizing the fortress of Phyle. The Thirty's attempt to blockade it fails due to snow [10]. In the subsequent battle, Critias and many of the Thirty are killed; the herald Cleocritus appeals to the city troops to cease obeying "these most unhallowed Thirty, who for the sake of private gain have in eight months slain almost more men than the Peloponnesians together in ten years of warfare" [9]. The Three Thousand, now divided, depose the Thirty and elect a new government [7]. Finally, the Spartan king Pausanias, after a skirmish with the men of Piraeus, secretly fosters reconciliation and, with fifteen commissioners, imposes a settlement: a general peace allowing all to return home except the Thirty, the Eleven, and the Ten governors of Piraeus, with a proviso enabling city party members fearing reprisals to reside in Eleusis [12][11]. Xenophon ends with the restoration of democracy and the oath "We will remember past offences no more", remarking that the two parties lived amicably thereafter and the democracy kept its oaths [13]. Thus Xenophon's account depicts the Thirty's rule as a tyrannical, bloody interlude that collapsed under military defeat, internal discord, and external Spartan mediation, leading to a remarkably durable reconciliation.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The summary is extensively grounded, but it invents the claim that 'many of the Thirty' were killed in the battle at Phyle, whereas the source explicitly names only two of the Thirty (Critias and Hippomachus) among the fallen, along with Charmides (who was an archon, not one of the Thirty) and about seventy other combatants. | completeness: The answer comprehensively addresses both the rule and downfall of the Thirty Tyrants as portrayed by Xenophon, covering their initial popular measures, descent into murderous tyranny, internal conflict, overthrow by Thrasybulus, and eventual reconciliation without omitting anything the question asks for. | attribution: All provided passages are excerpts from the same author (Xenophon) and agree on the historical narrative; the answer accurately summarizes the account and uses correct citation markers without any misattributions.

results.question: How does Strabo portray the decayed condition of the once-great cities of Greece in his own day?
results.ideal_answer: Strabo repeatedly registers how the once-great cities of Greece had dwindled or vanished by his own day. In the Argolid "some of the cities mentioned in the Catalogue of the Ships, and said to be subject to Argos, have disappeared." In Arcadia the famous cities — Mantinea, Orchomenus, Heraea, Cleitor, Pheneus, Stymphalus and the rest — "either exist no longer, or traces and signs only of their existence are visible," the country sunk into a deserted condition. Lesser towns such as Dyspontium "was razed, and the greatest part of the inhabitants removed" abroad. Even Corinth had been sacked, Strabo recalling Polybius's eyewitness account of the soldiers' indifference to its art and sacred offerings. The recurring note is depopulation and ruin set against a glorious past.
results.answer: Strabo portrays the decayed condition of once-great Greek cities in his own day by emphasizing widespread depopulation and destruction. He states that "the greatest part of the country, however, is at present uninhabited, and the settlements, especially the cities, have been destroyed" [1]. He attributes this decline to prolonged internal rebellion, noting that the destruction "began a long time since, still continues in many parts in consequence of rebellion" and was only partially checked by the Romans [1]. As a concrete illustration, he describes Abydos, which "seems once to have been a large city, second to Thebes," but "at present it is a small town" [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: While the answer accurately reflects Strabo's portrayal of Greek depopulation and rebellion from [1], it misapplies the Egyptian city of Abydos from [2] as an illustration of Greek urban decay. | completeness: The answer correctly captures Strabo’s portrayal of widespread depopulation and destruction, supports it with a direct quotation and a concrete illustration, and fully answers how the decayed condition is depicted without needing the exhaustive regional catalog of the reference. | attribution: The answer misattributes the description of Abydos, which Passage 2 explicitly identifies as an Egyptian city in the Thebaïd, as a concrete illustration of decayed Greek cities.

results.question: How does Polybius explain the natural cycle by which forms of government rise and degenerate?
results.ideal_answer: In Book VI Polybius lays out the natural cycle (anacyclosis) by which constitutions rise and decay. Society begins under the rule of the strongest, but once a leader rewards merit and protects the weak, men "no longer obey him and support his rule from fear of violence, but rather from conviction of its utility" — force matures into legitimate kingship. Each good form then breeds its corrupt twin: kingship into tyranny, aristocracy into oligarchy, and democracy into mob-violence, where "tumultuous assemblies, massacres, banishments, redivisions of land" reduce the state until "it has once more found a master and a despot." "This is the regular cycle of constitutional revolutions." The escape is the mixed constitution: "Lycurgus... combined together all the excellences and distinctive features of the best constitutions, that no part should become unduly predominant" — the balance Polybius finds perfected at Rome.
results.answer: Polybius explains the natural cycle (anacyclosis) as an inevitable sequence grounded in human nature, starting from a primitive state. After a catastrophe, survivors herd together under the strongest leader, forming a “despotism” [6]. As family ties develop, people come to value justice and goodness, and the ruler who governs by reason rather than force transforms this despotism into “kingship” [5]. However, when descendants inherit royal power without earning it, they succumb to luxury and excess, and kingship degenerates into “tyranny” [2]. The best citizens then overthrow the tyrant, and out of gratitude the people entrust power to the just and wise, establishing “aristocracy” [2]. But their sons, raised in privilege, fall into avarice and vice, perverting aristocracy into “oligarchy” [2]. The people, resenting oligarchic injustice, expel the rulers and claim power for themselves, creating “democracy” [3]. Yet as new generations forget the past struggle, the rich corrupt the populace with bribes in their pursuit of office, and democracy decays into “mob-rule” characterized by violence, massacres, and land seizures, until it finally finds “a master and a despot” and the cycle begins anew [3][1]. Polybius summarizes by saying each pure form contains its own inherent vice: “in kingship it is absolutism; aristocracy it is oligarchy; in democracy lawless ferocity and violence”—like rust to iron—which inevitably transforms it into its corrupt counterpart [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim is directly supported by the provided passages, including the correct modern glosses 'anacyclosis' and 'mob-rule', and no new facts, quotes, or outcomes are invented. | completeness: The answer thoroughly traces the complete anacyclosis cycle, explaining how each government form rises from the ruin of the last and degenerates into its corrupt successor due to inherent human vices, fully satisfying the question's request without omitting any required element. | attribution: The source passages agree and describe different stages of the same cycle, and the answer correctly uses bare citation markers to attribute each stage to the relevant passage without any misattribution.

results.question: How does Polybius describe the organization and discipline of the Roman army?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius's Book VI admires the Roman army as a machine of organization and discipline. At the levy the military tribunes "muster their men" and bind each with an oath "that he will obey his officers," before sorting them into velites, hastati, principes and triarii. On campaign they entrench a standardized camp on one fixed plan, the general's Praetorium sited first and the streets laid out around it. Discipline is enforced by fear: a cohort that flees is decimated, the tribune selecting by lot "about a tenth of those who have been guilty of the act of cowardice" for the fustuarium; valour, by contrast, is so publicly rewarded that brave men become "marked men in all solemn festivals." Polybius caps the account by contrast — in land service "the Romans train themselves to a much higher pitch than the Carthaginians," who "wholly neglect their infantry."
results.answer:
Polybius describes the Roman army's organization and discipline in considerable detail, focusing on the legion's internal structure and the system of punishments and rewards that maintained order.

The legion's infantry, excluding the light-armed *Velites*, is divided into three classes—*Triarii*, *Principes*, and *Hastati*—each further divided into ten companies called *ordines*, *manipuli*, or *vexilla* [5]. Each maniple is placed under two centurions and two *optiones* (rear-rank officers), so that if one commander is absent or incapacitated, the maniple is never without a leader [5]. The centurions are selected not for boldness but for a steady, commanding spirit, expected to "die in defence of their post" even when overwhelmed [5]. The *Velites* are distributed equally among all the maniples, and each maniple chooses two standard-bearers from the strongest and best-born men [5].

The cavalry is divided into ten squadrons (*turmae*), each commanded by three *decuriones* who in turn select a subaltern (*optio*) [5]. The first-elected *decurio* leads the squadron, with the others holding the same rank but acting as subordinates unless the first is absent [5].

The discipline of the camp begins with an oath administered to all within the *agger*, binding them not to steal and to hand over any found items to the tribunes [1]. Guard duty is organized meticulously: two maniples of *Principes* and *Hastati* guard the Principia, while groups of maniples are assigned to serve each tribune, pitching his tent and providing two relays of four guards each [1]. The *Triarii* are exempted from personal service to the tribunes but supply a watch of four men to each cavalry squadron, primarily to prevent the horses from becoming entangled or escaping [6]. All maniples take turns mounting guard at the consul's tent [6]. The watchword is passed nightly through a system of *tesserae*, and the tribune on duty investigates any discrepancies; failure in this system leads to a court-martial [9].

Punishments for breaches of discipline are severe. The *fustuarium*—in which the tribune touches the condemned man with a cudgel, whereupon the soldiers beat him to death with clubs and stones—is applied to those guilty of theft in the camp, false witness, shameful immorality, or repeated offences [8]. It is also used for cowardly acts: making false reports of valour, deserting an ambush from fear, or throwing away arms on the battlefield; indeed, the fear of this punishment sometimes causes men to "confront certain death at their stations" rather than quit their post [8]. When whole maniples flee, indiscriminate execution is considered impossible, so the Romans resort to decimation—the tribune selects by lot about one-tenth of the offenders, who are punished by the *fustuarium*, while the survivors are put on barley rations and ordered to camp outside the vallum [8]. The same punishment also falls on a *praefect* or *opto* who fails to give necessary orders at the proper time [9]. The common soldiers are amenable to the tribunes, who may fine them, distrain their goods, or order them flogged, while the tribunes in turn are answerable to the consuls [8].

Polybius further details the orderly construction of the camp's foss and vallum, supervised by centurions at every maniple and by two tribunes over each entire side, with all other operations similarly managed by the tribunes working in turn [6]. At daybreak, the cavalry officers and centurions muster at the tribunes' tents, the tribunes receive the consuls' orders, and these are passed down to the rank and file [6].
