results.question: How does Livy portray Hannibal as Rome's great antagonist across the Second Punic War?
results.ideal_answer: Livy makes Hannibal Rome's supreme antagonist, drawn double-edged from the first: matchless "high virtues of the hero" shadowed by "inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy, no truth... no fear of the gods, no respect for oaths." He proves the virtues at once, bringing his army into Italy "in the fifth month... having crossed the Alps in fifteen days" through appalling loss. Yet Livy fixes the fatal flaw after Cannae, when Maharbal urges an instant march on Rome and, refused, retorts "You know how to conquer, Hannibal; but you do not know how to make use of your victory" — a delay "believed to have been the preservation of the city and the empire." The long Italian war winds to its end at Zama, where Hannibal, "after performing this as it were his last work of valour, fled to Adrumetum," and confessed in the senate "that he was defeated, not only in the battle, but in the war."
results.answer:
Livy portrays Hannibal as Rome's supreme antagonist by building the entire Second Punic War narrative around him—framing it as "the most memorable of all wars that were ever waged" [2] and presenting Hannibal as a figure of extraordinary, dangerous polarity: a military genius whose towering virtues are counterbalanced by monstrous vices. He opens his account with a formal character sketch of Hannibal (Book XXI), and repeats its themes through dramatic speeches and episodes across the entire war. The portrait has several consistent threads.

**1. The sworn enemy from childhood.** Livy opens the war narrative by tracing Hannibal's antagonism to a sacred oath. He relates that at around nine years old, Hannibal "was conducted to the altar; and, having laid his hand on the offerings, was bound by an oath to prove himself, as soon as he could, an enemy to the Roman people" [2]. This oath, Livy repeatedly emphasizes, was no empty gesture: much later, as an exile at Antiochus's court, Hannibal himself declares, "Under the obligation of this oath, I carried arms against them for thirty-six years… I hate, and am hated by, the Romans" [1]. By bookending the war with the oath, Livy casts Hannibal as a man possessed by a lifelong, almost religious mission against Rome.

**2. A military genius of superhuman endurance.** Livy's formal character sketch in Book XXI is a catalogue of heroic soldierly qualities. He describes Hannibal as possessing "extreme" fearlessness and prudence, a body that "could not be exhausted, nor his mind subdued, by any toil," and the ability to endure heat and cold equally. His eating and drinking were governed by nature, not pleasure; his sleep snatched on the ground "amid the watches and outposts of the soldiers" wrapped in a common cloak. Above all, Livy says, "He was at once by far the first of the cavalry and infantry; and, foremost to advance to the charge, was last to leave the engagement" [9]. No other general in the *History* receives such a concentrated portrait of martial perfection — it serves to magnify the threat.

**3. …counterbalanced by monstrous vices.** Immediately after listing the virtues, Livy pivots to the dark side with equal force: "Excessive vices counterbalanced these high virtues of the hero; inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy, no truth, no reverence for things sacred, no fear of the gods, no respect for oaths, no sense of religion" [9]. This moral condemnation — "inhuman cruelty" and the phrase "more than Punic perfidy" applying an ethnic stereotype — is essential to Livy's portrayal: Hannibal is not merely a dangerous enemy but a morally monstrous one, a figure whose brilliance makes his faithlessness all the more terrifying. Livy returns to this theme narratively, describing how Hannibal is "nearly overcome, not by open war, but by his own arts of treachery and ambuscade" in the Alps [10].

**4. Relentless, world-conquering ambition.** Livy dramatizes Hannibal's terrifying drive through speeches, notably the address before the Alpine crossing. Hannibal rebukes his men for faltering "at the very gates of the enemy" after conquering from "the setting to the rising of the sun," and declares their purpose is "to blot out the name of the Romans, and to emancipate the world" [5]. The ambition is cosmic — Livy's Hannibal sees himself as a world-liberator whose target is Rome's very existence.

**5. The terror in the Roman psyche.** Livy shows the impact of Hannibal on the Roman consciousness itself. After the disasters at Ticinus, Trebia, and Trasimene, he reports that both Carthaginians and Romans "then at length began to feel that they were engaged in war with Romans, and in Italy" — the contempt the Carthaginians had held for Roman generals evaporates, and Livy adds that Hannibal himself compared Fabius's tactics to a storm breaking from the mountains [7]. The war becomes a personal contest between Hannibal and the Roman state, raised to mythic proportions. Later, at the gates of Rome, Hannibal's mere presence is depicted as an existential test: "Wild beasts, impelled by headlong fury and rage, you may divert from their object… The Romans could not be diverted from Capua by the blockade of Rome" [4] — the comparison to a wild beast underscores Livy's view of Hannibal as a force of nature that Rome must withstand.

In sum, Livy constructs Hannibal as the perfect antagonist: a once-in-history military prodigy driven by a sacred childhood hatred, whose superhuman qualities are rendered truly dangerous — and morally repugnant — by a complete absence of every virtue that, in Livy's Roman worldview, separates civilized men from barbarians. He is the immovable object against which Rome's character is tested and ultimately forged.
